The production equipment is designed to produce a **variety of products in batches**, with changeover between products achieved by altering the control program rather than physically retooling the machine.

**Key Identifiers:** Reprogrammable, batch production, moderate volume per part number, uses CNC machines, robots, PLCs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The CNC Milling Centre:** Consider a Computer Numerical Control (CNC) milling machine. To make a brass gear in the morning and a steel bracket in the afternoon, the operator simply loads a new G-code program. The physical machine is identical, but the *instructions* it follows have changed. This reprogramming capability is the hallmark of **Programmable Automation**.

### 🔹 3. Flexible Automation

**Flexible Automation** is an extension of programmable automation in which the system is capable of producing **a variety of products (mixed-model production) with virtually no lost time for changeovers**. The distinguishing feature is that the changeover between products happens **automatically, on the fly**, under computer control — without stopping the line for reprogramming.

**Key Identifiers:** Mixed-model assembly, real-time changeover, computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM), high capital cost, high utilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Modern Car Assembly Line:** A contemporary automotive assembly line builds a Maruti hatchback, then a Sedan, then an SUV — all on the **same conveyor** — based on the real-time order data fed into the line controller. The vehicle type is detected at each station, and the correct welding or fastening program is invoked *automatically*. This seamless, stop-less variety production is the essence of **Flexible Automation**.

---

## 1.3 Relationship with Production Volume and Variety

A foundational concept in manufacturing automation is the **Product-Volume-Variety Matrix**. As production volume **increases** and product variety **decreases**, fixed automation becomes more economical. As variety **increases** and batch sizes **shrink**, programmable and flexible automation dominate.

This relationship is typically illustrated using the famous **Product-Volume Curve** introduced in industrial engineering curricula.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"F-P-P-F"* — **F**ixed for **P**roduction (high volume), **P**rogrammable for **P**roduction (batch), **F**lexible for **F**amily (mixed models). The first letter of the type matches the production strategy.

# 📊 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Characteristic Breakdown

### ⚙️ 2.1.1 Fixed Automation — Engineering Anatomy

Fixed automation systems are engineered around **mechanically coordinated hardware** rather than software. The control logic is typically embedded in the mechanical design itself through:

- **Cams** (rotating discs with profiled grooves that actuate switches)
- **Limit switches** (electromechanical sensors triggered at specific positions)
- **Relays and timers** (electromagnetic switching logic)
- **Plugboards** (re-wired control panels for limited variation)

**Operational Characteristics:**

1. **Sequence is mechanically determined**, not software-defined.
2. **Changeover requires physical retooling** (hours to days).
3. **Cycle time is fixed and minimized** (often sub-second).
4. **Capital investment is very high** — amortized over millions of units.
5. **Best suited for mass production** — annual quantities typically exceed **1,00,000 units** per product variant.
6. **Lowest per-unit variable cost** among all three types.

**Classic Real-World Examples:**

- Automotive **transfer lines** (e.g., engine block machining lines)
- **Bottle-filling** and capping lines
- **Printed circuit board (PCB) component insertion** machines
- **Sheet metal stamping** presses with multi-stage dies

**Why It Works (The Engineering "Why"):** Because the product never changes, every motion of every actuator can be **optimized to a single, repetitive trajectory**. This elimination of decision-making overhead allows the system to operate at the **theoretical maximum mechanical speed** of its components.

---

### ⚙️ 2.1.2 Programmable Automation — Engineering Anatomy

Programmable automation decouples the **control logic from the physical hardware**. The same machine can perform different operations by interpreting a different set of instructions. The control is achieved through:

- **CNC controllers** (G-code & M-code interpreters)
- **PLCs** (Programmable Logic Controllers using ladder logic or structured text)
- **Industrial PCs** with motion control software
- **Microcontrollers** (in smaller-scale systems)

**Operational Characteristics:**

1. **Changeover is achieved by software reprogramming**, not hardware modification.
2. **Setup time** between batches typically ranges from **minutes to a few hours**.
3. **Production volume per product is medium** — typically **50 to 10,000 units** annually.
4. **Capital cost is moderate** to high — economically justified for higher-value components.
5. **Suitable for job-shop and batch manufacturing environments.**
6. **Higher per-unit cost than fixed automation** due to the overhead of general-purpose hardware.

**Classic Real-World Examples:**

- **CNC machining centres** (milling, turning, EDM)
- **Industrial robots** in weld cells (reprogrammed per car model batch)
- **PCB drilling and routing** machines
- **Injection moulding machines** with recipe-based controllers

**Why It Works:** The flexibility is provided by the **software layer** that abstracts the machine kinematics. The hardware is general-purpose; only the program changes. This is conceptually similar to how a **personal computer** can run different applications — the hardware is fixed, the behaviour is variable.

---

### ⚙️ 2.1.3 Flexible Automation — Engineering Anatomy

Flexible automation is the most sophisticated category. It extends programmable automation by adding **real-time decision-making** and **automatic changeover** capabilities. The system is typically part of a **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** or **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** framework.

**Operational Characteristics:**

1. **Mixed-model production** — multiple products simultaneously, not in batches.
2. **No lost time for changeovers** — tools and programs are switched automatically.
3. **Uses automated material handling** (AGVs, conveyors) and **automated tool changers.**
4. **Production volume is medium-to-high**, but with **very high variety** — typically dozens of part numbers on the same line.
5. **Capital cost is very high** — economically justified only for **high-mix, high-volume** scenarios.
6. **Lowest possible work-in-process (WIP)** inventory due to just-in-time flow.

**Classic Real-World Examples:**

- **Mazak Integrex** multi-tasking CNC centres with pallet changers
- **Automotive final assembly lines** (mixed-model)
- **Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)** at Boeing, Airbus, and Tata Motors
- **Automated warehousing and AS/RS** (Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems)

**Why It Works:** The system uses **distributed intelligence** — sensors identify which product is at each station, and the local controller instantly loads the correct program. This is analogous to a **smart traffic intersection** that reads vehicle types and adjusts signal timing in real time.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield KTU exam reference** that condenses all quantitative and qualitative decision criteria. Memorize this matrix for guaranteed marks in any comparison-based question.

| **Parameter** | **Fixed Automation** | **Programmable Automation** | **Flexible Automation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Volume** | Very High ($\geq 1 \times 10^{5}$ units/yr) | Medium ($10^{2}$ to $10^{4}$ units/yr) | Medium-High, mixed |
| **Product Variety** | Very Low (1 product) | Moderate (10–100 part numbers) | High (mixed-model) |
| **Changeover Method** | Physical retooling | Software reprogramming | Automatic, real-time |
| **Changeover Time** | Hours to Days | Minutes to Hours | Zero (continuous) |
| **Capital Investment** | Very High | Moderate to High | Very High |
| **Per-Unit Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate to Low |
| **Control Mechanism** | Cams, Relays, Mechanical | CNC, PLC, Software | CIM, FMS, Distributed Control |
| **Flexibility** | None | Moderate | Maximum |
| **Typical Industries** | Automotive stamping, Bottling | Job shops, Tool rooms | Aerospace, Auto final assembly |
| **System Examples** | Transfer lines | CNC machines | FMS, AGV-based lines |

### 📐 Quantitative Justification — Break-Even Concept

The economic selection of automation type is governed by the **unit production cost** and **production volume**. A simplified cost model used in KTU examinations is:

$$C_{u} = \frac{C_{fixed}}{Q} + C_{variable}$$

Where:
- $C_{u}$ = Cost per unit (Rupees)
- $C_{fixed}$ = Total fixed/initial investment (Rupees)
- $C_{variable}$ = Variable cost per unit (Rupees)
- $Q$ = Annual production quantity (units)

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This formula is used by **industrial engineers** in *capital budgeting decisions*. A factory owner deciding between a ₹2 crore dedicated transfer line and a ₹1 crore CNC cell uses this exact equation to find the **break-even production quantity** $Q_{BE}$ where both systems are equally economical.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{C_{fixed1} - C_{fixed2}}{C_{variable2} - C_{variable1}}$$

**Decision Rule (KTU Board Standard):**
- If expected $Q \gg Q_{BE}$ → choose the system with higher $C_{fixed}$ (typically Fixed Automation)
- If expected $Q \ll Q_{BE}$ → choose the system with lower $C_{fixed}$ (typically Programmable/Flexible)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Across Domains

| **Domain** | **Predominant Automation Type** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| High-volume consumer goods (PET bottles, soft drinks) | Fixed | Billions of units/year per SKU |
| Customized aerospace components | Programmable | Small batches, high precision, high variety |
| Automotive final assembly | Flexible | Mixed models on the same line daily |
| Job-shop machining for small vendors | Programmable | Low volume, high variety, general-purpose |
| Semiconductor fabrication | Fixed + Programmable hybrid | Wafer processing is fixed; lithography programs change |

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Break-Even Quantity Between Two Automation Systems

**Problem Setup:** A manufacturer is choosing between two automation alternatives for a new component:

- **System A (Fixed Automation - Transfer Line):**
  - Capital investment: $C_{fixed,A} = ₹\ 2{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (₹ 2 Crore)
  - Variable cost per unit: $C_{var,A} = ₹\ 50$

- **System B (Programmable Automation - CNC Cell):**
  - Capital investment: $C_{fixed,B} = ₹\ 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (₹ 1 Crore)
  - Variable cost per unit: $C_{var,B} = ₹\ 150$

Find the **break-even annual production quantity** $Q_{BE}$ at which both systems have equal total cost per year, and determine which system should be chosen for an expected demand of **50,000 units/year**.

---

### 📝 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the total annual cost equation for each system.

The total cost of any automation system over a year is the sum of fixed cost and total variable cost:

$$TC_{A} = C_{fixed,A} + (C_{var,A} \times Q)$$

$$TC_{B} = C_{fixed,B} + (C_{var,B} \times Q)$$

**Step 2:** At the break-even point, $TC_{A} = TC_{B}$.

Setting the two cost equations equal:

$$C_{fixed,A} + (C_{var,A} \times Q_{BE}) = C_{fixed,B} + (C_{var,B} \times Q_{BE})$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange the equation to isolate $Q_{BE}$ on one side.

Move all $Q_{BE}$ terms to the left and constant terms to the right:

$$(C_{var,A} \times Q_{BE}) - (C_{var,B} \times Q_{BE}) = C_{fixed,B} - C_{fixed,A}$$

Factor out $Q_{BE}$:

$$Q_{BE} \times (C_{var,A} - C_{var,B}) = C_{fixed,B} - C_{fixed,A}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $Q_{BE}$ by dividing both sides by the difference in variable costs.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{C_{fixed,B} - C_{fixed,A}}{C_{var,A} - C_{var,B}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{7}) - (2 \times 10^{7})}{50 - 150}$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{-1 \times 10^{7}}{-100}$$

$$Q_{BE} = 1 \times 10^{5} = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result using the decision rule.

The break-even quantity is $1{,}00{,}000$ units/year. For expected demand $Q = 50{,}000$ units/year, we have $Q < Q_{BE}$. Therefore, **System B (Programmable Automation / CNC Cell)** is the economically superior choice.

**Step 7 (Optional Verification):** Calculate the annual cost of each system at $Q = 50{,}000$.

$$TC_{A} = 2 \times 10^{7} + (50 \times 50{,}000) = 2 \times 10^{7} + 25 \times 10^{5} = ₹\ 2.25\ \text{Crore}$$

$$TC_{B} = 1 \times 10^{7} + (150 \times 50{,}000) = 1 \times 10^{7} + 75 \times 10^{5} = ₹\ 1.75\ \text{Crore}$$

Since $TC_{B} < TC_{A}$ at the given demand, the choice of **System B** is verified. ✅

---

## 3.2 Production-Volume Decision Matrix (Qualitative Justification Table)

The following matrix helps in **justifying the selection** of automation type based on production characteristics, as expected in KTU 14-mark essay questions.

| **Decision Parameter** | **Threshold / Range** | **Recommended Automation Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Annual volume $\geq 10^{6}$ units, single part | Very High Volume, Zero Variety | **Fixed Automation** |
| Annual volume $10^{3}$ to $10^{5}$, 5–50 part numbers | Medium Volume, Moderate Variety | **Programmable Automation** |
| Annual volume $\geq 10^{5}$, 50+ part numbers mixed daily | Medium-High Volume, High Variety | **Flexible Automation** |
| Tool room / R&D prototypes | Low Volume, High Variety | **Programmable (with manual intervention)** |

---

## 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical on Unit Cost Comparison

**Question:** A CNC lathe cell has a capital investment of ₹ 50 lakh and a variable cost of ₹ 200 per component. A dedicated automatic lathe for the same component costs ₹ 1.5 crore with a variable cost of ₹ 40 per component. At what annual production quantity do both options yield the same per-unit cost?

**Solution:**

Using the break-even equation:

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{C_{fixed,B} - C_{fixed,A}}{C_{var,A} - C_{var,B}}$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{7}) - (5 \times 10^{6})}{200 - 40}$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{7}}{160}$$

$$Q_{BE} = 62{,}500 \text{ units/year}$$

**Decision Rule:** Produce $Q \geq 62{,}500$ units → choose **Fixed Automation** (Dedicated Lathe); produce $Q < 62{,}500$ units → choose **Programmable Automation** (CNC Lathe).

# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> Define **Fixed Automation**. Mention any two of its distinguishing features and one industrial application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fixed Automation (also called Hard or Dedicated Automation) is a system in which the **sequence of processing operations is fixed by the equipment configuration**. The operations are predetermined by the mechanical design of the system (cams, limit switches, relays) and cannot be easily altered.

**Distinguishing Features (1 mark each — any two):**
1. **Very high production rate** — optimized for a single product, with cycle times often in seconds.
2. **Very high capital investment** — economically justified only for mass production (typically $> 10^{5}$ units/year).
3. **Changeover requires physical retooling** — usually takes hours to days; not feasible for high-variety production.
4. **Lowest per-unit variable cost** among all three automation types.

**Industrial Application (1 mark — any one):**
- Automotive **transfer lines** for engine block machining
- **Bottle-filling and capping** lines in beverage industries
- **PCB component insertion** machines in electronics manufacturing
- **Sheet metal stamping** presses with progressive dies

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> Differentiate between **Programmable Automation** and **Flexible Automation** in terms of changeover time and production strategy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Programmable Automation** | **Flexible Automation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Production Strategy** | Batch production — one product variant is made at a time, in batches | Mixed-model production — multiple product variants produced simultaneously on the same line |
| **Changeover Time** | Non-zero changeover (minutes to hours) — system is **stopped** to load new program | Near-zero changeover — switching happens **on the fly** in real time under computer control |
| **Control Logic** | Centralized program (CNC, PLC) invoked at startup | Distributed real-time controllers that detect product type and invoke correct recipe per station |

**Key Conceptual Distinction (1 mark):** Programmable automation *can* make different products, but only **one at a time, in batches**. Flexible automation makes different products **simultaneously and continuously**, with no stoppage between variants.
