> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing $R_p$ in *parts/min* and $T_c$ in *seconds* without conversion — the KTU key deducts **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Missing the Bottleneck Identification:** Line efficiency *requires* identifying $t_{max}$ first. Skipping this step is a **2-mark** penalty.
> 3. **Inverting MTBF/MTTR:** A very common error; remember *uptime base is the numerator*.
> 4. **No sketch in 7-mark sub-parts:** The KTU valuation key expects a "neat labelled diagram" — **2 marks reserved** for the sketch alone.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the formula before substituting:** Always *write* $\eta_L = \dots$ *before* plugging in numbers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Cycle time** of a flow line equals the **maximum station time** — never the average.
- ✅ **Production rate** $R_p = 3600 / T_c$ (with $T_c$ in seconds, gives parts/hr).
- ✅ **Line efficiency** $\eta_L = (\sum t_i) / (n \cdot t_{max}) \times 100$; ideal is **100 %**, practical ≥ **85 %**.
- ✅ **Balance delay** $BD = 1 - \eta_L$; ideal is **0**.
- ✅ **Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$; always keep MTBF in the **numerator**.
- ✅ A **buffer** is sized using $B_{min} = R_p (t_{max} - t_{next})$ and reported in **pallet-seconds + pallet count**.
- ✅ **Walking-beam** transfer is more accurate than lift-and-locate; **roller-track** is fastest.
- ✅ **Master PLC** handles sequence + interlock; **SCADA** handles monitoring; **ERP** handles scheduling.
- ✅ **PROFIBUS / PROFINET / EtherCAT** are the standard field-buses for KTU-level AFLs.
- ✅ Always **draw a labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark sub-part on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Line Performance Analysis: Average Production Time, Production Rate, and Line Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Line Performance Analysis: Average Production Time, Production Rate, and Line Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Flow Line?

A **flow line** (or assembly/production line) is a manufacturing configuration in which workstations are arranged in a **serial sequence** according to the operations required, and material flows continuously (or in batches) from one workstation to the next. Each station performs a specific task, and the product is built progressively as it moves down the line.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A flow line is a high-volume production layout where work units move through a predetermined sequence of workstations, each performing a specialized operation, with the goal of achieving a steady, predictable output rate.

The three fundamental performance parameters used to evaluate any flow line are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| Average Production Time | $APT$ | Time for **one** unit to traverse the entire line |
| Production Rate | $PR$ | Number of units produced **per unit time** |
| Line Efficiency | $LE$ | Ratio of **useful work** to **available work time** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Coffee Shop Drive-Through

Imagine a drive-through coffee shop with **three windows**:
- **Window 1** (Order taking): takes 1 minute
- **Window 2** (Payment): takes 2 minutes
- **Window 3** (Coffee preparation): takes 3 minutes

A car arriving at the line must pass through all three windows. Even though Window 1 is fast, the **slowest station (Window 3)** dictates how fast the next car can enter the line. This is exactly how a flow line behaves:

- The **slowest workstation** is the **bottleneck** and sets the **Cycle Time**.
- The **time for a single car** to go from entry to exit is the **Average Production Time**.
- The **number of cars served per hour** is the **Production Rate**.
- The **Line Efficiency** is essentially: *Are we using the fast workers efficiently, or are they sitting idle while the slow one catches up?*

## 1.3 Defining the Core Metrics (with Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Definitions for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Workstation Time ($t_i$)**: The processing time required at the $i^{th}$ workstation, measured in minutes (or seconds) per unit.
- **Bottleneck Time ($t_{max}$ or $t_b$)**: The **maximum** workstation time in the line. It is the **rate-controlling** activity.
- **Cycle Time ($CT$)**: The time interval between two successive units emerging from the end of the line. **Numerically, $CT = t_{max}$.**
- **Number of Workstations ($k$)**: The total number of stations in the flow line.
- **Total Work Content ($T_w$ or $\Sigma t_i$)**: The sum of all individual workstation times, $T_w = t_1 + t_2 + \cdots + t_k$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind the Metrics

A flow line is fundamentally a **series system of work stations**. To understand its performance, we must distinguish between two timescales:

1. **Steady-State Continuous Production (long run):** Once the line has been running for a while, a finished unit emerges every $CT$ minutes. In this regime, only the **bottleneck** matters.

2. **Single-Unit Lead Time (short run):** The very first unit must visit *every* station sequentially, accumulating all the processing times.

This dual-timescale perspective is the conceptual foundation of the formulas below.

## 2.2 The Three Core Performance Metrics — Derived Intuitively

### A. Average Production Time ($APT$)
When a unit enters the empty line, it must wait for the work at each station. The first unit's total time to exit is the **sum of all station times**. As the line fills, units overlap in processing, but the average time per unit remains equal to this sum for steady-state flow analysis.

### B. Production Rate ($PR$)
In the steady state, one unit exits every $CT$ minutes. Therefore the production rate is the reciprocal of the cycle time.

### C. Line Efficiency ($LE$)
Efficiency compares the *useful* work packed into the line versus the *theoretical maximum* if every station were fully utilized. A perfectly balanced line has every $t_i = t_{max}$, so the total work $\Sigma t_i = k \cdot t_{max}$, and $LE = 1$ (or 100%). Any deviation from balance causes some stations to idle, dropping efficiency below 100%.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize This for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|--------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | **Cycle Time** | $CT = \max(t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k) = t_{max}$ | min/unit | Equals bottleneck time |
| 2 | **Average Production Time (APT)** | $APT = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$ | min/unit | First-unit lead time |
| 3 | **Production Rate (PR)** | $PR = \dfrac{1}{CT} = \dfrac{1}{t_{max}}$ | units/min | Steady-state output rate |
| 4 | **Line Efficiency (LE)** | $LE = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i}{k \cdot t_{max}} = \dfrac{APT}{k \cdot CT}$ | dimensionless (×100% for %) | $0 \le LE \le 1$ |
| 5 | **Balance Delay (BD)** | $BD = 1 - LE$ | dimensionless | Time "wasted" due to imbalance |
| 6 | **Time to produce N units** | $T_N = APT + (N-1) \cdot CT$ | min | Includes pipeline fill effect |
| 7 | **Work-In-Process (WIP)** | $WIP = PR \cdot APT$ (Little's Law) | units | Steady-state inventory |
| 8 | **Hypothetical CT (for ideal balance)** | $CT_{ideal} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{k}$ | min/unit | The "fair share" per station |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Plants:** Ford's original Model T line was the first to apply APT/PR analysis at scale. Today, $LE > 85\%$ is industry standard; a Toyota line targeting $LE \approx 95\%$ is considered world-class.
- **Electronics Manufacturing (SMT lines):** Pick-and-place, reflow soldering, and AOI stations must be balanced so that the bottleneck is the placement head, not a slower inspection camera.
- **Bottleneck Detection:** If $PR_{actual} < 1/t_{max}$, the line has a hidden secondary bottleneck or suffers from downtime — a key diagnostic tool.
- **Cost Estimation:** Knowing $APT$ and the hourly labor cost gives the **labor content per unit**, used in activity-based costing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Production Rate

In the **steady-state regime**, the line behaves like a periodic pipeline. The bottleneck station releases one unit every $t_{max}$ minutes, and all other stations are arranged in series. Because no station can output faster than its own capacity, the **slowest** station dominates:

$$
PR = \frac{1 \text{ unit}}{t_{max} \text{ min}} = \frac{1}{CT} \quad \text{[units/min]}
$$

To convert to **units per hour** (a common KTU exam requirement):

$$
PR_{hr} = \frac{60}{t_{max}} \quad \text{[units/hour]}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Average Production Time (APT)

Consider a line with $k$ stations. The very first unit must:
- Spend $t_1$ min at station 1,
- Then $t_2$ min at station 2,
- ... up to $t_k$ min at station $k$.

There are **no idle waits** for the first unit (assuming the line starts empty). Therefore:

$$
APT = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \cdots + t_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The APT is **independent of the order of stations**. Whether the bottleneck is at the start, middle, or end of the line, the sum remains the same. However, the *idle time distribution* between stations depends on the order — a heavily used follow-up concept in line balancing.

## 3.3 Derivation of Line Efficiency

**Step 1 — Maximum theoretical work** that *could* be performed in the time it takes the bottleneck to release one unit is $k \cdot t_{max}$ (each of the $k$ stations works for $t_{max}$ minutes).

**Step 2 — Actual productive work** packed into one unit is $\sum t_i$.

**Step 3 — Efficiency** is the ratio:

$$
LE = \frac{\text{Actual Work}}{\text{Maximum Possible Work}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i}{k \cdot t_{max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage form** (commonly required in KTU answers):

$$
LE(\%) = \frac{\sum t_i}{k \cdot t_{max}} \times 100
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Time to Produce N Units

In the long run, once the pipeline is full, a new unit emerges every $CT$ minutes. For $N$ units:

- **Time for the first unit** = $APT$
- **Time for the next (N−1) units** = $(N-1) \cdot CT$ (steady state)

$$
T_N = APT + (N-1) \cdot CT = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i + (N-1) \cdot t_{max}
$$

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Solved Numerical Examples

### Example 1 (Basic — 7-Mark Style Problem)

A flow line has **4 workstations** with the following times:

$$
t_1 = 5 \text{ min}, \quad t_2 = 6 \text{ min}, \quad t_3 = 4 \text{ min}, \quad t_4 = 7 \text{ min}
$$

**Find the cycle time, production rate, APT, and line efficiency.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Bottleneck:**
$t_{max} = \max(5, 6, 4, 7) = 7 \text{ min}$ at station 4.

**Step 2 — Cycle Time:**

$$
CT = t_{max} = 7 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Production Rate:**

$$
PR = \frac{1}{CT} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429 \text{ units/min}
$$

In units per hour:

$$
PR = \frac{60}{7} \approx 8.57 \text{ units/hour}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Production Time:**

$$
APT = \sum t_i = 5 + 6 + 4 + 7 = 22 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Line Efficiency:**

$$
LE = \frac{APT}{k \cdot CT} = \frac{22}{4 \times 7} = \frac{22}{28} = 0.7857
$$

$$
LE = 78.57\%
$$

**Step 6 — Balance Delay:**

$$
BD = 1 - LE = 1 - 0.7857 = 0.2143 = 21.43\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always state the bottleneck station explicitly. Marks are awarded in KTU for *showing which station is the bottleneck*, not just for plugging numbers.

---

### Example 2 (Higher-Order — Involves Time for N Units)

A 5-station flow line has workstation times: $t_1 = 3, t_2 = 5, t_3 = 4, t_4 = 5, t_5 = 3$ minutes. Calculate:
(a) The cycle time and production rate.
(b) The APT and line efficiency.
(c) The time required to produce **100 units** in steady state.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bottleneck identification:**
$t_{max} = \max(3, 5, 4, 5, 3) = 5 \text{ min}$ (stations 2 and 4 are tied).

**Step 2 — Cycle Time:**

$$
CT = 5 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Production Rate:**

$$
PR = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ units/min} = 12 \text{ units/hour}
$$

**Step 4 — APT:**

$$
APT = 3 + 5 + 4 + 5 + 3 = 20 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Line Efficiency:**

$$
LE = \frac{20}{5 \times 5} = \frac{20}{25} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**Step 6 — Time to produce 100 units:**

$$
T_{100} = APT + (N-1) \cdot CT = 20 + (100-1) \times 5
$$

$$
T_{100} = 20 + 99 \times 5 = 20 + 495 = 515 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_{100} = 515 \text{ min} \approx 8 \text{ hours } 35 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 7 — Bonus — WIP using Little's Law:**

$$
WIP = PR \cdot APT = 0.2 \times 20 = 4 \text{ units (in pipeline)}
$$

---

### Example 3 (Diagnosing an Inefficient Line)

A 6-station line produces $PR_{actual} = 9$ units/hour, but its station times are $t = \{2, 5, 4, 6, 3, 4\}$ min. Is the line theoretically capable of higher output? If yes, by how much?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical PR:**

$$
t_{max} = 6 \text{ min} \implies PR_{theory} = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ units/hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Diagnose:**

$$
PR_{actual} = 9 < PR_{theory} = 10
$$

The line is **underperforming** by 1 unit/hour. Likely causes: secondary bottlenecks, downtime, or quality rework.

**Step 3 — Line Efficiency:**

$$
APT = 2+5+4+6+3+4 = 24 \text{ min}
$$

$$
LE = \frac{24}{6 \times 6} = \frac{24}{36} = 66.67\%
$$

**Conclusion:** With $LE = 66.67\%$, the line has significant balance delay (33.33%) and could benefit from re-balancing or investing in the bottleneck station.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Line Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below shows the **functional architecture of a generic 4-station flow line**, illustrating the material flow, workstation identification, and bottleneck highlighting.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Input] --> B[Station 1: t1 = 5 min]
    B --> C[Station 2: t2 = 6 min]
    C --> D[Station 3: t3 = 4 min]
    D --> E[Station 4: t4 = 7 min BOTTLENECK]
    E --> F[Finished Product Output]
    
    G[CT = tmax = 7 min] -.controls.-> B
    G -.controls.-> C
    G -.controls.-> D
    G -.controls.-> E
    
    H[APT = sum ti = 22 min] -.single unit lead time.-> A
    H -.single unit lead time.-> F
    
    I[LE = 22 / 28 = 78.57 percent] -.efficiency metric.-> G
```

## 4.2 Steady-State Timeline Diagram (Gantt-like Block Visualization)

The following block diagram shows **how units U1, U2, U3, U4** flow through a 4-station line with the same times as Example 1. Notice how the bottleneck (Station 4, 7 min) controls the *inter-unit* spacing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Station1[STATION 1 5 min]
        direction LR
        s1u1[U1] --> s1u2[U2] --> s1u3[U3] --> s1u4[U4]
    end
    
    subgraph Station2[STATION 2 6 min]
        direction LR
        s2u1[U1] --> s2u2[U2] --> s2u3[U3] --> s2u4[U4]
    end
    
    subgraph Station3[STATION 3 4 min]
        direction LR
        s3u1[U1] --> s3u2[U2] --> s3u3[U3] --> s3u4[U4]
    end
    
    subgraph Station4[STATION 4 BOTTLENECK 7 min]
        direction LR
        s4u1[U1] --> s4u2[U2] --> s4u3[U3] --> s4u4[U4]
    end
    
    Station1 --> Station2
    Station2 --> Station3
    Station3 --> Station4
    Station4 --> Output[OUTPUT Q every 7 min]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each unit (U1, U2, U3, U4) takes a horizontal lane through the stations. The bottleneck (Station 4) takes the widest lane (7 min), confirming $CT = 7$ min. Steady-state output emerges at 7-min intervals.

## 4.3 Performance Metric Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Workstation Times t1 t2 ... tk] --> P2[Identify tmax]
    P2 --> P3[Cycle Time CT equals tmax]
    P2 --> P4[APT equals sum of ti]
    P3 --> P5[Production Rate PR equals 1 over CT]
    P3 --> P6[Line Efficiency LE equals APT over k times CT]
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P7[Time for N units T_N equals APT plus N minus 1 times CT]
    P4 --> P7
    P6 --> P8[Balance Delay BD equals 1 minus LE]
    P5 --> P9[WIP equals PR times APT Little Law]
    P4 --> P9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

### Q1. Define the term *Cycle Time* of a flow line and state its relationship with the workstation times.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cycle time** of a flow line is the time interval between the completion of two consecutive units at the end of the line. It is the time required for the line to produce one unit in the **steady-state (continuous production)** regime.

**Relationship with workstation times:**

$$
CT = \max(t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_k) = t_{max}
$$

That is, the cycle time equals the **largest workstation time**, since the slowest station (bottleneck) governs the rate at which units can leave the line. All other stations with $t_i < t_{max}$ will have built-in idle time. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. Distinguish between *Average Production Time* and *Production Rate* of a flow line.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Average Production Time (APT) | Production Rate (PR) |
|--------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Time for **one** unit to traverse the line from start to finish | Number of units produced **per unit time** in steady state |
| **Formula** | $APT = \sum t_i$ | $PR = 1/CT$ |
| **Unit** | min/unit (a time) | units/min (a frequency) |
| **Determined by** | Sum of all workstation times | The bottleneck station only |
| **Concept** | A *latency* measure | A *throughput* measure |

> The two are related as **PR = 1/CT**, but APT depends on *all* stations while PR depends on *only one* (the bottleneck). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Module 2 Question]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A flow line has six workstations with the following processing times (in minutes):**

$$
t_1 = 4, \quad t_2 = 7, \quad t_3 = 5, \quad t_4 = 6, \quad t_5 = 8, \quad t_6 = 5
$$

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Determine the **cycle time, production rate (units/hr), and average production time** for the line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **line efficiency and balance delay**. If the manager wants to achieve a line efficiency of at least 90%, suggest **two practical remedies**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Cycle Time, PR, and APT**

**Step 1:** Identify bottleneck:

$$
t_{max} = \max(4, 7, 5, 6, 8, 5) = 8 \text{ min} \quad \text{(at Station 5)}
$$

**Step 2:** Cycle Time:

$$
CT = t_{max} = 8 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Step 3:** Production Rate:

$$
PR = \frac{1}{8} \text{ units/min} = \frac{60}{8} = 7.5 \text{ units/hour}
$$

**Step 4:** Average Production Time:

$$
APT = 4 + 7 + 5 + 6 + 8 + 5 = 35 \text{ min}
$$

> **(7 marks distributed: 2 for CT, 2 for PR, 3 for APT)**

---

**Part (b) — Line Efficiency, Balance Delay, Remedies**

**Step 5:** Line Efficiency:

$$
LE = \frac{APT}{k \cdot CT} = \frac{35}{6 \times 8} = \frac{35}{48} = 0.7292
$$

$$
LE = 72.92\%
$$

**Step 6:** Balance Delay:

$$
BD = 1 - LE = 1 - 0.7292 = 0.2708 = 27.08\%
$$

**Step 7 — Practical remedies to achieve LE ≥ 90%:**

1. **Improve the bottleneck station:** Station 5 (8 min) is the rate-limiting step. Installing a faster machine, adding a parallel sub-station, training the operator, or implementing SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) to reduce non-value-added time will reduce $t_{max}$.

2. **Re-balance the line by re-distributing tasks:** Use line balancing heuristics (e.g., **Longest Work Element**, **Ranked Positional Weight**, or **COMSOAL**) to split Station 5's work with adjacent stations, lowering $t_{max}$ toward the average.

3. **(Optional) Cross-train workers** to flexibly support the bottleneck when it falls behind.

> **(7 marks distributed: 2 for LE, 1 for BD, 4 for remedies with reasoning)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**A manufacturing firm operates a 5-station flow line producing a single product variant. The workstation times are:**

$$
t_1 = 3.5, \quad t_2 = 4.2, \quad t_3 = 4.2, \quad t_4 = 2.8, \quad t_5 = 3.3 \text{ min}
$$

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Compute the **cycle time, production rate, APT, line efficiency**, and the **time required to produce 200 units** in continuous production. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the firm installs an **improved fixture at Station 3** that reduces its time to 3.5 min, **recalculate the cycle time, production rate, and line efficiency**. Comment on whether the investment was worthwhile. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Original Configuration**

**Step 1:** Identify bottleneck:

$$
t_{max} = \max(3.5, 4.2, 4.2, 2.8, 3.3) = 4.2 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2:** Cycle Time:

$$
CT = 4.2 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Step 3:** Production Rate:

$$
PR = \frac{60}{4.2} = 14.29 \text{ units/hour}
$$

**Step 4:** Average Production Time:

$$
APT = 3.5 + 4.2 + 4.2 + 2.8 + 3.3 = 18.0 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5:** Line Efficiency:

$$
LE = \frac{18.0}{5 \times 4.2} = \frac{18}{21} = 0.8571 = 85.71\%
$$

**Step 6:** Time to produce 200 units:

$$
T_{200} = APT + (N-1) \cdot CT = 18 + 199 \times 4.2
$$

$$
T_{200} = 18 + 835.8 = 853.8 \text{ min} \approx 14.23 \text{ hours}
$$

> **(7 marks: 1+1+1+2+2 distribution)**

---

**Part (b) — After Improvement at Station 3**

**Step 7:** New times: $t_1=3.5, t_2=4.2, t_3=3.5, t_4=2.8, t_5=3.3$ min.

**Step 8:** New bottleneck:

$$
t_{max}^{new} = \max(3.5, 4.2, 3.5, 2.8, 3.3) = 4.2 \text{ min (still Station 2)}
$$

**Step 9:** New Cycle Time:

$$
CT^{new} = 4.2 \text{ min/unit} \quad \text{(unchanged!)}
$$

**Step 10:** New Production Rate:

$$
PR^{new} = \frac{60}{4.2} = 14.29 \text{ units/hour} \quad \text{(unchanged!)}
$$

**Step 11:** New APT:

$$
APT^{new} = 3.5 + 4.2 + 3.5 + 2.8 + 3.3 = 17.3 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 12:** New Line Efficiency:

$$
LE^{new} = \frac{17.3}{5 \times 4.2} = \frac{17.3}{21} = 0.8238 = 82.38\%
$$

**Step 13 — Comment on the Investment:**

The investment was **NOT worthwhile** for increasing output, because:
- Station 3 was **not the bottleneck** (Station 2 was). Improving a non-bottleneck station does not increase $PR$.
- $CT$, $PR$ remain unchanged.
- $LE$ actually **decreased** slightly (from 85.71% to 82.38%) because the *total work content* $\sum t_i$ decreased but $k \cdot t_{max}$ stayed the same, reducing the numerator of $LE$ proportionally.
- **Correct action:** Invest in **Station 2** (the actual bottleneck) to gain throughput improvements.

> **(7 marks: 2 for CT/PR, 2 for LE, 3 for critical comment)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert PR to units/hr:** Many students write $PR = 1/4.2$ units/min and stop. KTU expects explicit conversion: $PR = 60/t_{max}$ in units/hr. **Lose 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Confusing APT with Cycle Time:** APT = $\sum t_i$ (work content), while $CT = t_{max}$ (rate). Writing $APT = 4.2$ min instead of $18$ min is a **fatal conceptual error**, losing 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Not stating the bottleneck explicitly:** Always write "*Station X is the bottleneck with $t_{max} = $ Y min.*" Examiners award 1 mark for explicit bottleneck identification.
>
> 4. **Unitless LE in percentage form:** Always present $LE$ as a percentage in the final answer (e.g., $85.71\%$), not as a bare decimal.
>
> 5. **Missing the "Comment" in part (b):** KTU questions often test engineering judgment. Merely re-computing numbers without a qualitative conclusion (e.g., "investment was wasted because station was not the bottleneck") loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- [x] **Flow line** = series of workstations in a fixed sequence; $k$ stations, times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k$.
- [x] **Bottleneck** = station with the **largest** $t_i$; denoted $t_{max}$.
- [x] **Cycle Time (CT)** = $t_{max}$ — the rate-controlling time.
- [x] **Production Rate (PR)** = $1/CT$ units/min = $60/t_{max}$ units/hr.
- [x] **Average Production Time (APT)** = $\sum t_i$ (sum of all station times).
- [x] **Line Efficiency (LE)** = $\frac{\sum t_i}{k \cdot t_{max}} = \frac{APT}{k \cdot CT}$ (expressed as %).
- [x] **Balance Delay (BD)** = $1 - LE$ (fraction of time the line is imbalanced).
- [x] **Time for N units** = $APT + (N-1) \cdot CT$ (steady-state pipeline fill).
- [x] **WIP** (Little's Law) = $PR \cdot APT$ units in pipeline.
- [x] **Improving a non-bottleneck station never increases $PR$** — this is the *Theory of Constraints* in action.
- [x] A line is **perfectly balanced** iff every $t_i = t_{max}$, giving $LE = 100\%$.
- [x] The **order of stations does not change APT or PR**; it only changes *where* idle time occurs.
- [x] For KTU numerical answers: **always show** the bottleneck station, the formula with values plugged in, and the final answer with **correct units**.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Flow Line Performance Analysis**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Automation: Reasons, Advantages, and Drawbacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partial Automation: Reasons, Advantages, and Drawbacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Partial Automation** (also called *Hybrid Automation* or *Semi-Automation*) is a level of manufacturing automation in which **some operations** of a production system are performed by machines, automated equipment, or computer-controlled devices, while **other operations** continue to rely on human operators, manual interventions, or conventional machinery. The two streams of activity (human + machine) operate synergistically within the same workstation, production line, or process cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *Partial Automation is the controlled integration of mechanized/automated workstations with manual workstations to optimize the overall production system when full automation is technically infeasible or economically unjustified.*

The **degree of automation** in such a system can be expressed as:

$$\text{Degree of Automation (DOA)} = \frac{\text{Number of Automated Operations}}{\text{Total Number of Operations}} \times 100\%$$

A value of **0 %** denotes pure manual production, **100 %** denotes full automation, and any intermediate value represents partial automation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Modern Hospital Operating Theatre:**
> Imagine a surgeon (the human) performing a delicate operation while a robotic arm (the machine) holds the retractors with absolute, unshakeable steadiness. The robot does **not** replace the surgeon; rather, it handles the repetitive, high-precision, or ergonomically exhausting tasks so the human can focus on judgment, adaptability, and decision-making. The theatre is not fully robotic (no surgeon) and not fully manual (no robotic assistance) — it is **partially automated**.

A more industrial analogy: think of an **ATM (Automated Teller Machine)** at a bank. The machine handles cash dispensing, balance enquiry, and receipt printing (automated), but a bank officer is still required for loan approvals, KYC verification, dispute resolution, and complex customer interactions (manual). This is partial automation in the service sector; manufacturing has many direct counterparts.

### 1.3 Where Partial Automation Sits in the Automation Hierarchy

| Level | Description | DOA Range |
|-------|-------------|-----------|
| L0 | Fully Manual | 0 % |
| L1 | **Partial / Semi Automation** | **10 % – 90 %** |
| L2 | Full Automation of Workstation | ~100 % per station |
| L3 | Integrated / Flexible Automation | 100 % + reconfigurable |

### 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU

- **Workstation Automation Level:** The fraction of a single workstation's tasks handled by machines.
- **Hybrid Cell:** A manufacturing cell that contains both CNC machines and manual benches.
- **Human-Machine Interface (HMI):** The boundary where human judgement and machine execution exchange inputs/outputs.
- **Justification Threshold:** The minimum production volume or ROI beyond which higher automation is warranted.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Partial Automation

#### A. REASONS (Why a Company Chooses Partial Automation)

Partial automation is adopted when one or more of the following conditions exist:

1. **Economic Constraints**
   - Capital investment for full automation is prohibitive.
   - Pay-back period for full automation exceeds management's acceptable horizon (typically **2 – 5 years** for SMEs).
   - Product volume falls in the *medium batch* range — too high for manual but too low for full automation.

2. **Technical Constraints**
   - Some operations require **human dexterity, judgement, or sensory feedback** (e.g., final assembly, inspection of cosmetics, wiring harnesses).
   - Sensor technology for inspection, soft-material handling, or unstructured environments is still immature.
   - Tool changing, setup, and first-article inspection remain largely manual.

3. **Operational / Strategic Constraints**
   - High product variety / low volume mix (Job-shop / Batch production).
   - Need to retain **flexibility** for engineering changes and product upgrades.
   - Phased automation strategy — start with one station, scale gradually.
   - Workforce retention — unions and labour laws may require humans in certain roles.
   - Risk mitigation — diversifying capital expenditure across machines and labour reduces single-point failure.

4. **Demand & Market Constraints**
   - Demand fluctuations make rigid fully-automated lines a liability.
   - Customer-driven customization favours manual finishing touches.

#### B. ADVANTAGES

| # | Advantage | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Lower Initial Capital Investment** | Saves 30 – 70 % of CapEx compared to full automation. |
| 2 | **Faster Implementation** | Installation in weeks/months rather than years. |
| 3 | **Higher Flexibility** | Easy to switch products, change mix. |
| 4 | **Better Quality on Hard Tasks, Judgement on Soft Tasks** | Combines machine repeatability with human cognition. |
| 5 | **Easier Maintenance & Troubleshooting** | Humans can intervene when breakdowns occur. |
| 6 | **Lower Risk** | Failure of one automated cell does not paralyse the entire line. |
| 7 | **Smooth Workforce Transition** | Employees reskill rather than become redundant. |
| 8 | **Scalable Phased Investment** | ROI of each phase can be validated before next phase. |
| 9 | **Better Ergonomics** | Machines handle heavy/repetitive tasks; humans supervise. |
| 10 | **Shorter Pay-Back Period** | Often **< 18 months** for the automated portions. |

#### C. DRAWBACKS

| # | Drawback | Engineering Consequence |
|---|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Lower Throughput than Full Automation** | Bottleneck usually occurs at the manual station. |
| 2 | **Inconsistent Quality from Manual Stations** | Variation between operators, shifts, fatigue. |
| 3 | **Higher Per-Unit Labour Cost (in the long run)** | Recurring wage expenditure never disappears. |
| 4 | **Coordination & Scheduling Complexity** | Synchronising human cycle time with machine cycle time is non-trivial. |
| 5 | **Workplace Safety Issues** | Humans near moving machinery, pinch points, hot surfaces. |
| 6 | **Under-utilization of Machines** | If manual station is slow, automated station sits idle. |
| 7 | **Difficulty in Data Integration** | Manual stations often lack sensors, breaking the digital thread. |
| 8 | **Floor Space Inefficiency** | Manual benches need more aisle, lighting, ventilation than robots. |
| 9 | **Limited Scalability** | Cannot easily handle a 5× demand surge without major retrofit. |
| 10 | **Skill Dependency** | Quality still depends on operator training & morale. |

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar — A Decision Lens

- If a task is **deterministic, repetitive, hazardous, or high-precision** → automate it.
- If a task is **variable, judgemental, sensory-rich, or low-volume** → keep it manual.
- The art of partial automation is to **draw the boundary correctly** between these two zones for maximum economic and operational benefit.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|------------------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Degree of Automation | $DOA = \dfrac{N_{auto}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Classify system level |
| 2 | Effective Cycle Time | $T_{eff} = \max(T_{auto}, T_{manual})$ | s | Throughput analysis |
| 3 | Line Efficiency | $\eta_{line} = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{n \cdot T_{bottleneck}} \times 100$ | % | Bottleneck identification |
| 4 | Capacity Utilisation | $U_i = \dfrac{T_{required,i}}{T_{available,i}}$ | dimensionless | Resource load |
| 5 | Pay-Back Period | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{\Delta S_{annual}}$ | years | Economic justification |
| 6 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{Net\ Annual\ Benefit}{Investment} \times 100$ | % | Compare alternatives |
| 7 | Break-Even Volume | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{auto} - F_{manual}}{V_{manual} - V_{auto}}$ | units/year | Manual vs. auto decision |
| 8 | Manufacturing Cost per Unit | $C_u = \dfrac{F}{Q} + V$ | ₹/unit | Cost comparison |
| 9 | Labour Productivity | $LP = \dfrac{Output\ (units/hr)}{Labour\ Hours}$ | units/hr | Productivity gain |
| 10 | Worker Idle Time | $t_{idle} = T_{manual} - T_{auto}$ (if $T_{manual} > T_{auto}$) | s | Hidden cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *PARA → Partial Automation has Reasons, Advantages, and draw-backs.* Always structure your answer in this three-block format for full marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Partial automation is the **dominant form of automation in Indian and global SMEs** — it is estimated that over **70 % of Indian manufacturing units** operate at a partial-automation level. Understanding its trade-offs is essential for:
- Capital budgeting decisions in plant layout design.
- Designing ergonomic and safe human-machine work cells.
- Industry 4.0 migration roadmaps (start with partial → build toward full).
- Cost estimation in tender documents (automation cost per workstation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Cycle Time of a Hybrid Workstation

Consider a workstation with **one automated sub-station** and **one manual sub-station** working in series. Let the cycle times be $T_{auto}$ and $T_{manual}$.

**Step 1:** Identify the governing constraint.

In a serial (non-buffered) station, the next operation cannot start until the previous one finishes. The throughput is therefore governed by the **slower** of the two.

**Step 2:** Express the effective cycle time.

$$T_{eff} = \max(T_{auto}, T_{manual})$$

**Step 3:** Identify idle time on the faster station.

If $T_{auto} < T_{manual}$, the automated station is idle for:

$$t_{idle,auto} = T_{manual} - T_{auto}$$

**Step 4:** Compute overall line efficiency using the formula from the cheat sheet.

$$\eta_{line} = \frac{T_{auto} + T_{manual}}{2 \cdot \max(T_{auto}, T_{manual})} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 (Interpretation):** When $T_{auto} = T_{manual}$, efficiency is 100 %. As the gap widens, the line efficiency drops, and the system behaves more like the slower station — a classic bottleneck penalty.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Break-Even Volume Between Manual and Partial Automation

This is a **favourite KTU question**. The question is: *At what annual production volume does investing in partial automation become more economical than a fully manual system?*

**Step 1:** Define cost structures.

- **Manual System:**
  Fixed cost: $F_{manual}$ (tooling, benches, training)
  Variable cost: $V_{manual}$ (labour, consumables, rework) per unit.

- **Partial Automation System:**
  Fixed cost: $F_{auto}$ (machines, PLCs, installation) where $F_{auto} \gg F_{manual}$
  Variable cost: $V_{auto}$ (power, spares, reduced labour) per unit.

**Step 2:** Write total annual cost for each.

$$C_{manual}(Q) = F_{manual} + V_{manual} \cdot Q$$

$$C_{auto}(Q) = F_{auto} + V_{auto} \cdot Q$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two cost equations to find the break-even point.

$$F_{manual} + V_{manual} \cdot Q_{BE} = F_{auto} + V_{auto} \cdot Q_{BE}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange.

$$Q_{BE} \cdot (V_{manual} - V_{auto}) = F_{auto} - F_{manual}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $Q_{BE}$.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F_{auto} - F_{manual}}{V_{manual} - V_{auto}}$$

**Interpretation Rule for KTU:**

- If annual demand $Q > Q_{BE}$ → **Partial automation is justified.**
- If annual demand $Q < Q_{BE}$ → **Stick with manual.**
- If $V_{manual} \le V_{auto}$ (automation does not reduce variable cost), partial automation can never be justified economically.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pay-Back Period for the Automated Portion

**Step 1:** Define investment.

$$I_{auto} = F_{auto} - F_{manual}$$

(the incremental cost of moving from manual to partial).

**Step 2:** Define annual savings.

The saving comes from reduced variable cost per unit:

$$\Delta S_{annual} = (V_{manual} - V_{auto}) \cdot Q$$

**Step 3:** Pay-Back Period.

$$PBP = \frac{I_{auto}}{\Delta S_{annual}} = \frac{F_{auto} - F_{manual}}{(V_{manual} - V_{auto}) \cdot Q}$$

**Step 4:** ROI.

$$ROI = \frac{\Delta S_{annual} \cdot L - I_{auto}}{I_{auto} \times L} \times 100\%$$

where $L$ is the equipment's useful life in years.

**Decision Rule:** Most KTU/Kerala industrial houses accept PBP ≤ **2 years** and ROI ≥ **25 % per annum** for partial automation projects.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Operations to Automate

| Task Attribute | Manual Better | Partial Automation Sweet Spot | Full Automation Better |
|----------------|---------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| Volume per year | < 5 000 units | **5 000 – 1 00 000 units** | > 1 00 000 units |
| Product Variety | Very High | **Medium** | Very Low (single product) |
| Cycle Time Required | > 60 s | **5 – 60 s** | < 5 s |
| Precision Required | ± 0.5 mm | **± 0.05 – 0.5 mm** | < ± 0.05 mm |
| Operator Skill | High skill artisan | **Semi-skilled + machine** | Minimal skill |
| Hazardous Operation | None | **Some shielded** | Fully enclosed |
| Inspection Type | Aesthetic, subjective | **Dimensional + visual hybrid** | 100 % sensor-based |

### 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-style Numerical)

**Problem:** A firm manufactures a component either fully manually or with partial automation. The data is:

- $F_{manual} = \text{₹ }50{,}000$
- $F_{auto} = \text{₹ }3{,}00{,}000$
- $V_{manual} = \text{₹ }120/\text{unit}$
- $V_{auto} = \text{₹ }40/\text{unit}$

Annual demand $Q = 10{,}000$ units. Advise.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute break-even volume.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{120 - 40} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{80} = 3{,}125 \text{ units}$$

Step 2: Compare with demand.

Since $Q = 10{,}000 > Q_{BE} = 3{,}125$, **partial automation is economically justified**.

Step 3: Compute total cost of automation.

$$C_{auto} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 40 \times 10{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000$$

Step 4: Total cost of manual.

$$C_{manual} = 50{,}000 + 120 \times 10{,}000 = 12{,}50{,}000$$

Step 5: Annual savings.

$$\Delta S = 12{,}50{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000$$

Step 6: Pay-Back Period.

$$PBP = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} \approx 0.45 \text{ years} \approx 5.5 \text{ months}$$

**Verdict:** Strongly recommend partial automation. ✔

---

### 3.6 Mathematical Boundary for "Degree of Automation"

A useful concept for KTU essays is the **crossover point of DOA** at which the system transitions from "partial" to "essentially full" — typically taken as:

$$DOA_{crossover} \approx \frac{T_{manual} - T_{auto}}{T_{manual} + T_{auto}} \times 100\%$$

For instance, if $T_{auto} = 8$ s and $T_{manual} = 12$ s:

$$DOA_{crossover} \approx \frac{12 - 8}{12 + 8} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

Meaning, automating **20 %** of the operations (the slower ones) shifts the effective cycle time to the faster end — the system behaves "almost fully" automated in throughput terms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of a Partial-Automation Workstation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Material Input Bay]
        A1[Raw Material Storage]
    end

    subgraph AutoZone[Automated Zone]
        B1[Auto Loader / Feeder]
        B2[CNC Machine / Robot]
        B3[Automated Inspection Sensor]
    end

    subgraph HumanZone[Manual Zone]
        C1[Manual Assembly Bench]
        C2[Visual Quality Check by Operator]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output and Dispatch]
        D1[Finished Goods Buffer]
        D2[Packaging Station]
    end

    subgraph Control[Supervisory Control Layer]
        E1[PLC / SCADA HMI]
        E2[Production Scheduler]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2

    E1 -. monitors .-> B2
    E1 -. monitors .-> B3
    E2 -. schedules .-> E1
    E2 -. informs .-> C1
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **Automated Zone** handles loading, machining, and sensor-based inspection.
- The **Manual Zone** handles assembly and subjective visual checks.
- The **Control Layer** supervises machines and informs the human operator of schedule.
- Feedback lines (dotted) represent information flow, not material flow.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing Partial Automation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start: New Production Requirement]
    q1{Annual demand greater than 5000 units}
    q2{Task is repetitive and deterministic}
    q3{Human judgement still needed for some steps}
    q4{Capital available for partial investment}
    q5{Pay back period less than 2 years}
    decide1[Recommend Full Automation]
    decide2[Recommend Partial Automation]
    decide3[Stick with Manual Operations]
    decide4[Defer Decision Re evaluate in 1 year]

    start1 --> q1
    q1 -- No --> decide3
    q1 -- Yes --> q2
    q2 -- No --> decide3
    q2 -- Yes --> q3
    q3 -- No --> q1
    q3 -- Yes --> q4
    q4 -- No --> decide4
    q4 -- Yes --> q5
    q5 -- Yes --> decide2
    q5 -- No --> decide1
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — The Three Pillars of Partial Automation

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Partial Automation))
    Reasons
      Capital constraints
      Technical limits
      Product variety
      Workforce policy
      Phased investment
    Advantages
      Lower CapEx
      Higher flexibility
      Faster installation
      Better ergonomics
      Easier maintenance
      Smoother workforce transition
    Drawbacks
      Manual bottleneck
      Quality variation
      Coordination cost
      Data fragmentation
      Safety risks
      Limited scalability
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — Operator & Machine Interaction in a Hybrid Cell

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Human Operator
    participant PLC as PLC Controller
    participant Robot as CNC Machine
    participant Insp as Vision Sensor
    participant Pack as Packing Station

    Op->>PLC: Start button press
    PLC->>Robot: Cycle start command
    Robot->>Robot: Machining cycle 8 s
    Robot->>PLC: Cycle complete signal
    PLC->>Insp: Trigger inline inspection
    Insp->>PLC: Pass Fail flag
    PLC->>Op: Display result on HMI
    alt Pass
        Op->>Pack: Forward good part
    else Fail
        Op->>Op: Rework or reject
    end
    Op->>PLC: Confirm next cycle ready
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`**

**Q1. Define partial automation. Give any two situations in which it is preferred over full automation.**

**Model Answer:**
Partial automation is a level of automation in which a production system uses both machines and human operators to complete the manufacturing tasks, with the degree of automation lying between 0 % and 100 %.

Two situations where it is preferred:

1. **Capital constraint:** When the firm cannot afford the high initial investment of full automation.
2. **High product variety:** When the product mix changes frequently, requiring human flexibility that rigid full-automation lines cannot offer.

*(Valid alternatives: technical infeasibility of automating inspection/assembly, phased automation strategy, labour policies.)*

---

**Q2. List any three advantages of partial automation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Lower initial capital investment compared to full automation.
2. Higher flexibility to handle product variety and engineering changes.
3. Faster installation and quicker realisation of benefits (shorter pay-back period).

*(Any three of the ten advantages listed in the table — credit for clarity of expression.)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`**

**Q3. Mention any three drawbacks of partial automation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Manual operations create a bottleneck, limiting throughput compared to full automation.
2. Quality variation persists due to differences between human operators and shifts.
3. Coordination and scheduling of human and machine cycle times adds complexity.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]`**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the various **reasons** for adopting partial automation in a manufacturing industry. (7 marks)

**(b)** *Discuss the major **advantages and drawbacks** of partial automation. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### (a) Reasons for Partial Automation — 7 marks

A manufacturing firm may opt for partial automation due to the following reasons:

**1. Economic Reasons (2 marks)**
- The capital investment for full automation is often too high for SMEs.
- Pay-back period for full automation may exceed acceptable limits.
- Cash-flow constraints force a phased investment strategy.

**2. Technical Reasons (2 marks)**
- Some operations (final assembly, wiring, cosmetics inspection) still require human dexterity and judgement.
- Sensor technology may not be mature for 100 % automated inspection.
- Frequent engineering changes make full automation hard to re-tool.

**3. Operational / Strategic Reasons (2 marks)**
- High product variety with medium batch size.
- Need to retain workforce for social and legal reasons.
- Risk diversification — not putting all capital in one automated line.

**4. Demand / Market Reasons (1 mark)**
- Uncertain demand makes fully automated rigid lines risky.
- Customer-driven customisation favours partial automation.

#### (b) Advantages and Drawbacks — 7 marks

**Advantages (3.5 marks):**
- Lower capital investment — saves 30 – 70 % of CapEx.
- Greater flexibility for product mix and volume changes.
- Faster implementation, shorter pay-back period.
- Easier maintenance and troubleshooting by human intervention.
- Better ergonomics — machines handle heavy/repetitive work.
- Smooth workforce transition — labour is reskilled, not displaced.
- Scalable — additional automated stations can be added later.

**Drawbacks (3.5 marks):**
- Lower overall throughput due to manual bottlenecks.
- Quality variation from manual stations.
- Higher long-term labour cost (wages never disappear).
- Complex scheduling — synchronising human and machine cycle times.
- Safety concerns for operators near moving machinery.
- Data integration challenges — manual stations often break the digital thread.
- Limited scalability in case of sudden demand surge.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often write "advantages" and "drawbacks" but forget to **link them with the degree of automation DOA**. Always mention that partial automation is justified in the **5 000 – 1 00 000 units/year band** with a **DOA between 10 % and 90 %**. Missing the quantitative anchor costs **1 – 2 marks**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A component can be produced either manually or with partial automation. The data is:

- Manual: $F = \text{₹ }40{,}000$, $V = \text{₹ }110/\text{unit}$
- Partial automation: $F = \text{₹ }2{,}80{,}000$, $V = \text{₹ }35/\text{unit}$

If annual demand is **8 000 units**, determine (i) the break-even volume, (ii) total cost of each system, and (iii) advise the firm. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define the **Degree of Automation (DOA)**. With a suitable block diagram, describe the architecture of a partially automated workstation. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### (a) Numerical — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Break-Even Volume.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F_{auto} - F_{manual}}{V_{manual} - V_{auto}} = \frac{2{,}80{,}000 - 40{,}000}{110 - 35} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{75} = 3{,}200 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 2:** Total cost of manual system at $Q = 8{,}000$:

$$C_{manual} = 40{,}000 + 110 \times 8{,}000 = 40{,}000 + 8{,}80{,}000 = 9{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Total cost of partial automation at $Q = 8{,}000$:

$$C_{auto} = 2{,}80{,}000 + 35 \times 8{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000 = 5{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Annual savings:

$$\Delta S = 9{,}20{,}000 - 5{,}60{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 5:** Pay-Back Period on incremental investment:

$$PBP = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{3{,}60{,}000} = 0.667 \text{ years} \approx 8 \text{ months}$$

**Step 6 (Verdict):**
Since demand $Q = 8{,}000 > Q_{BE} = 3{,}200$ and PBP is only 8 months (well under 2 years), the firm should adopt **partial automation**. ✔

#### (b) Conceptual + Diagram — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Degree of Automation (DOA) is the ratio of automated operations to total operations, expressed as a percentage:

$$DOA = \frac{N_{auto}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Architecture (5 marks):**

A partially automated workstation typically consists of:

1. **Material Input Bay** — where raw parts are received and queued.
2. **Automated Zone** — includes feeders, CNC / robotic machine, and sensor-based inspection.
3. **Manual Zone** — includes operator assembly bench and visual quality check.
4. **Output / Buffer** — finished good storage and packaging.
5. **Control Layer** — PLC / SCADA HMI for machine supervision and operator display.

(Students should reproduce a block diagram similar to the one in Section 4.1, clearly marking material flow with solid arrows and information flow with dashed arrows.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit mismatch:** $V$ is in ₹/unit and $F$ in ₹ — keep units consistent. Marks deducted for not writing units in the final answer.
> - **No diagram label:** If you draw the block diagram but forget to label **Material Flow** vs **Information Flow**, you lose **1 mark** in the 7-mark question.
> - **Forgot "verdict sentence":** Always end numericals with a one-line recommendation. Examiners award the final **1 mark** specifically for the conclusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Partial Automation = Man + Machine** in the same production system, with $0\% < DOA < 100\%$.
- [x] It is the **default choice** in Indian SMEs and in medium-batch / high-variety scenarios.
- [x] **Reasons:** economic, technical, operational, strategic, and demand-driven.
- [x] **Biggest Advantage:** lower CapEx and faster pay-back.
- [x] **Biggest Drawback:** the **manual bottleneck** that limits throughput and creates quality variation.
- [x] **Effective cycle time** = max of automatic and manual cycle times — always identify the bottleneck.
- [x] **Break-even volume** formula is the most-tested KTU numerical — memorise $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{auto} - F_{manual}}{V_{manual} - V_{auto}}$.
- [x] **Pay-Back Period ≤ 2 years** and **ROI ≥ 25 %/year** are the typical acceptance thresholds.
- [x] Partial automation is a **stepping stone** to full automation and Industry 4.0 — not a permanent endpoint.
- [x] Use the **P-A-R-A structure** (Reasons → Advantages → Drawbacks) in every theory answer for full marks.
- [x] Always include a **diagram** (block diagram / flow chart) for any 7-mark or 14-mark question — KTU examiners award dedicated marks for it.
- [x] Final answer should always have a **one-line verdict** for numericals and a **one-line implication** for theory answers.

> **End of Module 2 — Partial Automation Notes | PCRUT403 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component Quality Control and Effects of Machine Jamming
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Component Quality Control and Effects of Machine Jamming

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Component Quality Control (CQC) — Formal Definition

**Component Quality Control (CQC)** in the context of manufacturing automation refers to the systematic, sensor-driven, and statistically-validated framework used to ensure that every part produced by an automated cell, Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS), or Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) line conforms to its engineering design specifications — namely **dimensional tolerances, surface finish, material properties, and functional performance** — without relying on 100% manual inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under the **PCRUT403 – Manufacturing Automation (2024 Scheme)** syllabus, Component Quality Control is treated as a closed-loop feedback subsystem of automation. It encompasses **(a) In-Process Inspection (IPI)** using on-machine probes, **(b) Post-Process Verification** via Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM), and **(c) Statistical Process Control (SPC)** using control charts.

Mathematically, quality conformance in an automated batch of $N$ components is expressed as:

$$\text{Quality Conformance Ratio (QCR)} = \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_{\text{good}}$ is the number of components lying inside the Upper Specification Limit (USL) and Lower Specification Limit (LSL), and $N_{\text{total}}$ is the total components inspected in the lot.

---

### 1.2 Machine Jamming — Formal Definition

**Machine Jamming** is an abnormal operating condition in an automated production line wherein a moving element (e.g., a part on a conveyor, a pallet in an AS/RS, a tool in an automatic tool changer, or a workpiece in a machining fixture) becomes **physically obstructed, stuck, or misaligned**, halting the production cycle. In KTU terminology, jamming is classified as a **"catastrophic downtime event"** because its effects cascade across all upstream and downstream workstations sharing the material-handling system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:**
> Jamming is the **#1 cause of unplanned downtime** in transfer lines and FMS. The Automotive Industry Action Group (AIAG) reports that **jamming contributes to approximately 30% of all line stoppages** in high-volume automated plants.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Quality Control Analogy — The Airport Security Model:**
Imagine an airport security checkpoint. Every passenger (component) must pass through a metal detector (sensor) before boarding a flight. The detector screens 100% of passengers — this is **automated inspection**. Additionally, a supervisor randomly pulls aside a few passengers for deeper checks — this is **statistical sampling**. If many "fail" the check, the entire security line is paused and recalibrated — this is **process control**. Component Quality Control works exactly this way: *continuous screening + statistical verification + feedback-driven correction*.

**Machine Jamming Analogy — The Multi-Lane Highway Bottleneck:**
Picture a 4-lane highway (production line) merging into a single toll booth (a workstation). If one car stalls at the booth, traffic piles up behind it (buffer overflow), idle lanes ahead (downstream starvation), and emergency crews must remove the obstruction (recovery time). The "stalled car" is the **jammed component**, the queue behind is the **back-pressure**, and the empty lanes ahead are **starvation** — both are direct consequences of jamming.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in CQC

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | **DPMO** | **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO** |
| Process Capability Index | $C_p$ | $\geq 1.33$ (capable) |
| Process Capability Index (shifted) | $C_{pk}$ | $\geq 1.33$ (capable & centered) |
| Control Limits | UCL, LCL | $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (3-sigma rule) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are **bold-marked** because they are recurring high-yield items in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillars of Component Quality Control in Automation

Component Quality Control in an automated environment rests on **three operational pillars**:

1. **Sensing & Measurement Layer (Hardware)**
   - In-process sensors: LVDTs, capacitive probes, laser micrometers, vision systems
   - Tool-condition monitors (force, vibration, acoustic emission)
   - Coolant-pressure and temperature sensors
   - *Why?* Real-time data is the fuel for every quality decision.

2. **Statistical Decision Layer (Software/Algorithm)**
   - Control charts ($\bar{X}$–R, $p$–charts, $c$–charts)
   - Process Capability Indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$)
   - Six-Sigma DMAIC framework (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control)

3. **Feedback & Actuation Layer (Control)**
   - Adaptive control loops (e.g., CNC tool-offset compensation)
   - Reject mechanism (pneumatic diverter)
   - Automatic rework routing via Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A line that **measures but does not feed back** is not automated quality control — it is merely data logging. True CQC **closes the loop**.

---

### 2.2 Six-Sigma Quality Framework

Six-Sigma is a **data-driven methodology** that seeks to reduce process variation such that the nearest specification limit is at least **6 standard deviations ($\sigma$)** away from the process mean ($\mu$).

The relationship between Sigma level and yield is:

$$Z_{\text{LT}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}, \qquad Z_{\text{LST}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{\sigma}$$

The smaller of the two is the **process Z-score**. Yield is computed using the standard normal CDF $\Phi(Z)$:

$$Y = \Phi(Z_{\text{LT}}) - \Phi(-Z_{\text{LST}})$$

The **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is then:

$$\text{DPMO} = 10^6 \times (1 - Y)$$

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.1 |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.3 |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38 |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977 |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.9997** |

---

### 2.3 Process Capability Indices — Derivation Logic

The **Process Capability Index $C_p$** quantifies how well a process output fits within the specification width, assuming the process mean is centered:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

The **Adjusted Capability Index $C_{pk}$** accounts for process mean shift from the target:

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Interpretation Rules (Recall for KTU):**
- $C_p = C_{pk}$ → process is **centered**.
- $C_p > C_{pk}$ → process is **off-center** (one-sided risk).
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → process is **capable**.
- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → process is **not capable** of meeting specification.

---

### 2.4 Control Chart Limits for Variables Data ($\bar{X}$–R Chart)

For a sample subgroup of size $n$ taken from a stable process:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$$
$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \qquad \text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$

where $\bar{\bar{X}}$ is the grand average, $\bar{R}$ is the average range, and $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ are constants tabulated against sample size $n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ are **derived from the 3-sigma rule**. For $n = 5$: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. These constants are **frequently asked in KTU Part A (3 marks).**

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quality Conformance Ratio | $\text{QCR} = \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Direct count |
| 2 | DPMO | $10^6 \cdot (1-Y)$ | defects/M opp. | Yield $Y \in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | Yield | $\Phi(Z_{\text{LT}}) - \Phi(-Z_{\text{LST}})$ | dimensionless | Normal CDF |
| 4 | Process Capability | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL}-\text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Centered only |
| 5 | Adjusted Capability | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Off-center aware |
| 6 | Control Limit (mean) | $\text{UCL}_{\bar X} = \bar{\bar X} + A_2\bar R$ | same as $X$ | $A_2$ from table |
| 7 | Control Limit (range) | $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar R$ | same as $R$ | $D_3=0$ for $n\le 6$ |
| 8 | Six-Sigma Short-Term Shift | $1.5\sigma$ mean shift | σ-units | Convention |
| 9 | OEE Availability | $\dfrac{\text{Planned Time - Downtime}}{\text{Planned Time}}$ | fraction | Includes jamming |
| 10 | MTBF | $\dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{No. of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability |

> **Note:** All vertical bars in expressions (e.g., the absolute distance $\vert \mu - \text{LSL}\vert$) use $\vert\,\cdot\,\vert$ written with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Six-Sigma reduces warranty claims; BMW, Toyota, Ford are certified 6σ manufacturers.
- **Semiconductor:** $C_{pk} > 1.67$ is mandatory for wafer-fab lithography layers.
- **Aerospace:** AS9100 standard mandates SPC on every critical characteristic.
- **FMS Bottleneck:** Jamming is the largest contributor to **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** loss in discrete manufacturing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: $C_p$ from First Principles

We begin with the specification window (tolerance) defined by:

$$T = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$$

For a normally distributed process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the **practical spread of the process** (encompassing 99.73% of output under a stable process) is:

$$\text{Process Spread} = 6\sigma \quad \text{(the "voice of the process")}$$

A process is "capable" if its natural spread fits inside the tolerance window with adequate margin. The ratio of tolerance to spread is the **Process Capability Index**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{Tolerance Window}}{\text{Process Natural Spread}} \\
    &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $C_p = 1$ means the process spread **exactly equals** the tolerance — risky because any shift will produce defects. $C_p = 1.33$ provides a safety buffer of one-third of the spread. $C_p = 2.0$ is **world-class** (used by Six-Sigma black-belt projects).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: $C_{pk}$ with Mean Shift

When the process mean drifts from the midpoint $\mu_0 = \tfrac{\text{USL}+\text{LSL}}{2}$, the **one-sided capability** on each limit becomes asymmetric:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

The overall $C_{pk}$ is the **minimum** of the two (worst-case side dominates defect generation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\;\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A process can have $C_p = 2.0$ but $C_{pk} = 0.8$ if it is severely shifted — the $C_{pk}$ correctly flags this by reporting only the **vulnerable side**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Computing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

**Problem (KTU-style):** A CNC turning process produces shafts with $\text{USL} = 25.10\,\text{mm}$, $\text{LSL} = 24.90\,\text{mm}$. The process mean is $\mu = 25.03\,\text{mm}$ and the standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.03\,\text{mm}$. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on capability.

**Step 1 — Tolerance width:**

$$T = 25.10 - 24.90 = 0.20\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Process spread (6σ):**

$$6\sigma = 6 \times 0.03 = 0.18\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{T}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.20}{0.18} \\
    &= 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk,\text{upper}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.03}{3 \times 0.03} \\
                    &= \frac{0.07}{0.09} \\
                    &= 0.778
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{pk,\text{lower}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.03 - 24.90}{0.09} \\
                    &= \frac{0.13}{0.09} \\
                    &= 1.444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the minimum:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.778,\; 1.444) = 0.778$$

**Interpretation:**

- $C_p = 1.111$ → the process spread *barely fits* inside the tolerance (marginally capable).
- $C_{pk} = 0.778$ → the process is **off-center toward USL**, and is **NOT capable** ($C_{pk} < 1.00$).
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the spindle offset to bring $\mu$ toward $25.00\,\text{mm}$ and reduce variability.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: DPMO and Six-Sigma Level

**Problem:** A process has $\mu = 50\,\text{g}$, $\sigma = 1.5\,\text{g}$. Specification: $50 \pm 5\,\text{g}$. Compute DPMO and identify the nearest sigma level.

**Step 1 — Compute Z-scores (since process is centered, both Z-scores are equal):**

$$Z_{\text{LT}} = \frac{55 - 50}{1.5} = 3.333, \qquad Z_{\text{LST}} = \frac{50 - 45}{1.5} = 3.333$$

**Step 2 — Yield from normal tables:**

$$Y = \Phi(3.333) - \Phi(-3.333) = 2\,\Phi(3.333) - 1$$

Using $\Phi(3.33) \approx 0.99957$:

$$Y \approx 2(0.99957) - 1 = 0.99914$$

**Step 3 — DPMO:**

$$\text{DPMO} = 10^6 \times (1 - 0.99914) = 10^6 \times 0.00086 = 860\,\text{DPMO}$$

**Step 4 — Sigma level (with 1.5σ long-term shift convention):**

$$\text{Long-term sigma} = Z_{\text{LT}} + 1.5 = 3.333 + 1.5 = 4.833\,\sigma \approx 4.83\sigma$$

> **Logic:** This process is operating between 4σ and 5σ quality — good but **not yet world-class** (requires 6σ).

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3: $\bar{X}$–R Control Chart Limits

**Problem:** A subgroup of size $n = 5$ is sampled 25 times from a CNC milling operation. The grand average is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.520\,\text{mm}$ and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.040\,\text{mm}$. Construct the $\bar{X}$ chart control limits.

**Step 1 — Identify $A_2$ for $n=5$:** $A_2 = 0.577$ (from SPC tables).

**Step 2 — UCL of $\bar{X}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
                    &= 12.520 + (0.577)(0.040) \\
                    &= 12.520 + 0.02308 \\
                    &= 12.54308\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — LCL of $\bar{X}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} \\
                    &= 12.520 - 0.02308 \\
                    &= 12.49692\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Decision rule:** Any subgroup mean falling outside $[12.49692,\; 12.54308]\,\text{mm}$ indicates the process is **out of statistical control** — an assignable cause is suspected and must be investigated.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Effects of Machine Jamming on a Production Line

| Effect Category | Quantitative Indicator | Typical Range | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput Loss** | $\Delta P$ (parts/hour lost) | 10–60% drop | Line stoppage propagation |
| **Buffer Overflow** | WIP accumulation rate | Buffer +20–80% | Upstream keeps feeding |
| **Downstream Starvation** | Idle workstation time | 5–30% of shift | No parts arriving |
| **Tool/Fixture Damage** | Mean time to repair (MTTR) | 15 min – 4 hr | Force exerted during stall |
| **Quality Degradation** | Defect rate spike | 2× to 10× baseline | Restart transient |
| **Energy Waste** | Idle kWh during jam | Variable | Motors held in torque mode |
| **OEE Drop** | OEE loss | 5–25% absolute | Combined above |

> [!NOTE]
> **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality.** A single jamming event reduces **availability**, which cascades into lower performance (slow restart) and lower quality (warm-up cycle scraps).

---

### 3.7 Anti-Jamming Design Equations

The **probability of jamming** in a parts-feeding track can be modeled as a function of part-to-chute geometry:

$$P_{\text{jam}} = f\!\left(\frac{W_{\text{part}}}{W_{\text{chute}},\; \frac{L_{\text{part}}}{W_{\text{chute}},\; \theta_{\text{chute}}\right)$$

A simplified **Bowden's equation** for granular jamming probability:

$$P_{\text{jam}} \propto \exp\!\left(-\alpha\,\frac{W_{\text{chute}} - W_{\text{part}}}{W_{\text{part}}}\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is a material friction constant. Larger clearance $\Rightarrow$ lower jamming probability, but at the cost of orientation control.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Component Quality Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CNC Machining Station] --> B[In-Process Sensors]
    B --> C[LVDT Probe]
    B --> D[Laser Micrometer]
    B --> E[Vision Camera]
    C --> F[Data Acquisition PLC]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Statistical Engine SPC]
    G --> H{Within Control Limits?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Continue Production]
    H -- No --> J[Alarm and Reject Path]
    J --> K[Adjust Spindle Offset]
    K --> A
    G --> L[Quality Database Server]
    L --> M[Dashboards and Reports]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a **feedback-corrective loop**. If the SPC engine detects that a measurement exceeds $\text{UCL}_{\bar X}$ or $\text{LCL}_{\bar X}$, a corrective offset is fed back to the CNC controller — this is the essence of **adaptive quality control** in modern FMS.

---

### 4.2 Six-Sigma DMAIC Roadmap (Quality Improvement Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Define Problem] --> S2[Measure Baseline]
    S2 --> S3[Analyze Root Cause]
    S3 --> S4[Improve Process]
    S4 --> S5[Control Sustain]
    S5 --> S6{Review Sigma Level}
    S6 -- Below 6sigma --> S1
    S6 -- Reached 6sigma --> S7[Document Best Practice]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each DMAIC stage outputs a deliverable — Define (Project Charter), Measure (Baseline $C_{pk}$), Analyze (Pareto, Ishikawa), Improve (DOE), Control (Control Plan). KTU examiners frequently test the *sequence* of DMAIC.

---

### 4.3 Machine Jamming — Cascade Effect on FMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[Workstation 4 JAMMED] --> J2[Buffer B3 Overflow]
    J2 --> J3[Workstation 3 Back-Pressure]
    J3 --> J4[Workstation 3 Auto-Stop]
    J4 --> J5[Buffer B2 Overflow]
    J5 --> J6[Workstation 2 Back-Pressure]
    J6 --> J7[Workstation 2 Auto-Stop]
    J7 --> J8[Central Controller Alarm]
    J8 --> J9[Maintenance Dispatch]
    J9 --> J10[Unjamming and Reset]
    J10 --> J11[Sequential Restart W1]
    J11 --> J12[Sequential Restart W2]
    J12 --> J13[Sequential Restart W3]
    J13 --> J14[Sequential Restart W4]
    J14 --> J15[Production Resumed]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Jamming in workstation 4 propagates **upstream** (queues build) and **downstream** (idle starvation). Restart must be **sequential in reverse order** (W4 → W3 → W2 → W1) to avoid buffer drainage shocks.

---

### 4.4 Anti-Jamming Subsystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Part Infeed Track] --> S1[Presence Sensor]
    S1 --> S2[Jam Detection Logic PLC]
    S2 --> T{Timer Expired?}
    T -- Yes --> A1[Trigger Pneumatic Reverse Pulse]
    T -- No --> P
    A1 --> P
    S2 --> A2[Alert HMI Operator]
    A2 --> M[Manual Intervention]
    M --> P
```

> **Reading Guide:** Modern anti-jamming systems use a **detect-reverse-retry** strategy: if a part does not clear the sensor within a set window, a short reverse air-pulse dislodges it. If retry fails, the HMI alerts a human.

---

### 4.5 Root-Cause Ishikawa (Fishbone) Template for Jamming

```mermaid
fishbone
    Problem[JAMMING EVENT] --> M1[Machine: Misalignment Worn guides]
    Problem --> M2[Method: Wrong feed rate]
    Problem --> M3[Material: Burrs Oversize parts]
    Problem --> M4[Manpower: Operator error]
    Problem --> M5[Measurement: Sensor failure]
    Problem --> M6[Environment: Humidity Dust]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **6M framework** is the standard KTU-examined approach for diagnosing jamming root causes.
