## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Process Capability Index $C_p$ and state the conditions under which $C_{pk}$ differs from $C_p$.**

**Model Answer:**

The Process Capability Index $C_p$ quantifies the ability of a stable process to produce output within specification limits, defined as:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$C_{pk}$ differs from $C_p$ when the process mean $\mu$ is **not centered** at the midpoint of the specification band:

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\;\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

If $\mu = \tfrac{\text{USL}+\text{LSL}}{2}$, then $C_p = C_{pk}$. Otherwise $C_{pk} < C_p$, indicating a one-sided capability loss.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (2), condition (1).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three causes and three effects of machine jamming in an automated production line.**

**Model Answer:**

**Causes:** (1) Part burrs and dimensional oversize, (2) Misaligned transfer track or pallet fixture, (3) Sensor failure or foreign-object contamination.

**Effects:** (1) Upstream buffer overflow and back-pressure stoppage, (2) Downstream workstation starvation and idle time, (3) Quality degradation due to restart transients and tool wear from stall torque.

**Mark Distribution:** Any 3 causes (1.5), any 3 effects (1.5).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**a)** Define Six-Sigma quality. With a neat block diagram, explain the **DMAIC** methodology for achieving Six-Sigma quality in an automated manufacturing line. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A turning process produces shafts with specification $\text{USL} = 30.05\,\text{mm}$ and $\text{LSL} = 29.95\,\text{mm}$. After 25 subgroups of size 5, the process mean is $\mu = 30.02\,\text{mm}$ and standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.02\,\text{mm}$. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, DPMO, and comment on the sigma quality level. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Six-Sigma Definition:**
Six-Sigma is a disciplined, data-driven methodology that aims to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, corresponding to a process where the specification limits are at least $\pm 6\sigma$ from the mean (after accounting for a $1.5\sigma$ long-term shift).

**DMAIC Stages (with manufacturing examples):**

| Stage | Activity | Example in Automated Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **D**efine | Identify CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) parameters | Shaft diameter tolerance |
| **M**easure | Collect baseline data, install gauges | Mount laser micrometer on lathe |
| **A**nalyze | Identify root causes using Pareto, Ishikawa, Regression | Vibration → surface roughness |
| **I**mprove | Implement DOE-based optimal settings | Reduce feed, optimize RPM |
| **C**ontrol | Lock gains via Control Plan and SPC charts | Maintain $\bar X$–$R$ chart on HMI |

```
Define -> Measure -> Analyze -> Improve -> Control (loop back if <6sigma)
```

**Mark Split:** Definition (2), DMAIC stages (3), Manufacturing examples (2).

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\text{USL} = 30.05\,\text{mm}$, $\text{LSL} = 29.95\,\text{mm}$, $\mu = 30.02\,\text{mm}$, $\sigma = 0.02\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{30.05 - 29.95}{6(0.02)} = \frac{0.10}{0.12} = 0.833$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{30.05 - 30.02}{3(0.02)} = \frac{0.03}{0.06} = 0.500$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{30.02 - 29.95}{0.06} = \frac{0.07}{0.06} = 1.167$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.500,\; 1.167) = 0.500$$

**Step 3 — Compute DPMO:**

$$Z_{\text{LT}} = \frac{30.05 - 30.02}{0.02} = 1.5, \qquad Z_{\text{LST}} = \frac{30.02 - 29.95}{0.02} = 3.5$$

$$Y = \Phi(1.5) - \Phi(-3.5) = 0.9332 - 0.000233 = 0.9320$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 10^6 \times (1 - 0.9320) = 10^6 \times 0.0680 = 68{,}000\,\text{DPMO}$$

**Step 4 — Sigma Level:**

$$\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = Z_{\text{min}} + 1.5 = 1.5 + 1.5 = 3.0\,\sigma$$

**Conclusion:** The process is operating at **3σ quality**, far below Six-Sigma. $C_{pk} = 0.500 < 1.0$ confirms the process is **not capable**, primarily due to **mean shift toward USL** (upper-side risk). Corrective action: re-center $\mu$ toward 30.00 mm and reduce variability.

**Mark Split:** $C_p$ (1.5), $C_{pk}$ (2), DPMO (2), Sigma level + interpretation (1.5).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**a)** What is machine jamming in an FMS? Explain with a block diagram the **propagation of jamming effects** through upstream and downstream workstations. List four anti-jamming techniques. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A broaching process is monitored using an $\bar X$–$R$ chart with subgroup size $n = 5$. After 20 subgroups, $\bar{\bar X} = 45.20\,\text{mm}$ and $\bar R = 0.05\,\text{mm}$. Construct the $\bar X$ chart control limits. A new subgroup gives measurements (in mm): 45.26, 45.24, 45.27, 45.25, 45.23. Determine whether the process is in statistical control. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Machine Jamming Definition:**
Machine jamming is the abnormal obstruction of a moving element (part, pallet, tool) within an FMS workstation, halting production. It is a leading cause of unplanned downtime.

**Propagation Block Diagram:**

```
W1 -> Buffer B1 -> W2 -> Buffer B2 -> W3 -> Buffer B3 -> W4 (JAMMED)
                                                         |
                                  Back-pressure ----------+
                                       |
                                  B3 overflow
                                       |
                                  W3 stops
                                       |
                                  B2 overflow
                                       |
                                  W2 stops
```

**Anti-Jamming Techniques (any 4):**
1. **Sensor-based detection** (proximity, vision) with PLC auto-stop.
2. **Pneumatic reverse-pulse** to dislodge stuck parts.
3. **Conveyor track redesign** — wider clearance, polished guides.
4. **Predictive maintenance** using vibration/load signatures.
5. **Tool-tip and fixture anti-adhesion coatings.**
6. **Sequential restart logic** to avoid drain shocks.

**Mark Split:** Definition (1), Diagram (2), Propagation logic (1), Anti-jamming (3).

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify SPC constants for $n = 5$:**

$A_2 = 0.577,\; D_3 = 0,\; D_4 = 2.114$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar X$ chart limits:**

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar X} = \bar{\bar X} + A_2 \bar R = 45.20 + (0.577)(0.05) = 45.20 + 0.02885 = 45.22885\,\text{mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar X} = \bar{\bar X} - A_2 \bar R = 45.20 - 0.02885 = 45.17115\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute new subgroup mean and range:**

$$\bar X_{\text{new}} = \frac{45.26 + 45.24 + 45.27 + 45.25 + 45.23}{5} = \frac{226.25}{5} = 45.250\,\text{mm}$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = 45.27 - 45.23 = 0.04\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to limits:**

$$\bar X_{\text{new}} = 45.250 > \text{UCL}_{\bar X} = 45.22885 \;\; \Rightarrow\; \text{OUT OF CONTROL}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R$ chart limits:**

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar R = (2.114)(0.05) = 0.1057\,\text{mm}$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = 0.04 < 0.1057 \;\; \Rightarrow\; \text{IN CONTROL on R chart}$$

**Conclusion:** The $\bar X$ chart detects an **upward shift in the process mean**, indicating a **mean-shift assignable cause** (e.g., tool wear, heat buildup). The variability (R chart) is still stable. Immediate action: investigate tool condition and reduce spindle warm-up drift.

**Mark Split:** Constants (0.5), UCL/LCL of $\bar X$ (2), Subgroup mean & R (1.5), Comparison + decision (2), Recommendation (1).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:** Many students write $C_p$ for an off-center process. Always compute **both** and explain.
> 2. **Using $\Phi(Z)$ values from memory incorrectly:** For $Z = 3.5$, students often quote $\Phi = 0.9998$ (wrong — correct is $\approx 0.999767$). Use the standard normal table **explicitly** in solutions.
> 3. **Forgetting the $1.5\sigma$ long-term shift convention** in Six-Sigma calculations — it must be added to the short-term Z to get the long-term sigma level.
> 4. **Confusing USL/LSL:** USL is the *upper* (larger numerical) limit; LSL is the *lower*. Reversing them flips $C_{pk}$ sign.
> 5. **In $\bar X$–$R$ problems, students often compute $A_2 \bar R$ but forget to add/subtract from $\bar{\bar X}$** — leading to zero limits (impossible).
> 6. **For jamming diagrams, students often draw only "blockage" without showing upstream overflow AND downstream starvation** — both must be present for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **CQC Pillars:** Sensing → Statistical Decision → Feedback Actuation. *No feedback loop = no automation.*
- **$C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ ignores centering; $C_{pk}$ reports the worst side. **Always use $C_{pk}$ for capability assessment.**
- **Benchmark:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ (world-class, semiconductor grade).
- **Six-Sigma:** 3.4 DPMO; uses 1.5σ long-term shift; total $Z = Z_{\text{short}} + 1.5$.
- **DMAIC Order:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control. Memorize the order — frequent 2-mark recall.
- **Control Charts:** $\bar X$ chart monitors location; $R$ chart monitors variability. **Process is "in control" only if BOTH charts pass.**
- **Constants for $n=5$:** $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$ — memorize.
- **Machine Jamming Causes (6M):** Machine, Method, Material, Manpower, Measurement, Environment.
- **Jamming Propagation:** Upstream = buffer overflow; Downstream = starvation. Restart must be **reverse-sequential**.
- **Anti-Jamming Hierarchy:** Detect → Reverse-pulse → Manual intervention → Predictive maintenance.
- **OEE Link:** Availability lost to jamming → Performance lost during restart → Quality lost in warm-up cycle.
- **High-Yield Keywords for 14-mark answers:** *closed-loop feedback, statistical process control, assignable cause, in-control vs capable, six-sigma DMAIC, anti-jamming sensor, sequential restart.*
- **Always write units in final answers** — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing $\text{mm}$, $\text{DPMO}$, $\sigma$, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Assembly Systems: Historical Developments of the Assembly Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automated Assembly Systems: Historical Developments of the Assembly Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automated Assembly System**: An integrated configuration of machines, material handling equipment, sensors, controllers, and computer hardware/software designed to perform the joining of multiple discrete components into a complete subassembly or finished product with minimal or no direct human intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assembly Process**: A manufacturing operation in which two or more separate parts are joined together using mechanical fastening, welding, adhesive bonding, snap-fit, or other joining techniques to form a functional entity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Development of Assembly**: The chronological evolution of assembly methodologies from purely manual (handcraft) operations to present-day cyber-physical, AI-driven, fully autonomous assembly cells, driven by the three pillars of **Productivity**, **Quality**, and **Ergonomics**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **packing a lunchbox** every morning.

- **Manual Assembly**: You personally pick each item (roti, sabzi, spoon) and place it inside, one by one, slowly. (Slow, but flexible.)
- **Mechanized Assembly (Assembly Line)**: A conveyor belt moves the box past stations — Station 1 puts roti, Station 2 pours sabzi, Station 3 adds a spoon. (Fast for big volumes, but rigid.)
- **Flexible Automation**: A **robotic arm** picks whatever you ask — roti OR sandwich OR pasta — based on a recipe code. (Fast *and* flexible.)
- **Smart Assembly (Industry 4.0)**: The robotic arm **sees** which container arrives, **decides** itself what to add, **talks** to your fridge, and **predicts** tomorrow's lunch. (Self-aware, connected, intelligent.)

The journey of assembly automation is essentially this progression: **Human Muscle → Mechanized Power → Programmable Intelligence → Cognitive Autonomy**.

### 1.3 Core Metrics Used in Historical Comparisons

> [!NOTE]
> The three standard benchmarking metrics universally used to evaluate any assembly system across history are:
> - **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** — measured in **seconds/part** (s/pc).
> - **Production Rate ($R_p$)** — measured in **parts/hour** (pc/hr) or **parts/minute** (ppm).
> - **Labor Content ($L_c$)** — measured in **man-hours/unit** (hr/unit).
> 
> Modern automated systems target $T_c \leq 5$ s, $R_p \geq 720$ pc/hr, and $L_c \to 0$ hr/unit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Historical Phases of Assembly Automation

The evolution of the assembly process can be partitioned into **six distinct phases**, each triggered by socioeconomic and technological forces.

#### Phase 1 — Pre-Industrial Manual Assembly (Before 1800)
- Entirely handcrafted; assembly performed by skilled artisans.
- Production rate: typically **< 1 part/hour**.
- **Zero automation**; reliant on human dexterity and craftsmanship.

#### Phase 2 — Mechanized Assembly & the Dawn of Mass Production (1800 – 1910)
- Introduction of **interchangeable parts** concept (Eli Whitney, 1799).
- Steam-powered machinery supplemented manual effort.
- Still predominantly human-driven but with mechanical aids.

#### Phase 3 — The Classical Assembly Line (1913 – 1950)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1913 — Henry Ford's Highland Park Assembly Line** for the Model T is the most pivotal milestone. It reduced chassis assembly time from **12.5 hours** to **93 minutes** — a **~8× improvement**.

- Principles: **Work division**, **station specialization**, **moving conveyor**.
- One operator performs **one task** repeatedly → reduced learning loss, increased speed.

#### Phase 4 — Hard (Fixed) Automation (1950 – 1970)
- Purpose-built mechanisms with **dedicated tooling**.
- Extremely high speed (e.g., **1 part every 1–2 seconds** in automotive).
- **Disadvantage**: Zero flexibility; retooling for a new product is prohibitively expensive.

#### Phase 5 — Flexible / Programmable Automation (1970 – 2000)
- Emergence of **industrial robots** (Unimate, 1961; Cincinnati Milacron T3, 1973; SCARA, 1978 by Hiroshi Makino).
- **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** introduced in 1968 (Modicon 084).
- Vision systems (1970s) enabled inspection and guidance.
- Allowed **batch-of-one** flexibility with near-mass-production speed.

#### Phase 6 — Smart / Cyber-Physical Assembly (2000 – Present)
- **Industry 4.0** (post-2011): IoT, CPS, AI, digital twins, cobots.
- **Collaborative Robots (Cobots)** — Universal Robots UR5 (2008).
- Self-optimizing, predictive, and self-diagnostic assembly cells.
- Cycle times: **1–3 seconds** for high-volume parts; full **lights-out** manufacturing feasible.

### 2.2 The Four Strategic Drivers of Automation

The historical transition between phases was always propelled by:

1. **Productivity** — Lower $T_c$ and higher $R_p$.
2. **Quality** — Defect rates reduced from **~10% (manual)** to **< 50 ppm (automated)**.
3. **Worker Safety & Ergonomics** — Elimination of repetitive strain and hazardous tasks.
4. **Economic Necessity** — Rising labor cost, competitive global markets.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_p = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Production rate from cycle time | pc/hr |
| 2 | $T_c = \dfrac{T_{op}}{N_s} + T_h$ | Cycle time in a balanced line: $T_{op}$ = total operation time, $N_s$ = stations, $T_h$ = handling | s |
| 3 | $\eta_b = \dfrac{\sum T_{op,i}}{N_s \cdot T_c}$ | Line balancing efficiency (0 to 1, ideally = 1) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_u = C_0 + V \cdot N$ | Unit cost: $C_0$ = fixed cost/unit, $V$ = variable cost, $N$ = quantity | ₹/unit |
| 5 | $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{V - C_v}$ | Break-even point for automation investment | units |
| 6 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (Availability $\times$ Performance $\times$ Quality) | 0–1 |
| 7 | $T_{ROI} = \dfrac{I_{\text{auto}} - I_{\text{manual}}}{S_{\text{annual}}}$ | Payback period for automation | years |
| 8 | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{D_{\text{count}}}{N_{\text{produced}}} \times 10^6$ | Parts-per-million defect metric | ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **vertical bar symbol is written as `\vert`** to maintain markdown table integrity. E.g., $\vert T_c \vert$ for absolute cycle time deviation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas govern **every** decision a manufacturing engineer makes when upgrading a manual line to a robotic one. KTU frequently tests the **BEP** and **Payback Period** computations, as they justify the capital expenditure (CAPEX) of automation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Production Rate from Cycle Time

We want to derive the relationship between the number of parts produced per hour and the time required to produce one part.

**Step 1 — Define cycle time.**
Cycle time $T_c$ is the elapsed time between two consecutive finished units leaving the assembly station.

$$
T_c = \text{time per part} \quad [\text{seconds/part}]
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to parts per second.**
If one part takes $T_c$ seconds, then in one second we get $\dfrac{1}{T_c}$ parts.

$$
\text{Rate (parts/sec)} = \frac{1}{T_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to parts per hour.**
There are 3600 seconds in one hour, so multiply:

$$
R_p = 3600 \times \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad [\text{parts/hour}]
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check.**
If $T_c = 6$ s, then $R_p = 3600 / 6 = 600$ parts/hour. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time of a Balanced Assembly Line

**Step 1 — Total workload.**
The sum of the times of all individual operations to be performed on a product:

$$
T_{op,\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} t_i
$$

where $m$ is the total number of operations and $t_i$ is the time of the $i^{\text{th}}$ operation.

**Step 2 — Distribute workload across stations.**
When the line is balanced into $N_s$ stations, the ideal cycle time is:

$$
T_c^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{T_{op,\text{total}}}{N_s}
$$

**Step 3 — Add handling and transfer time.**
Real systems incur material handling time $T_h$:

$$
T_c = \frac{T_{op,\text{total}}}{N_s} + T_h
$$

**Step 4 — Balancing efficiency.**
Effective time utilized versus available time:

$$
\eta_b = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} t_i^{\text{actual}}}{N_s \cdot T_c}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Break-Even Point for Automation

**Step 1 — Cost of automated line.**
$$
C_{\text{auto}}(N) = I_{\text{auto}} + V_a \cdot N
$$
where $I_{\text{auto}}$ = initial investment (CAPEX), $V_a$ = variable cost per unit, $N$ = units produced.

**Step 2 — Cost of manual line.**
$$
C_{\text{manual}}(N) = V_m \cdot N
$$
(manual lines have negligible fixed cost but high variable labor cost).

**Step 3 — Equate the two cost equations.**
$$
I_{\text{auto}} + V_a \cdot N = V_m \cdot N
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$ (Break-Even Point, BEP).**
$$
I_{\text{auto}} = (V_m - V_a) \cdot N
$$

$$
N_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{I_{\text{auto}}}{V_m - V_a}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Producing more than $N_{\text{BEP}}$ units makes automation economically superior.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem**: An assembly operation has 8 tasks with times (s): 12, 15, 8, 20, 10, 18, 14, 13. The line is to be balanced into 3 stations, and $T_h = 5$ s. Find cycle time, production rate, and balancing efficiency if actual station times are [29, 28, 33] s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total operation time.**
$$
T_{op,\text{total}} = 12 + 15 + 8 + 20 + 10 + 18 + 14 + 13 = 110 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Ideal cycle time.**
$$
T_c^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{110}{3} = 36.67 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual cycle time (governing station).**
$$
T_c = \max(29, 28, 33) + 5 = 33 + 5 = 38 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Production rate.**
$$
R_p = \frac{3600}{38} = 94.74 \text{ parts/hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Balancing efficiency.**
$$
\eta_b = \frac{29 + 28 + 33}{3 \times 33} = \frac{90}{99} = 0.909 = 90.9\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slight inefficiency (9.1%) arises because the work could not be divided perfectly among 3 stations — a classic KTU problem outcome.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Timeline — Historical Development of Assembly Automation

```mermaid
timeline
    title Historical Evolution of the Assembly Process
    1799 : Interchangeable Parts Concept (Eli Whitney)
    1913 : Ford's Moving Assembly Line for Model T
    1946 : First Digital Computer (ENIAC)
    1950 : Hard / Fixed Automation Era Begins
    1961 : First Industrial Robot Unimate
    1968 : First PLC Modicon 084
    1973 : Cincinnati Milacron T3 Robot
    1978 : SCARA Robot (Hiroshi Makino)
    1985 : FMS and CIM Concepts Mature
    2008 : Collaborative Robots (Universal Robots UR5)
    2011 : Industry 4.0 Concept (Hannover Fair)
    2020 : AI Driven Vision Based Assembly
    2024 : Autonomous Self Optimizing Assembly Cells
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Automated Assembly System

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Raw Part Input Storage] --> B[Part Feeding and Orienting Unit]
    B --> C[Conveyor Transfer System]
    C --> D[Station 1 Robotized Assembly Cell]
    C --> E[Station 2 Robotized Assembly Cell]
    C --> F[Station 3 Inspection and Testing Cell]
    D --> G[Central PLC Controller]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[SCADA HMI Supervisory Layer]
    H --> I[Manufacturing Execution System MES]
    I --> J[Enterprise Resource Planning ERP]
    G --> K[Sensor Network Vision Force Torque]
    K --> D
    K --> E
    K --> F
    F --> L[Finished Goods Output Buffer]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting Assembly Automation Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Product Design Analysis] --> Q{Demand Volume per Year}
    Q --> R1000[Low Volume]
    Q --> S10000[Medium Volume]
    Q --> T100000[High Volume]
    R1000 --> U1[Use Manual or Standalone Robot Cell]
    S10000 --> U2[Use Flexible Automation with FMS]
    T100000 --> U3[Use Hard Automation Dedicated Line]
    P --> V{Product Variety Index}
    V --> WHigh[High Variety]
    V --> XLow[Low Variety]
    WHigh --> U2
    XLow --> U3
    U1 --> Y[Final Selection]
    U2 --> Y
    U3 --> Y
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture Table

| Era | Dominant System | Cycle Time | Flexibility | Capital Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-1910 | Manual Bench | 600 – 3600 s | Very High | Very Low |
| 1913 – 1950 | Moving Line | 60 – 600 s | Low | Medium |
| 1950 – 1970 | Hard Auto | 1 – 10 s | Very Low | Very High |
| 1970 – 2000 | Robot Cell | 5 – 30 s | High | High |
| 2000 – Present | Smart Cell | 1 – 5 s | Very High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the term "Automated Assembly System" and list any two historical milestones in its evolution.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Automated Assembly System is an integrated configuration of machines, sensors, controllers, and material-handling devices that joins discrete components into a finished product with minimal or zero human intervention. *(2 marks)*
Two historical milestones: *(1 mark)*
1. **1913** — Henry Ford's moving assembly line at Highland Park (reduced Model T assembly time from 12.5 hr to 93 min).
2. **1961** — Deployment of the **Unimate** robot on a General Motors die-casting line, marking the first industrial robot application.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]**
*What is meant by "Hard Automation"? Why was it gradually replaced by flexible automation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hard (Fixed) Automation refers to assembly systems built with dedicated, special-purpose mechanisms for producing a single product at very high speed. *(1.5 marks)*
It was replaced because: *(1.5 marks)*
- It has **zero flexibility** — any product change requires complete retooling.
- **High CAPEX** makes it uneconomical for small/medium batches.
- Modern markets demand **product variety** and **mass customization**, which flexible robotic systems (programmable, vision-guided) handle economically.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain, with suitable examples, the **six major historical phases** in the development of the assembly process. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A product requires **6 assembly operations** with times: 14, 22, 18, 26, 12, 20 seconds. The line is to be balanced across **3 workstations** with material handling time $T_h = 4$ s. Determine the **(i)** ideal cycle time, **(ii)** actual cycle time (assuming actual station loads are [32, 36, 30] s), **(iii)** production rate per hour, and **(iv)** balancing efficiency. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Historical Phases (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-Industrial Manual Assembly (Before 1800):** Pure craftsmanship; artisans built assemblies by hand; productivity < 1 pc/hr; zero automation. *(1 mark)*
2. **Mechanized Assembly (1800–1910):** Introduction of interchangeable parts (Eli Whitney, 1799) and steam-powered machinery; partial mechanization but still largely human-driven. *(1 mark)*
3. **Classical Assembly Line (1913–1950):** Henry Ford's 1913 Highland Park moving line; work division and station specialization; cycle times dropped from hours to minutes; mass production era began. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Hard / Fixed Automation (1950–1970):** Purpose-built dedicated machines; ultra-high speed (1–2 s per part); inflexible; best suited to large volumes of identical products (e.g., automotive sub-assemblies). *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Flexible / Programmable Automation (1970–2000):** Industrial robots (Unimate 1961, SCARA 1978), PLCs (Modicon 1968), vision systems (1970s); enabled batch-of-one production with mass-production speed. *(1 mark)*
6. **Smart / Cyber-Physical Assembly (2000–Present):** Industry 4.0, IoT, digital twins, AI vision, cobots; self-optimizing cells; lights-out manufacturing; OEE > 85%. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total operation time.**
$$
T_{op,\text{total}} = 14 + 22 + 18 + 26 + 12 + 20 = 112 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Ideal cycle time.**
$$
T_c^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{112}{3} = 37.33 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Actual cycle time.**
The bottleneck (slowest) station determines the actual cycle.
$$
T_c = \max(32, 36, 30) + T_h = 36 + 4 = 40 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Production rate.**
$$
R_p = \frac{3600}{40} = 90 \text{ parts/hr}
$$

**Step 5 — (iv) Balancing efficiency.**
$$
\eta_b = \frac{32 + 36 + 30}{3 \times 36} = \frac{98}{108} = 0.907 = 90.74\%
$$

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the evolution of assembly automation, highlighting the **key role of the Unimate robot, PLCs, and SCARA** in transforming modern assembly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company is evaluating automation of a manual assembly line. The manual variable cost is ₹**150** per unit. The automated line requires an initial investment of ₹**25,00,000** with a variable cost of ₹**60** per unit. Calculate the **break-even point** and comment on the decision if the expected annual demand is **40,000 units**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Evolution – Key Technologies (7 marks):**

- **Unimate Robot (1961):** First industrial robot installed on a GM die-casting line at Inland Fisher Guide Plant. It performed repetitive, hazardous tasks (extracting hot die-cast parts) and laid the foundation for **robotic assembly**. *(2 marks)*
- **Programmable Logic Controllers (1968 – Modicon 084):** Replaced hard-wired relay panels; offered flexible, software-based sequence control; drastically simplified retooling of assembly lines. *(2.5 marks)*
- **SCARA Robot (1978 – Hiroshi Makino, Japan):** Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm — optimized for vertical assembly insertions; revolutionized PCB and small-parts assembly with high speed and precision. *(2.5 marks)*

**(b) Break-Even Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the two cost equations.**
$$
C_{\text{manual}}(N) = 150N
$$
$$
C_{\text{auto}}(N) = 25{,}00{,}000 + 60N
$$

**Step 2 — Set equal.**
$$
150N = 25{,}00{,}000 + 60N
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for N.**
$$
150N - 60N = 25{,}00{,}000
$$
$$
90N = 25{,}00{,}000
$$
$$
N_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{90} = 27{,}778 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with expected demand (40,000 units).**
$$
40{,}000 > 27{,}778 \Rightarrow \text{Demand exceeds BEP}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
Since the expected annual demand of **40,000 units** is **above the BEP of 27,778 units**, the automation investment is **economically justified**. The company will save:
$$
\text{Saving} = (150 - 60) \times 40{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = 36{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }11{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting $T_h$** in the cycle time formula — students often compute $T_c$ as just $\max(t_i)$ and miss the handling time. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Using $T_c^{\text{ideal}}$ instead of $T_c^{\text{actual}}$** in the production rate formula — the **bottleneck station time** is what governs $R_p$, not the average.
> 3. **In BEP problems**, students sometimes forget to subtract $V_a$ from $V_m$ in the denominator — this yields a numerically wrong answer.
> 4. **Historical questions** demand **specific years and names** (1913, Ford; 1961, Unimate; 1978, SCARA). Vague answers like "in the past" get **0 marks**.
> 5. Always mention the **three drivers** (Productivity, Quality, Ergonomics) when discussing "why automation" — KTU rewards this triadic framing.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **6 Phases**: Manual → Mechanized → Assembly Line (1913) → Hard Auto → Flexible/Robotic → Smart (Industry 4.0).
> - **Key Dates**: **1799** (interchangeable parts), **1913** (Ford line), **1961** (Unimate), **1968** (PLC), **1978** (SCARA), **2008** (Cobot UR5), **2011** (Industry 4.0).
> - **Core Formulas**: $R_p = 3600/T_c$; $T_c = T_{op,\text{total}}/N_s + T_h$; $\eta_b = \sum t_i / (N_s \cdot T_c)$; BEP $= I_{\text{auto}}/(V_m - V_a)$.
> - **Three Drivers**: Productivity, Quality, Ergonomics.
> - **Hard vs Flexible**: Hard = high speed, low flexibility, high volume; Flexible = moderate speed, high flexibility, mixed volume.
> - **Bottleneck station** dictates the actual $T_c$, not the average.
> - **OEE** is the gold standard for measuring modern assembly line effectiveness; target = **> 85%**.
> - **Industry 4.0** integrates IoT, CPS, AI, Digital Twins — the current frontier.
> - Always include $T_h$ (handling time) in cycle time computations.
> - **Cobots** are the modern face of human-robot collaborative assembly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choice of Assembly Methods: Cost, Production Rate, Labour Availability, and Market Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Choice of Assembly Methods: Cost, Production Rate, Labour Availability, and Market Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choice of Assembly Methods** refers to the systematic engineering evaluation process used to select the most appropriate assembly technique — **Manual Assembly**, **Semi-Automated Assembly**, or **Fully Automated Assembly** — by analyzing four critical decision variables: **Cost**, **Production Rate**, **Labour Availability**, and **Market Life** of the product.

The decision is essentially a **capital investment versus operating expense trade-off** problem, where the engineer must determine the break-even production volume beyond which automation becomes economically justified.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Laundry Problem"** 🧺
>
> Imagine you need to wash clothes. There are three choices:
> 1. **Hand Wash (Manual Assembly)** — Cheap to set up (just a bucket), but slow and tiring if you have 500 shirts.
> 2. **Semi-Automatic Washing Machine (Semi-Automated)** — Moderate cost, faster, but you still load/unload clothes.
> 3. **Fully Automatic Washing Machine + Dryer (Fully Automated)** — Expensive machine, but washes 500 shirts while you sleep.
>
> **Which would you buy?** It depends on:
> - **How many shirts you wash daily** (Production Rate / Volume)
> - **How much the machine costs vs. your time** (Cost)
> - **Whether help is easily available at home** (Labour Availability)
> - **How long you'll keep needing to wash shirts** (Market Life)
>
> Manufacturing engineers face **the exact same decision** for assembly systems!

## 1.3 The Three Assembly Methods — Quick Reference

| Method | Capital Investment | Labour Need | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual Assembly** | Very Low | High (Skilled/Unskilled) | Low volume, high variety |
| **Semi-Automated** | Moderate | Moderate | Medium volume, medium variety |
| **Fully Automated** | Very High | Very Low (Supervisory) | High volume, low variety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The selection of an assembly method is **NOT** a one-factor decision. All four parameters (Cost, Production Rate, Labour, Market Life) must be jointly evaluated. A common mistake is to over-automate low-volume products, leading to **capital lock-up** and poor ROI.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Units to Remember)

- **Production Volume (Q):** units/year
- **Fixed Cost (F):** ₹/year (machine depreciation, tooling, factory overhead)
- **Variable Cost per Unit (V):** ₹/unit (labour, materials, energy)
- **Break-Even Point (BEP):** units/year
- **Return on Investment (ROI):** % per year

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Decision Variables — Exhaustive Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Cost Analysis 💰

The **total cost** of an assembly method is the sum of **Fixed Cost (F)** and **Variable Cost (V × Q)**:

$$C_{total} = F + (V \times Q)$$

where:
- $F$ = Fixed cost (₹/year) — independent of production volume
- $V$ = Variable cost per unit (₹/unit) — directly proportional to volume
- $Q$ = Annual production quantity (units/year)

**Cost Components per Assembly Method:**

| Cost Component | Manual | Semi-Auto | Fully Auto |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tooling & Fixtures | Low | Medium | High |
| Equipment Depreciation | Negligible | Moderate | Very High |
| Direct Labour | Very High | Medium | Low |
| Maintenance | Low | Medium | High |
| Floor Space Required | High | Medium | Compact |

### 2.1.2 Production Rate Analysis ⚙️

**Production Rate (R)** is the number of units produced per unit time:

$$R = \frac{\text{Operating Time Available}}{\text{Cycle Time per Unit}}$$

For a given demand, if required production rate > manual capacity → **Automation is forced**.

**Cycle Time Comparison (Indicative — Assembly of 10 parts):**

| Method | Typical Cycle Time |
|---|---|
| Manual (single worker) | 120 – 300 sec |
| Semi-Automatic (powered tools + manual placement) | 30 – 90 sec |
| Fully Automated (robotic line) | 5 – 30 sec |

### 2.1.3 Labour Availability 👷

Considered through three sub-factors:
1. **Wage Rate (W):** ₹/hour of the local labour market
2. **Skill Level Required:** Unskilled < Semi-skilled < Skilled
3. **Availability Index:** Urban areas have higher availability; rural/specialized zones face scarcity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Exam Favorite):** If skilled labour is **cheap AND abundant**, manual assembly is preferred. If labour is **scarce AND expensive**, automation becomes attractive even at moderate volumes.

### 2.1.4 Market Life of the Product 📅

Market Life = Duration the product will remain commercially viable before obsolescence.

- **Short Market Life (< 2 years):** → Manual/Semi-Auto (avoid heavy capital investment)
- **Long Market Life (> 5 years):** → Fully Automated systems justified (amortize cost over many years)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{total} = F + V \cdot Q$ | Total annual cost of an assembly method | ₹/year |
| 2 | $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$ | Break-even production quantity between two methods | units/year |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{T_{available}}{t_c}$ | Production rate | units/hour |
| 4 | $\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{\text{Extra Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Time to recover automation cost | years |
| 5 | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Return on investment | % per year |
| 6 | $V_{manual} = L + M$ | Variable cost (manual) = Labour + Material | ₹/unit |
| 7 | $V_{auto} = E + W_p + M$ | Variable cost (auto) = Energy + Maintenance + Material | ₹/unit |
| 8 | $\text{Amortization} = \dfrac{F_{machine}}{L_{market}}$ | Cost recovery per year of market life | ₹/year |

> *Note: In the formulas above, $F_1$ and $V_1$ refer to Method 1 (e.g., Manual) and $F_2, V_2$ to Method 2 (e.g., Automated). $L$ = labour cost, $M$ = material cost, $E$ = energy, $W_p$ = wear-parts, $L_{market}$ = market life in years.*

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This is Applied

- **Automotive industry:** Selecting between robotic and manual welding stations
- **Electronics manufacturing:** Choosing pick-and-place machines vs. hand soldering
- **Consumer goods:** Bottling plants, packaging lines
- **Aerospace:** High-precision assembly with strict tolerance control

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Point (BEP) Formula

The **Break-Even Point** is the production volume at which the total cost of two alternative assembly methods becomes **equal**. Beyond this point, one method becomes more economical than the other.

### 3.1.1 Mathematical Setup

Let us consider two assembly methods, **Method 1 (Manual)** and **Method 2 (Automated)**, with:
- $F_1, V_1$ → Fixed and Variable costs for Method 1
- $F_2, V_2$ → Fixed and Variable costs for Method 2

The total cost of each method is:

$$C_1(Q) = F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q$$

$$C_2(Q) = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Equate the two cost equations at the break-even point:**

$$C_1(Q_{BEP}) = C_2(Q_{BEP})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expressions:**

$$F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q_{BEP} = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q_{BEP}$$

**Step 3 — Bring the $Q_{BEP}$ terms to one side:**

$$F_1 - F_2 = V_2 \cdot Q_{BEP} - V_1 \cdot Q_{BEP}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $Q_{BEP}$:**

$$F_1 - F_2 = Q_{BEP} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Q_{BEP}$:**

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If the required annual production $Q > Q_{BEP}$, choose **Method 2** (typically automated). If $Q < Q_{BEP}$, choose **Method 1** (typically manual).

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU 2024 Numerical Style

> **Problem (Model):** A company is evaluating manual vs. automated assembly for a new product.
> - **Manual Method:** $F_1$ = ₹50,000/year, $V_1$ = ₹80/unit
> - **Automated Method:** $F_2$ = ₹8,00,000/year, $V_2$ = ₹20/unit
> - **Annual demand:** 15,000 units
>
> Determine: (a) Break-even quantity, (b) Most economical method, (c) Annual savings at the chosen method.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**

$$F_1 = 50{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}, \quad V_1 = 80 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$F_2 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}, \quad V_2 = 20 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$Q = 15{,}000 \text{ units/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Break-Even Quantity using the derived formula:**

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$$

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{50{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{20 - 80}$$

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{-7{,}50{,}000}{-60}$$

$$Q_{BEP} = 12{,}500 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compare $Q$ with $Q_{BEP}$:**

$$Q = 15{,}000 > Q_{BEP} = 12{,}500$$

Since actual demand **exceeds** break-even, choose **Automated Assembly**.

**Step 4 — Compute total cost for both methods to verify:**

$$C_1 = F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q = 50{,}000 + (80 \times 15{,}000)$$

$$C_1 = 50{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

$$C_2 = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q = 8{,}00{,}000 + (20 \times 15{,}000)$$

$$C_2 = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Compute annual savings by choosing automation:**

$$\text{Savings} = C_1 - C_2 = 12{,}50{,}000 - 11{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Savings} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 6 — Compute payback period on extra investment:**

$$\text{Extra Investment} = F_2 - F_1 = 8{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{7{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = 5 \text{ years}$$

If the **market life > 5 years**, automation is justified. ✅

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — Quick Selection Guide

| Condition | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Low volume + High variety + Cheap labour + Short market life | **Manual** | Low setup cost, flexible |
| Medium volume + Moderate variety + Moderate labour cost | **Semi-Automated** | Balanced cost & output |
| High volume + Low variety + Expensive/scarce labour + Long market life | **Fully Automated** | High throughput, low per-unit cost |
| Demand uncertain / Product evolving | **Manual / Semi-Auto** | Avoid capital lock-up |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Assembly Method Selection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product Design Ready] --> B[Estimate Annual Demand Q]
    B --> C[Assess Market Life ML in years]
    C --> D[Survey Local Labour: Wage, Skill, Availability]
    D --> E[Compute Fixed Cost F and Variable Cost V for each method]
    E --> F[Calculate Break-Even Point Q sub BEP]
    F --> G{Compare Q with Q sub BEP}
    G -- Q less than Q sub BEP --> H[Choose Manual Assembly]
    G -- Q greater than Q sub BEP --> I{Is Market Life greater than Payback Period}
    I -- No --> J[Choose Semi-Automated Assembly]
    I -- Yes --> K[Choose Fully Automated Assembly]
    H --> L[End]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from top to bottom. The decision diamonds determine the final choice. Each path represents a real engineering trade-off.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cost Components of Each Assembly Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MANUAL["Manual Assembly"]
        M1[Tooling Cost] --> M2[Total Manual Cost]
        M3[Direct Labour Cost] --> M2
        M4[Material Cost] --> M2
    end
    subgraph SEMI["Semi-Automated Assembly"]
        S1[Power Tool Cost] --> S2[Total Semi-Auto Cost]
        S3[Semi-Skilled Labour] --> S2
        S4[Material Cost] --> S2
        S5[Moderate Energy] --> S2
    end
    subgraph AUTO["Fully Automated Assembly"]
        A1[Robotic Equipment Cost] --> A2[Total Automated Cost]
        A3[Maintenance Cost] --> A2
        A4[Energy Cost] --> A2
        A5[Material Cost] --> A2
        A6[Supervisory Labour] --> A2
    end
```

## 4.3 Graphical Concept — Break-Even Chart (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Cost Axis Y in Rupees]:::axis
    P2[Volume Axis X in Units per year]:::axis
    P3[Manual Total Cost Line: Slope V1 intercept F1]:::lineM
    P4[Automated Total Cost Line: Slope V2 intercept F2]:::lineA
    P5[Intersection Point equals Break Even Point]:::point
    P1 --- P2
    P3 --- P5
    P4 --- P5
    classDef axis fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lineM fill:#cce,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lineA fill:#eef,stroke:#909,stroke-width:2px
    classDef point fill:#fc6,stroke:#f60,stroke-width:3px
```

> **Interpretation:** The manual line starts lower (small $F_1$) but rises steeply (large $V_1$). The automated line starts higher (large $F_2$) but is flatter (small $V_2$). They cross at $Q_{BEP}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024]`
**Explain the role of "Market Life" in selecting an assembly method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Market life is the expected duration a product will remain commercially viable. If the market life is **short** (e.g., < 2 years), high capital investment in automation is risky because the system may not be fully amortized before the product becomes obsolete. If the market life is **long** (e.g., > 5 years), the high fixed cost of automation can be spread over many production years, making **fully automated assembly** economically attractive. (3 marks)

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024]`
**Define Break-Even Point in the context of assembly method selection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the annual production quantity at which the total cost of two competing assembly methods (e.g., manual and automated) becomes equal. It is given by:

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$$

If the required production volume $Q > Q_{BEP}$, the method with higher fixed cost (typically automated) is preferred; otherwise, the method with lower fixed cost (typically manual) is preferred. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024]`

**a)** Discuss in detail the **four key factors** that govern the choice of an assembly method in modern manufacturing. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A company can produce a component using either a **manual assembly** or a **semi-automated assembly** system. For manual: $F_1$ = ₹1,00,000/yr, $V_1$ = ₹50/unit. For semi-automated: $F_2$ = ₹5,00,000/yr, $V_2$ = ₹30/unit. The annual demand is **20,000 units** and the expected market life of the product is **6 years**. Determine the most economical method and comment on the payback period. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The four key factors governing the choice of an assembly method are:

**1. Cost (3 marks for explanation):**
The total annual cost is $C = F + V \cdot Q$. Manual assembly has low fixed cost but high variable cost (labour). Automated assembly has high fixed cost (machine + tooling) but low variable cost. The choice depends on the break-even analysis between the two.

**2. Production Rate (2 marks):**
Manual systems have limited output per worker. If the required production rate exceeds what manual labour can deliver in the available time, automation becomes mandatory. Cycle time for automated systems is typically 5–10× faster than manual.

**3. Labour Availability (1 mark):**
In regions where skilled labour is **expensive or scarce**, automation reduces dependency on human resources. In regions with **abundant, cheap labour**, manual assembly remains competitive.

**4. Market Life (1 mark):**
If the product's market life is short, investing in expensive automation is risky. Long market life justifies automation by allowing recovery of capital cost.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$$F_1 = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}, \quad V_1 = 50 \text{ ₹/unit}$$
$$F_2 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}, \quad V_2 = 30 \text{ ₹/unit}$$
$$Q = 20{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad ML = 6 \text{ years}$$

**Step 1 — Break-Even Point:**

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{30 - 50}$$

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{-4{,}00{,}000}{-20} = 20{,}000 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 2 — Compare $Q$ with $Q_{BEP}$:**

$$Q = 20{,}000 = Q_{BEP} = 20{,}000$$

Since $Q$ is **equal** to $Q_{BEP}$, both methods are equally economical at this exact volume. However, the company should plan for a slight increase in demand and choose **semi-automated assembly** to leverage the lower variable cost.

**Step 3 — Verify by computing total costs:**

$$C_1 = 1{,}00{,}000 + (50 \times 20{,}000) = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

$$C_2 = 5{,}00{,}000 + (30 \times 20{,}000) = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

Both costs are equal at ₹11,00,000/yr. ✅

**Step 4 — Payback period analysis:**

If demand rises even slightly above 20,000 units, semi-automated method yields savings. Extra investment = ₹4,00,000. With market life of 6 years, the company can comfortably amortize the cost. **Recommendation: Semi-Automated Assembly.** (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**a)** Compare **Manual, Semi-Automated, and Fully Automated** assembly systems on the basis of cost, flexibility, production rate, and labour requirement. Use a suitable table. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A manufacturer is choosing between a **manual workstation** and a **fully automated robotic cell** for assembling an electronic gadget.
- Manual: $F_1$ = ₹2,00,000/yr, $V_1$ = ₹100/unit
- Automated: $F_2$ = ₹25,00,000/yr, $V_2$ = ₹15/unit
- Annual demand = 40,000 units
- Expected market life = 8 years
Determine: (i) Break-even quantity, (ii) Which method is economical, (iii) Payback period, (iv) Is automation justified? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Manual Assembly | Semi-Automated | Fully Automated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Investment** | Very Low (₹) | Moderate (₹₹) | Very High (₹₹₹₹) |
| **Variable Cost / Unit** | Very High (labour-intensive) | Moderate | Very Low |
| **Production Rate** | Low (20–50 pcs/hr) | Medium (100–300 pcs/hr) | High (500–2000 pcs/hr) |
| **Flexibility** | Very High (easy to changeover) | Moderate | Low (dedicated tooling) |
| **Labour Requirement** | High (many workers) | Moderate | Very Low (supervisors only) |
| **Skill Level Needed** | Low to Medium | Medium | High (engineers/programmers) |
| **Best for Volume** | Low (< 10,000/yr) | Medium (10k–1L/yr) | High (> 1,00,000/yr) |
| **Product Variety** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Market Life Suitability** | Short life | Medium life | Long life |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$$F_1 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}, \quad V_1 = 100 \text{ ₹/unit}$$
$$F_2 = 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}, \quad V_2 = 15 \text{ ₹/unit}$$
$$Q = 40{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad ML = 8 \text{ years}$$

**(i) Break-Even Point:**

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000}{15 - 100}$$

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{-23{,}00{,}000}{-85} \approx 27{,}059 \text{ units/year}$$

**(ii) Economical Method:**

Since $Q = 40{,}000 > Q_{BEP} = 27{,}059$, the **fully automated robotic cell** is more economical.

**Verification:**

$$C_1 = 2{,}00{,}000 + (100 \times 40{,}000) = 42{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

$$C_2 = 25{,}00{,}000 + (15 \times 40{,}000) = 31{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

**Savings = ₹11,00,000/yr** by choosing automation. ✅

**(iii) Payback Period:**

$$\text{Extra Investment} = 25{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 23{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{23{,}00{,}000}{11{,}00{,}000} \approx 2.09 \text{ years}$$

**(iv) Is Automation Justified?**

$$\text{Market Life} = 8 \text{ years} \quad > \quad \text{Payback Period} = 2.09 \text{ years}$$

**Yes, fully automated assembly is strongly justified.** The investment is recovered in just over 2 years, leaving 6 more years of pure savings. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in BEP Formula:** Students often write $Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_2 - F_1}{V_1 - V_2}$ (swapped numerator and denominator) — this gives a negative or incorrect value. Always keep $F$ and $V$ of the *same method pair* consistent.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** $F$ must be in ₹/year and $V$ in ₹/unit for the formula to yield units/year.
> 3. **Ignoring Market Life:** Students often declare automation "best" without comparing payback period with market life. If payback > market life, the decision is **wrong** even if BEP favours automation.
> 4. **Not Verifying by Total Cost:** Always plug $Q$ back into both cost equations as a cross-check — examiners award method marks for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — READ BEFORE EXAM**

- ⭐ **Three Assembly Methods:** Manual (low $F$, high $V$), Semi-Auto (balanced), Fully Auto (high $F$, low $V$).
- ⭐ **Master Formula:** $Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$ — the cornerstone of every KTU numerical on this topic.
- ⭐ **Decision Rule:** If $Q > Q_{BEP}$ → choose higher fixed-cost method (Automation). If $Q < Q_{BEP}$ → choose lower fixed-cost method (Manual).
- ⭐ **Total Cost Equation:** $C = F + V \cdot Q$ — always use this for verification.
- ⭐ **Payback Period** must be **less than Market Life** for automation to be justified.
- ⭐ **Four Decision Factors (in priority order for KTU answers):**
  1. **Cost** → Break-even analysis
  2. **Production Rate** → Cycle time & throughput
  3. **Labour Availability** → Wage rate, skill, scarcity
  4. **Market Life** → Product longevity vs. investment recovery
- ⭐ **Variable Cost in Manual** = Labour + Material; **Variable Cost in Auto** = Energy + Wear-parts + Material.
- ⭐ **ROI Formula:** $\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ — useful for ranking alternative investments.
- ⭐ **Flexibility Rule:** Manual > Semi-Auto > Fully Auto (in terms of product changeover ability).
- ⭐ **Volume Rule:** Low → Manual; Medium → Semi-Auto; High → Fully Auto.
- ⭐ **Never** recommend full automation for short-lived products — capital recovery is impossible.
- ⭐ **Always cross-verify** by computing total cost of both methods at the actual demand — examiners love this step.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Automatic Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Automatic Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automatic Assembly** is a manufacturing process in which discrete components are progressively joined together by automated workstations, programmable mechanisms, and feedback-controlled machinery to produce a finished product with minimal or no direct human intervention.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT403 – Manufacturing Automation)**, automatic assembly is positioned as a subset of *Flexible / Hard Automation* in which the *assembly function* (joining, fastening, inserting, welding, riveting, etc.) is mechanized to deliver **high-volume, repeatable, and consistent** product output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Syllabus Highlight):**
> - *Manual Assembly* → Human operator performs the joining.
> - *Semi-Automatic Assembly* → Operator loads/unloads; machine performs the joining.
> - *Fully Automatic Assembly* → Material handling, joining, inspection, and ejection are all machine-controlled.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Marble Track" Intuition

Imagine building a LEGO castle:

- **Manual Assembly** = You, by hand, picking up each brick, aligning it, and pressing it into place. Slow, but flexible.
- **Automatic Assembly** = A pre-built conveyor "brick highway" where robotic arms grab the exact brick, place it in the exact position, and press it with a precise force — every 4 seconds, 24×7, without ever getting tired.

The robotic arm does not "think" — it executes a *programmed sequence* (analogous to a music box playing a tune). Because the instructions never change (high repeatability) and the machine never fatigues (consistent stamina), the output is **uniform, fast, and cost-effective at scale**.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Used to Justify Automation

The following **bolded metrics** are used globally (and accepted by KTU valuation) to argue *why* automatic assembly is preferred:

- **Cycle Time (t<sub>c</sub>)** – seconds per assembly
- **Uptime (η)** – % of available time the line is productive
- **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** – % assemblies passed without rework
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** – Availability × Performance × Quality
- **Cost-per-Assembly (CPA)** – total landed cost ÷ units produced

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any "advantage of automatic assembly" in a KTU answer must ultimately be traced back to an **improvement in one or more of the metrics above**. Memorize this link — examiners reward it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition: The "Why" Behind Each Advantage

Automatic assembly does not deliver value on its own. It delivers value because the **engineering rationale** behind it solves a real, measurable production pain-point. The advantages are best grouped into **five functional categories** that map directly to the KTU Module-2 cognitive outcomes.

### A. Productivity & Throughput Advantages

1. **Higher Production Rate** – Machines operate at fixed, optimized cycle times (often **2–10 seconds** per assembly), unaffected by fatigue.
2. **Continuous Operation** – 24/7/365 capability (subject to scheduled maintenance) vs. ~8 hours/day for a manual operator.
3. **Reduced Cycle Time** – Parallel processing on multi-station transfer lines shortens total assembly time.
4. **Higher Machine Utilization** – Automated material handling keeps workstations loaded, minimizing *starvation* and *blocking*.

### B. Quality & Consistency Advantages

1. **Repeatability** – Position accuracy of ±0.01 mm to ±0.05 mm in modern robotic assemblers.
2. **Reduced Human Error** – Eliminates variability from fatigue, distraction, or skill differences.
3. **Built-in Inspection** – Vision systems, torque sensors, and in-line gauges verify every assembly.
4. **Lower Rejection Rate** – Tighter tolerances → fewer defects → lower scrap cost.
5. **Improved First-Pass Yield (FPY)** – Direct contribution to **Six-Sigma**-style quality targets.

### C. Cost & Economic Advantages

1. **Lower Long-Term Labor Cost** – High capital cost, low variable cost → favorable beyond the **break-even volume**.
2. **Reduced Material Waste** – Precise dispensing of adhesives, fasteners, and lubricants.
3. **Lower Rework & Warranty Cost** – Higher initial quality reduces downstream expenses.
4. **Better Inventory Control** – Synchronized flow reduces Work-In-Progress (WIP) holding cost.
5. **Economies of Scale** – Per-unit cost drops sharply as production volume rises.

### D. Safety & Ergonomic Advantages

1. **Removal of Humans from Hazardous Zones** – Welding fumes, heat, pinch points, heavy lifting.
2. **Ergonomic Relief** – Eliminates Repetitive Strain Injuries (RSI).
3. **Compliance with Safety Norms** – Easier alignment with OSHA / Factory Act regulations.
4. **Lower Accident-Related Downtime & Insurance Cost**.

### E. Flexibility & Information Advantages (Modern / Flexible Systems)

1. **Programmability** – Changeover via software, not mechanical retooling (in flexible systems).
2. **Data Acquisition** – Every cycle is logged → real-time dashboards, predictive maintenance.
3. **Integration with Industry 4.0** – IIoT, MES, and ERP connectivity for smart factories.
4. **Scalability** – Additional stations or parallel lines can be added modularly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a 14-mark KTU question, the examiner expects you to **group the advantages under these five headings** and provide at least **2 sub-points per heading** with real engineering context. Random bullet lists are penalized.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cycle Time | $t_c$ | $t_c = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | s/unit | Throughput planning |
| 2 | Production Rate | $R_p$ | $R_p = \dfrac{1}{t_c}$ | units/s | Capacity sizing |
| 3 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $OEE$ | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % (decimal) | Equipment benchmarking |
| 4 | Availability | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | % | Downtime tracking |
| 5 | Performance | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Units}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | % | Speed loss tracking |
| 6 | Quality | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | % | Yield tracking |
| 7 | First-Pass Yield | $FPY$ | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units Passed First Time}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}}$ | % | Quality KPI |
| 8 | Cost per Assembly | $CPA$ | $CPA = \dfrac{C_{capital} + C_{labor} + C_{material} + C_{overhead}}{N_{units}}$ | ₹/unit | Cost justification |
| 9 | Break-Even Volume | $N_{BE}$ | $N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{auto} - C_{manual}}{C_{manual/unit} - C_{auto/unit}}$ | units | Automation decision |
| 10 | Return on Investment | $ROI$ | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Savings}}{C_{capital}} \times 100$ | % | Investment approval |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** All absolute values in equations are written with `\vert` / `\mid` to keep markdown tables intact. Example: $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as $\vert x \vert$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sector** – Engine block assembly, dashboard assembly (typical t<sub>c</sub> ≈ 60–90 s).
- **Electronics** – PCB component placement (t<sub>c</sub> ≈ 0.05 s per pick-and-place).
- **Medical Devices** – Syringe assembly under Class 8 cleanrooms.
- **Consumer Goods** – Pen assembly, lipstick assembly, aerosol valve crimping.
- **Aerospace** – Riveting of fuselage panels with positional accuracy ≤ 0.1 mm.

The universality of these metrics explains why **automatic assembly is a cross-industry KTU-relevant technology**, not confined to a single domain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Break-Even Volume Justification

The single most common numerical question in a KTU Module-2 exam is: *"At what production volume does automatic assembly become cheaper than manual assembly?"*

**Given Data (typical textbook assumption):**
- Manual cost per assembly: $C_m$ = ₹ 10
- Automatic cost per assembly (variable part only): $C_a$ = ₹ 4
- Capital cost of automatic system: $K$ = ₹ 12,00,000

**Step 1:** Define total cost for each method.

$$
C_{manual,total} = C_m \cdot N
$$

$$
C_{auto,total} = K + C_a \cdot N
$$

Where $N$ = number of units produced.

**Step 2:** Set them equal to find the break-even point.

$$
K + C_a \cdot N = C_m \cdot N
$$

**Step 3:** Isolate the capital cost term.

$$
K = C_m \cdot N - C_a \cdot N
$$

$$
K = (C_m - C_a) \cdot N
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N_{BE}$.

$$
N_{BE} = \dfrac{K}{C_m - C_a}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
N_{BE} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{10 - 4} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{6} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation.

> If the annual demand is greater than 2,00,000 units, **automatic assembly is economically superior**. Below this, manual assembly is cheaper. This single equation is the *quantitative backbone* of the "Lower Long-Term Cost" advantage.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — OEE Improvement as a "Quality Advantage" Proof

**Step 1:** Take a baseline manual line OEE.

$$
OEE_{manual} = A_m \times P_m \times Q_m = 0.85 \times 0.80 \times 0.95 = 0.646 \;(64.6\%)
$$

**Step 2:** Take an automated equivalent (machines have less downtime, no fatigue).

$$
OEE_{auto} = A_a \times P_a \times Q_a = 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 = 0.893 \;(89.3\%)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the relative improvement.

$$
\Delta OEE = OEE_{auto} - OEE_{manual} = 0.893 - 0.646 = 0.247
$$

**Step 4:** Translate to effective capacity.

If the plant runs **250 days × 16 hours = 4000 hours/year**, then effective production hours become:

$$
t_{effective,manual} = 4000 \times 0.646 = 2584 \text{ hr}
$$

$$
t_{effective,auto} = 4000 \times 0.893 = 3572 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 5:** Effective capacity gain.

$$
\text{Gain} = \dfrac{3572 - 2584}{2584} \times 100 \approx 38.2\%
$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion.

> Automatic assembly delivers an effective capacity gain of **~38%** purely through superior OEE — this is the *mathematical proof* of the "Higher Productivity" and "Better Quality" advantages.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — "Advantage ↔ Pain-Point Solved ↔ KPI Improved"

| # | Stated Advantage | Production Pain-Point Solved | KPI Improved | Trade-off / Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Higher Throughput | Slow manual cycle | $R_p$ ↑ | High capital outlay |
| 2 | 24/7 Operation | Single-shift limit | $A$ ↑ | Maintenance cost |
| 3 | Repeatability | Operator variability | $Q$ ↑, FPY ↑ | Inflexibility (hard auto.) |
| 4 | Lower Long-Term Cost | Rising wage bills | $CPA$ ↓ | Break-even risk |
| 5 | Less Material Waste | Over-dispensing, spillage | $C_{material}$ ↓ | Sensor calibration |
| 6 | Worker Safety | Injuries, lawsuits | Insurance ↓ | Initial safety audit cost |
| 7 | Data & Traceability | Paper-based records | IIoT integration | Cybersecurity risk |
| 8 | Ergonomic Relief | RSI, fatigue | Labor retention | Reskilling cost |
| 9 | Modular Scalability | Demand fluctuation | Capacity elasticity | Floor-space needs |
| 10 | Lower Rework Cost | Field failures | Warranty cost ↓ | Capital depreciation |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners **love** such matrices. Drawing this table in your exam script for a 14-mark question immediately signals senior-level understanding and is worth 2–3 bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six advantages of automatic assembly systems over manual assembly.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Higher and consistent production rate** — fixed cycle time independent of operator fatigue.
2. **Improved quality and repeatability** — positional accuracy of the order of ±0.05 mm.
3. **Lower long-term cost per assembly** — once the break-even volume is crossed, variable cost dominates.
4. **Reduced material waste** — precise dispensing and torque control.
5. **Enhanced worker safety** — humans removed from hazardous, hot, or noisy zones.
6. **Data traceability and IIoT compatibility** — every cycle logged for Industry 4.0 dashboards.

*(Half-mark per correct point; any six required.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term *First-Pass Yield (FPY)* and explain its relevance as an advantage of automatic assembly.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** First-Pass Yield (FPY) is the ratio of units that pass inspection at the first attempt, without requiring any rework, to the total units inspected in a given period.

**Mathematically:**

$$
FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units Passed First Time}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} \times 100\%
$$

**Relevance:**
- Automatic assembly integrates **in-line sensors and vision systems** that catch defects *during* the cycle, not after.
- This drives FPY to values **> 99%**, far above typical manual lines (≈ 85–92%).
- High FPY directly reduces **rework cost, warranty claims, and customer returns** — a key economic advantage of automation.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark relevance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> *Discuss in detail the major advantages of automatic assembly. Group your answer under the headings: (a) Productivity and Throughput, (b) Quality and Consistency, (c) Cost Economics, (d) Safety and Ergonomics, (e) Flexibility and Information.*

#### Part (a) — Productivity and Throughput (7 marks)

Automatic assembly systems achieve superior productivity because machine actions are governed by **closed-loop servo control** rather than human reaction times.

- **Higher production rate** — modern pick-and-place and rotary indexing machines operate at cycle times of **2–10 seconds**, an order of magnitude faster than manual assembly (60–180 s).
- **Continuous operation** — machines can run multiple shifts (16–24 hours/day) with only scheduled downtime, while a human operator is restricted to ~8 hours/day with fatigue breaks.
- **Reduced cycle time via parallelization** — multi-station transfer lines perform *n* sub-assemblies concurrently, compressing the total assembly time.
- **Higher machine utilization** — automated parts feeders, bowl feeders, and conveyors eliminate idle gaps; this raises **Availability (A)** in the OEE formula.

**Quantitative summary:**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q
$$

A typical auto-line achieves OEE ≈ **85–90%**, while a manual line struggles to exceed **60–65%**.

#### Part (b) — Quality, Cost, Safety, and Flexibility (7 marks)

**Quality & Consistency:**
- Positional repeatability of **±0.01–0.05 mm** ensures every part is identical.
- **Vision systems, force-torque sensors, and laser gauges** provide 100% in-line inspection → rejection rates drop from ~5% to <0.5%.
- This directly improves **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** and customer satisfaction.

**Cost Economics:**
- **Break-even volume** governs the decision. Using the formula:

$$
N_{BE} = \dfrac{K}{C_m - C_a}
$$

where $K$ is capital, $C_m$ and $C_a$ are unit costs. For $K$ = ₹12,00,000, $C_m$ = ₹10, $C_a$ = ₹4, the break-even is **2,00,000 units**. Beyond this, automatic assembly is unambiguously cheaper per unit.

**Safety & Ergonomics:**
- Human operators are removed from **welding fumes, heat, pinch points, and heavy lifts**, drastically reducing occupational injuries and the company's liability/insurance premium.

**Flexibility & Information:**
- Modern **flexible assembly systems** allow rapid changeover via software recipes, not mechanical retooling.
- Every cycle is logged → enables **predictive maintenance, OEE dashboards, and Industry 4.0 integration (IIoT/MES/ERP)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT list random advantages** without grouping. The examiner explicitly rewards structured headings.
> - **Do NOT skip numerical justification.** A 14-mark answer without at least one formula (cycle time, break-even, or OEE) loses ~2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT confuse flexibility** of *hard automation* (which is *inflexible*) with *flexible automation* (which is reprogrammable). Be precise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *(a) Explain the concept of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). Derive how automatic assembly improves OEE compared to manual assembly, using a numerical example. (7 marks)*
> *(b) A manufacturer is evaluating a capital investment of ₹ 20,00,000 for an automatic assembly cell. The manual cost per unit is ₹ 15 and the automatic variable cost per unit is ₹ 5. Determine the break-even production volume. Comment on the decision if the annual demand is (i) 1,00,000 units and (ii) 5,00,000 units. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
OEE is the **gold-standard metric** for measuring manufacturing productivity. It is the product of three ratios:

$$
OEE = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

**Step 2 — Components:**

- $A = \dfrac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ — measures *downtime losses*.
- $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Units}}{\text{Run Time}}$ — measures *speed losses*.
- $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ — measures *quality losses*.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**

Assume the plant runs **Planned Production Time = 480 min/day**.

| Parameter | Manual Line | Automatic Line |
|---|---|---|
| Run Time (min) | 380 | 460 |
| Ideal Cycle Time (s) | 60 | 30 |
| Total Units Produced | 380 | 920 |
| Good Units | 350 | 910 |

**Step 4 — Compute for Manual:**

$$
A_m = \dfrac{380}{480} = 0.792
$$

$$
P_m = \dfrac{60 \times 380}{380 \times 60} = 1.000
$$

$$
Q_m = \dfrac{350}{380} = 0.921
$$

$$
OEE_m = 0.792 \times 1.000 \times 0.921 = 0.729 \;(72.9\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute for Automatic:**

$$
A_a = \dfrac{460}{480} = 0.958
$$

$$
P_a = \dfrac{30 \times 920}{460 \times 60} = \dfrac{27600}{27600} = 1.000
$$

$$
Q_a = \dfrac{910}{920} = 0.989
$$

$$
OEE_a = 0.958 \times 1.000 \times 0.989 = 0.947 \;(94.7\%)
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

> Automatic assembly improves OEE by **~22 percentage points** (72.9% → 94.7%), translating to a **~30% effective capacity gain** — this is the mathematical evidence of its productivity advantage.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark each for A, P, Q formulas + 2 marks computation + 1 mark conclusion.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $K = ₹20,00,000$
- $C_m = ₹15$ per unit
- $C_a = ₹5$ per unit

**Step 1 — Apply the break-even formula:**

$$
N_{BE} = \dfrac{K}{C_m - C_a} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{15 - 5} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{10} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify by computing total costs at N = 2,00,000:**

$$
C_{manual,total} = 15 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹30,00,000
$$

$$
C_{auto,total} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹30,00{,}000
$$

(The two are equal, confirming the break-even point.)

**Step 3 — Decision Case (i): N = 1,00,000 units**

$$
C_{manual} = 15 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹15,00,000
$$

$$
C_{auto} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹25,00{,}000
$$

Manual is **₹10,00,000 cheaper** → **Do NOT automate.** Stay with manual assembly.

**Step 4 — Decision Case (ii): N = 5,00,000 units**

$$
C_{manual} = 15 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹75,00,000
$$

$$
C_{auto} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹45,00{,}000
$$

Automatic is **₹30,00,000 cheaper** → **Automate immediately.**

**Step 5 — Engineering Insight:**

> The break-even volume is **2,00,000 units/period**. Below it, manual assembly is more economical. Above it, automatic assembly yields substantial savings. The "Lower Long-Term Cost" advantage of automation is therefore **volume-conditional**, not absolute.

*(2 marks formula + 1 mark break-even calculation + 2 marks for each sub-case + 1 mark insight.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Break-Even Problems:**
> - Forgetting to **add the capital cost** $K$ to the automatic total cost.
> - Comparing only the *unit* cost instead of the *total* cost — a frequent 1-mark loss.
> - Not stating a **clear decision** ("automate" or "do not automate") for both sub-cases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Advantages of Automatic Assembly**

- **Five Universal Categories:** Productivity, Quality, Cost, Safety, Flexibility/Information. Use these as answer headings.
- **Top-3 Numerical Anchors:** (1) Break-even volume $N_{BE} = K / (C_m - C_a)$; (2) OEE $= A \times P \times Q$; (3) Production rate $R_p = 1/t_c$.
- **Quantitative Wins to Memorize:** Repeatability ±0.05 mm; OEE 85–90% (auto) vs 60–65% (manual); FPY > 99% (auto) vs 85–92% (manual).
- **The "Volume Conditional" Caveat:** Automation is cheaper **only above the break-even volume** — always state this in cost questions.
- **Distinguish Hard vs Flexible Automation:** Hard = high throughput, low flexibility; Flexible = reprogrammable, moderate throughput.
- **Industry 4.0 Hook:** Mention IIoT, MES/ERP integration, predictive maintenance — examiners reward modern context.
- **Avoid Vague Statements:** Replace *"faster"* with *"cycle time reduced from 60 s to 8 s"*; replace *"cheaper"* with *"CPA drops by 40% above 2,00,000 units"*.
- **The "Why" Test:** For every advantage you write, you should be able to answer *which KPI it improves*. If you can't, the point is incomplete.
- **One Formula per Answer:** A 14-mark answer that contains at least one correctly derived equation scores significantly higher than one with pure prose.
- **Draw the Matrix:** The "Advantage ↔ Pain-Point ↔ KPI ↔ Trade-off" matrix is the single most efficient way to earn full marks in a descriptive question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Automated Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design for Automated Assembly # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Design for Automated Assembly (DFAA)** is a structured, systematic engineering methodology that integrates assembly considerations into the earliest stages of product design to facilitate efficient, reliable, and economical **automated (robotic) assembly operations**. It is formally defined as: > "The practice of designing products such that the total cost of assembly is minimized, the rate of assembly is maximized, and the probability of assembly defects is minimized, by exploiting the deterministic, repeatable, and force-controlled capabilities of programmable manipulators and dedicated fixturing systems." In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, DFAA is positioned as a **concurrent engineering** activity that bridges **Product Design (CAD)** and **Manufacturing Process Planning (CAPP)**, ensuring that the geometrical, dimensional, and material features of a product are inherently "assembly-friendly" before any tooling is committed. **Core Synonyms/Acronyms:** - **DFA** — Design for Assembly - **DFAA** — Design for Automated Assembly - **Boothroyd DFA** — The most widely used quantitative DFA methodology - **DFM** — Design for Manufacture (parent discipline) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The University expects students to clearly distinguish between **Design for Manual Assembly (DFMA-hand)** and **Design for Automated Assembly (DFAA)**. In automated assembly, tolerances are **tighter**, parts must be **self-locating**, and the **human dexterity factor is removed**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego vs. Custom Furniture" Intuition Imagine two ways to put together a piece of furniture: - **Custom Carpentry (Poor DFA):** A skilled carpenter hand-fits tenons, applies glue, hammers dowels, clamps, and levels each joint. Highly skilled labor compensates for poor part design.
