```mermaid
    MASS --> SPRING[Spring Stiffness k]
    SPRING --> DAMPER[Damping c]
    DAMPER --> OUT[Steady State Response x of t equals A sin omega t minus phi]
    F -.Coupled via.-> SPRING
    F -.Coupled via.-> DAMPER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first diagram maps the *hardware* and the *flow of parts*. The second diagram maps the *temporal chain of cause and effect* during one vibration cycle. The third diagram is the **lumped-parameter model** used in Section 3.1 for analytical work — this is the form KTU examiners expect you to draw in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
*Define a vibratory bowl feeder. List any four of its main components.* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A vibratory bowl feeder (VBF) is an automatic parts-feeding device that uses high-frequency, small-amplitude vibration combined with a helical track to transport, orient, and discharge bulk parts in single file. The four main components are: **(i)** conical/cylindrical bowl with helical track, **(ii)** main support springs (inclined), **(iii)** electromagnetic drive coil with armature, and **(iv)** rectifier/power controller. *(Acceptable: base frame, tuning springs, tooling.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*State the role of inclined leaf springs in a vibratory bowl feeder. Why are at least three springs used?* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The inclined leaf springs **(i)** suspend the bowl on a defined natural frequency, **(ii)** store and release vibrational energy to sustain oscillation between electromagnetic pulses, and **(iii)** constrain the bowl's motion to the desired axis. **At least three springs are used** so that the bowl is statically determinate (a tri-pod arrangement) and cannot rotate or rock — two springs would allow the bowl to pivot about the line joining them, and one spring would be unstable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B.*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a vibratory bowl feeder. List its main components and state the function of each.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch:** (Draw a vertical cross-section showing the conical bowl, helical track inside, three inclined springs, electromagnetic coil at the base, armature, rectifier, and exit chute. Use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as a reference.)

**Working Principle — Stepwise Explanation:**

1. Bulk parts are loaded into the bowl.
2. AC mains (50 Hz) → rectifier → pulsed DC → energises the **electromagnetic coil**.
3. Each pulse pulls the **armature** downward, stretching the main springs.
4. Between pulses, the **springs release** stored energy, snapping the bowl upward.
5. The bowl vibrates at the supply frequency (typically 100 Hz for full-wave rectification).
6. The vertical vibration causes parts to **leave the track** (when $A\omega^2 > g$) and **re-land slightly uphill** due to friction and the helix angle.
7. Parts climb the spiral track; **tooling orients** them into a single attitude.
8. Oriented parts are **discharged** through the exit chute for downstream pick-and-place.

**Component Table (include in answer):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Bowl | Holds parts, provides helix surface |
| Helical Track | Lifts parts by frictional thrust |
| Main Springs | Define natural frequency, store energy |
| Electromagnetic Coil | Converts electrical pulses to mechanical thrust |
| Armature | Couples magnetic force to bowl via tuning springs |
| Tuning Springs | Allow independent control of bowl amplitude |
| Rectifier | Provides pulsed DC to coil |
| Tooling | Selectively orients parts |

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vibratory bowl feeder has the following specifications: combined spring stiffness $k = 1.6 \times 10^{5}$ N/m, total moving mass $m = 5$ kg, operating frequency $f = 100$ Hz, amplitude $A = 1$ mm, and track helix angle $\alpha = 5°$. Friction angle between part and track is $\phi = 25°$. Calculate:
   (i) the natural frequency of the bowl-spring system,
   (ii) the peak vertical acceleration of the bowl,
   (iii) the conveying velocity of the part along the track.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Natural Frequency:**

$$
f_n \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{5}}{5}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{3.2 \times 10^{4}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3.2 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; 178.885 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
f_n \;=\; \dfrac{178.885}{2\pi} \;\approx\; 28.47 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Peak Vertical Acceleration:**

$$
a_{max} \;=\; A\omega^{2} \;=\; A\,(2\pi f)^{2} \;=\; (1 \times 10^{-3})\times(2\pi \times 100)^{2}
$$

$$
a_{max} \;=\; 10^{-3} \times (628.32)^{2} \;=\; 10^{-3} \times 3.948 \times 10^{5} \;\approx\; 394.8 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{a_{max}}{g} \;=\; \dfrac{394.8}{9.81} \;\approx\; 40.3\,g
$$

> Parts experience ~40 g — well above the lift-off threshold, confirming the VBF will throw parts off the track reliably.

**(iii) Conveying Velocity (Boothroyd formula):**

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{\pi f A \cos\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}
$$

With $\alpha + \phi = 30°$, $\tan 30° = 0.5774$, $\cos 5° = 0.9962$:

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 100 \times 1 \times 0.9962}{0.5774} \;\approx\; 541.8 \text{ mm/s}
$$

> Conveying velocity $\approx$ **0.542 m/s**, a typical industrial value.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the electromagnetic drive mechanism of a vibratory bowl feeder. Discuss how the rectifier circuit affects the operating frequency.

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram:** (Draw the base with electromagnetic coil wound around a central core, an armature/lamination stack above it suspended by tuning springs, and the bowl above the armature supported on three inclined main springs. Show AC input and rectifier.)

**Mechanism:**

1. **AC mains (50 Hz, single phase)** is fed to a **bridge rectifier** that converts it to unidirectional pulsed DC.
2. Two operating modes are possible:
   - **Half-wave rectification:** One pulse per AC cycle → **50 pulses/sec → operating frequency = 50 Hz**.
   - **Full-wave rectification:** Two pulses per AC cycle → **100 pulses/sec → operating frequency = 100 Hz**.
3. Each pulse drives current through the coil, generating a magnetic flux that **pulls the armature downward** against the spring force.
4. When the pulse decays to zero, the springs **lift the armature (and bowl) back up**.
5. Hence the bowl vibrates at **twice the mains frequency in full-wave mode** — the most common industrial setting because higher frequency gives smoother conveying.

**Why frequency matters:**
- Higher frequency → more conveying cycles per second → faster feed rate.
- Higher frequency → smaller amplitude needed to maintain $A\omega^2 > g$ → gentler handling of delicate parts.
- Trade-off: Higher frequency increases power dissipation (recall $P \propto \omega^{2}$).

**(b) [7 Marks]** The moving mass of a VBF is 4 kg, and its three main springs have a combined stiffness of $1.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/m. If the feeder operates at 100 Hz (full-wave rectified 50 Hz supply) with an amplitude of 0.8 mm, calculate: **(i)** the natural frequency, **(ii)** the operating-to-natural frequency ratio (state the operating regime), and **(iii)** the stroke.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Natural Frequency:**

$$
f_n \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{5}}{4}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{4}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; 158.11 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
f_n \;=\; \dfrac{158.11}{2\pi} \;\approx\; 25.16 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Frequency Ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{f_{op}}{f_n} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{25.16} \;\approx\; 3.97
$$

> Since $f_{op} > f_n$ (ratio > 1), the VBF is operating in the **super-resonant regime** — the stable, industrial-standard mode of operation. The large ratio (≈4) ensures robust, well-damped motion insensitive to small variations in part mass.

**(iii) Stroke:**

$$
S \;=\; 2A \;=\; 2 \times 0.8 \text{ mm} \;=\; 1.6 \text{ mm}
$$

> Stroke = **1.6 mm** (peak-to-peak displacement of the bowl rim).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the VBF module:**
> 1. **Conflating "natural frequency" with "operating frequency."** $f_n$ is set by $k$ and $m$ (design choice). $f_{op}$ is locked to the mains supply (50/60/100/120 Hz). They are *not* the same.
> 2. **Confusing amplitude $A$ with stroke $S$.** Amplitude is the *peak* displacement; stroke is *peak-to-peak* ($S=2A$). Examiners specifically test this in numericals.
> 3. **Forgetting the friction angle $\phi$ in the conveying velocity formula.** Writing $v = \pi f A$ without accounting for $\phi$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Wrong rectifier → wrong frequency.** Half-wave = mains frequency. Full-wave = 2× mains frequency. Mixing these up gives wrong operating frequency.
> 5. **Drawing the springs as vertical.** In VBFs the springs are deliberately *inclined* to soften the system along the vibration axis ($k_{eff} = k\cos^2\beta$); a vertical-spring sketch is a structural red flag to the examiner.
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark per missing unit in long-answer sub-parts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VBF = self-feeding + self-orienting device** driven by electromagnetic vibration at 50/100 Hz.
- **Critical formula set (must be memorised):**
  - $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$
  - $a_{max} = A\omega^{2}$, must exceed $g$ for lift-off.
  - $v = \dfrac{\pi f A \cos\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$
  - $P_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,c\,\omega^{2}\,A^{2}$
- **Half-wave → $f_{op}$ = mains; Full-wave → $f_{op}$ = 2 × mains.** Memorise this!
- **Super-resonant operation** is the industrial norm ($f_{op} > f_n$); sub-resonant is rare and unstable.
- **Track helix angle α = 2° to 7°** — too steep → parts slide back; too shallow → no upward drift.
- **Components you must be able to label in a sketch:** bowl, helix track, main springs (3 inclined), electromagnetic coil, armature, tuning springs, rectifier, base, exit chute, orienting tooling.
- **Stroke = 2A** (peak-to-peak). **Amplitude = A** (peak). Do not interchange.
- **Friction angle $\phi$ (typically 20°–30° for metal-on-metal)** appears in *every* conveying-velocity numerical — never omit it.
- **Throughput** $Q$ depends on conveying velocity $v$, number of tracks $n$, and pitch $p$: $Q = 60\,n\,v/p$ parts/min.
- **Power dissipation scales as $\omega^{2}A^{2}$** — explains why high-frequency/high-amplitude VBFs need industrial-grade coils and forced cooling.
- **Engineering use cases:** fasteners, electronic connectors, caps, ball bearings, syringes, watch parts — all mass-production assembly lines.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme alignment:** Questions test the *derivation* of conveying velocity (Boothroyd), *natural vs operating frequency* distinction, and *component identification* — practice all three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Frequency, Track Acceleration, and Vibration Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Frequency, Track Acceleration, and Vibration Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Vibratory Bowl Feeders (VBF)** and **Linear Vibratory Feeders** used in automated part-orienting and conveying systems, the **operating performance** is governed by three interconnected kinematic and geometric parameters:

1. **Frequency of Vibration (f)**: The number of vibratory cycles per second (measured in **Hz**), driven by an electromagnetic or mechanical exciter.
2. **Track Acceleration (a)**: The instantaneous rate of change of velocity of the track surface, which must exceed a critical threshold to overcome the part's frictional/inertial resistance and produce net forward motion.
3. **Vibration Angle (θ)**: The angular orientation of the vibration vector relative to the horizontal track surface, which determines the efficiency of energy transfer from vibration to directional part motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Vibratory Feeder Design):**
> The performance of a vibratory feeder — specifically its **conveying velocity**, **part stability**, and **feed rate** — is a *coupled function* of frequency, amplitude (via acceleration), and vibration angle. Isolating one parameter without considering the others leads to suboptimal design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a toy car up a slight ramp:
- If you **shake the ramp very fast but with tiny shakes** (high frequency, low amplitude), the car vibrates in place but barely moves forward.
- If you **shake slowly with large pushes** (low frequency, high amplitude), the car moves in big jumps but with poor control.
- If you **shake at the wrong angle** (e.g., purely vertical), the car just bounces up and down without traveling along the ramp.

The **sweet spot** — where frequency, acceleration magnitude, and direction are tuned together — is what makes a vibratory feeder in a factory reliably deliver thousands of oriented parts per hour to an assembly station.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Conveying Speed (v_c):**
> The net average forward velocity of a part along the track, typically measured in **mm/s** or **m/min**, given by:
> $$v_c \approx \frac{A \cdot \omega}{2\pi} \cdot f(\theta, \mu, g)$$
> where $A$ is the vibration amplitude, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, $\theta$ is the vibration angle, $\mu$ is the friction coefficient, and $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** is gravitational acceleration.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Frequency | $f$ | 50 – 300 (typical), 50/60 (mains-locked) | **Hz** |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | $2\pi f$ | **rad/s** |
| Amplitude | $A$ | 0.5 – 5.0 | **mm** |
| Track Acceleration (peak) | $a_{max}$ | 2 – 20 | **g** (multiples of 9.81 m/s²) |
| Vibration Angle | $\theta$ | 20° – 35° | **degrees** |
| Conveying Speed | $v_c$ | 0.05 – 0.5 | **m/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### A. Effect of Frequency (f)

The frequency $f$ governs how often a part is "kicked" forward by the track per unit time.

**Operating Principle:**
- A higher frequency means more impulses per second → higher potential feed rate.
- However, the *stroke length* (amplitude) decreases as frequency rises (for a constant drive power).
- The relationship between frequency, amplitude, and the resulting **track acceleration** is non-linear: $a \propto f^2 \cdot A$.

**Key Effects:**
- **Too low f:** Parts slide, lose orientation control, may jam.
- **Too high f:** Parts "float" on the track, lose contact, conveying becomes erratic; also induces resonance in supporting structures.
- **Mains-locked f = 50 Hz / 60 Hz** is preferred in industry for synchronization with AC electromagnets.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:**
> The peak track acceleration is $a_{max} = \omega^2 A = (2\pi f)^2 A$. Doubling the frequency *quadruples* the acceleration for the same amplitude.

---

### B. Effect of Track Acceleration (a)

The instantaneous track displacement is:
$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$$

Differentiating twice:
$$a(t) = \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -A \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$

The **peak (maximum) track acceleration** is therefore:
$$a_{max} = A \omega^2 = A (2\pi f)^2$$

**Condition for Part Movement:**
For a part resting on an inclined track, motion occurs when the track acceleration projected along the track exceeds gravity and friction forces. For a horizontal track:
$$a_{max} \geq g \cdot \mu$$
where $\mu$ is the coefficient of static friction. In practice, a safety factor of **2 to 5 g** is used:
$$a_{design} = (2 \text{ to } 5) \cdot g$$

**Industrial Standard:** Most commercial vibratory feeders operate with $a_{max}$ between **2g and 8g** ($19.6$ to $78.5$ **m/s²**).

---

### C. Effect of Vibration Angle (θ)

The **vibration angle θ** is defined as the angle between the vibration direction (of the track) and the horizontal track surface.

**Geometric Significance:**
- The vibration force vector $F_v$ can be decomposed into:
  - **Horizontal component:** $F_h = F_v \cos\theta$ → drives conveying
  - **Vertical component:** $F_v = F_v \sin\theta$ → lifts the part

**Optimal Range:**
- **θ < 20°:** Insufficient vertical lift; parts drag, orientation lost.
- **θ > 35°:** Excessive vertical bouncing; parts lose track contact, feed rate drops.
- **θ_optimum ≈ 25° – 30°** for most ferrous cylindrical parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two-Mass" Theory Connection:**
> For a tuned vibratory feeder, the vibration angle is set by the **spring orientation** (leaf springs or rubber isolators). The angle determines the **throw angle** of the parts, and the optimum value minimizes backward slip.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | **rad/s** |
| 2 | $a_{max} = A \omega^2$ | Peak track acceleration | **m/s²** |
| 3 | $a_{max} = A(2\pi f)^2$ | Peak acceleration in terms of $f$ | **m/s²** |
| 4 | $a_{design} = k \cdot g$ (where $k = 2$ to $5$) | Design acceleration with safety factor | **m/s²** |
| 5 | $F_h = F_v \cos\theta$ | Horizontal force component | **N** |
| 6 | $F_v = F_v \sin\theta$ | Vertical force component | **N** |
| 7 | $v_c \propto f \cdot A \cdot \sin(2\theta)$ | Conveying speed approximation | **m/s** |
| 8 | $f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of feeder system | **Hz** |
| 9 | $P = F \cdot v \cdot \eta^{-1}$ | Drive power required | **W** |
| 10 | $\beta_{crit} = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | Critical tilt angle for slip | **radians** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Pipe Substitution:** All instances of $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in formulas above are written without the pipe character to prevent table parsing errors in markdown. They denote magnitude, e.g., $\vert a \vert$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These three parameters are tuned together in:
- **Automotive parts sorting** (e.g., feeding bolts, springs to assembly lines)
- **Pharmaceutical capsule orientation**
- **Electronic component feeding** (SMT chip feeders)
- **Cap and closure feeding** in bottling plants
- **Medical device assembly** (syringes, vials)

A poorly tuned combination leads to **jamming**, **misorientation**, **low feed rates**, and **excessive noise** (>85 dB) — a common KTU viva question topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Track Acceleration

**Step 1: Define the displacement of the track surface**
The track vibrates sinusoidally about its mean position. Let the instantaneous displacement be:
$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$$

where:
- $A$ = peak amplitude of vibration (**m**)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency (**rad/s**)
- $t$ = time (**s**)

**Step 2: Compute the velocity by first differentiation**
$$\frac{dx}{dt} = v(t) = A \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The peak velocity is:
$$v_{max} = A \omega$$

**Step 3: Compute the acceleration by second differentiation**
$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = a(t) = -A \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4: Determine the peak (maximum) magnitude of acceleration**
The peak value of $\vert \sin(\omega t) \vert$ is **1**, so:
$$a_{max} = A \omega^2$$

**Step 5: Express in terms of frequency $f$**
Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$:
$$a_{max} = A (2\pi f)^2 = 4 \pi^2 f^2 A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:**
> If $f = 50$ **Hz** and $A = 1$ **mm** $= 10^{-3}$ **m**, then:
> $$a_{max} = 4 \pi^2 (50)^2 \times 10^{-3} \approx 98.7 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 10\,g$$
> This is a typical industrial design value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conveying Speed as a Function of θ

**Step 1: Define the part motion cycle**
During each cycle, the part moves forward when the track acceleration exceeds the gravitational component along the incline, and may slip backward during the return stroke.

**Step 2: Forward stroke displacement**
The forward displacement per cycle is approximated as:
$$\Delta x_f = \frac{A \cos\theta}{1 - (g/a_{max}) \sin\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the track inclination angle.

**Step 3: Return stroke slip (backward)**
$$\Delta x_b = \frac{A \cos\theta}{1 + (g/a_{max}) \sin\alpha}$$

**Step 4: Net forward displacement per cycle**
$$\Delta x_{net} = \Delta x_f - \Delta x_b = \frac{2 A \cos\theta \cdot (g/a_{max}) \sin\alpha}{1 - (g/a_{max})^2 \sin^2\alpha}$$

**Step 5: Conveying speed**
With $N = f$ cycles per second:
$$v_c = f \cdot \Delta x_{net} = \frac{2 f A \cos\theta \cdot (g/a_{max}) \sin\alpha}{1 - (g/a_{max})^2 \sin^2\alpha}$$

**Simplification (for small α, i.e., nearly horizontal track):**
$$v_c \approx 2 f A \cos\theta \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \left(\frac{g}{a_{max}}\right) \cdot \left(1 + \left(\frac{g}{a_{max}}\right)^2 \sin^2\alpha + \cdots\right)$$

**Leading-order term:**
$$v_c \approx \frac{2 f A g \sin\alpha \cos\theta}{a_{max}}$$

Using $a_{max} = 4\pi^2 f^2 A$:
$$v_c \approx \frac{g \sin\alpha \cos\theta}{2 \pi^2 f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive result:** For a fixed track geometry, *increasing frequency actually decreases conveying speed* when amplitude is held constant. In practice, amplitude is reduced at higher frequencies due to drive dynamics, making the relationship more complex.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Parameter

| Parameter | Increase Effect on $a_{max}$ | Increase Effect on $v_c$ | Effect on Part Stability | Risk if Over-Increased |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency $f$ ↑ | ↑ Quadratically ($f^2$) | ↓ (holding $A$ constant) | ↓ (parts float) | Resonance, noise, part damage |
| Amplitude $A$ ↑ | ↑ Linearly | ↑ Linearly | Variable | Track overstress |
| Vibration Angle $\theta$ ↑ (0°→90°) | No direct effect on $a_{max}$ | Maximum at $\theta \approx 30°$ | ↓ at extremes | Bouncing or dragging |
| Acceleration $a_{max}$ ↑ | — | ↑ Up to a limit | ↑ at moderate $a$ | Excessive wear, ejection |

---

## 3.4 Combined Design Equation (KTU Exam Favorite)

The design selection of $(f, A, \theta)$ must simultaneously satisfy:

**Equation 1 — Acceleration Constraint:**
$$A (2\pi f)^2 \geq k \cdot g, \quad k \in [2, 5]$$

**Equation 2 — Conveying Speed Constraint:**
$$v_c = \frac{g \sin\alpha \cos\theta}{2\pi^2 f} \cdot N \geq v_{required}$$

**Equation 3 — Resonance Avoidance:**
$$f_{operating} \neq f_{natural} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{spring}}{m_{total}}}$$

**Equation 4 — Power Constraint:**
$$P_{drive} = \frac{m_{total} \cdot a_{max}^2}{4 \pi^2 f \cdot \eta} \leq P_{available}$$

where $\eta$ is the electromechanical efficiency of the drive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Parameter Interaction in a Vibratory Feeder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 50Hz] --> B[Electromagnetic Coil Drive]
    B --> C[Vibration Exciter Unit]
    C --> D[Leaf Spring Set at Angle theta]
    D --> E[Vibrating Track Surface]
    E --> F[Part on Track]
    G[Amplitude A] --> E
    H[Frequency f] --> C
    I[Angle theta 20 to 35 deg] --> D
    E --> J[Horizontal Force F h equals F v cos theta]
    E --> K[Vertical Force F v equals F v sin theta]
    J --> L[Forward Conveying Motion]
    K --> M[Part Lift and Throw]
    L --> N[Net Conveying Speed v c]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Oriented Part Delivery to Next Station]
    P[Safety Factor k equals 2 to 5] --> Q[Design Acceleration a design equals k times g]
    Q --> R[Peak Track Acceleration a max equals A omega squared]
    R --> S[Feasibility Check]
    S --> T[Final Operating Point]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Design Flowchart for Parameter Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Part Mass m and Friction Coeff mu] --> S2[Step 2: Choose Track Inclination alpha]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Estimate Required Conveying Speed v req]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Select Safety Factor k in 2 to 5]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Required a max equals k times g]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Choose Vibration Angle theta in 20 to 35 deg]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Select Drive Frequency f equals 50 or 60 Hz]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute Required Amplitude A equals a max over 4 pi squared f squared]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Check Resonant Frequency Avoidance]
    S9 --> S10{Resonance Safe}
    S10 -->|Yes| S11[Verify Conveying Speed Achieved]
    S10 -->|No| S7
    S11 --> S12[Compute Drive Power and Finalize]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: Force Decomposition at Vibration Angle θ

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Fv[Total Vibration Force F v] --> FH[Horizontal Component F h equals F v cos theta]
    Fv --> FV[Vertical Component F v equals F v sin theta]
    FH --> Motion1[Drives Part Forward Along Track]
    FV --> Motion2[Lifts Part Off Track During Stroke]
    Motion1 --> NetV[Net Conveying Velocity v c]
    Motion2 --> NetV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> The vibration force vector $F_v$ acts along the vibration axis. The horizontal component $F_h$ does useful work in conveying, while the vertical component $F_v$ is responsible for momentarily lifting the part to reduce sliding friction. The angle $\theta$ is therefore the **design knob** that allocates energy between these two effects.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define **track acceleration** in the context of a vibratory feeder. Why must it exceed a critical multiple of $g$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Track acceleration is the instantaneous rate of change of velocity of the vibrating track surface. For a sinusoidal vibration $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$, the peak track acceleration is $a_{max} = A \omega^2 = A(2\pi f)^2$.

It must exceed a critical multiple of $g$ (typically $2g$ to $5g$) to **(1)** overcome the static friction holding the part on the track, **(2)** provide a net forward impulse per cycle that exceeds the backward slip during the return stroke, and **(3)** ensure that the part is momentarily airborne (micro-throw) so that it does not continuously drag and lose orientation.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark for the three reasons.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
What is the **vibration angle (θ)** in a vibratory bowl feeder? State its typical range and the consequence of operating outside this range.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The vibration angle $\theta$ is the angle made by the vibration vector (driven by the leaf spring orientation) with the horizontal track surface.

**Typical range:** $\theta = 20°$ to $35°$, with an optimum near $25°$–$30°$.

**Consequences of deviation:**
- $\theta < 20°$: Insufficient vertical lift; parts drag and lose orientation, feed rate collapses.
- $\theta > 35°$: Excessive vertical bouncing; parts lose track contact, conveying becomes erratic and noisy.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark range, 1 mark consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve the chosen question completely.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** A linear vibratory feeder operates at a frequency of **50 Hz** with a peak amplitude of **1.2 mm**. The vibration angle is set to **30°**. Calculate:
1. The angular frequency $\omega$.
2. The peak track acceleration $a_{max}$ in m/s² and in units of $g$.
3. The horizontal and vertical components of the peak vibration force on a part of mass **0.25 kg** (assume the peak force equals $m \cdot a_{max}$).

**(b)** Discuss how a change in operating frequency from 50 Hz to 100 Hz (with amplitude adjusted to maintain the same $a_{max}$) would affect (i) the conveying speed, (ii) the noise level, and (iii) the part orientation stability. Use suitable equations.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $f = 50$ **Hz**
- $A = 1.2$ **mm** $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ **m**
- $\theta = 30°$
- $m = 0.25$ **kg**
- $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**

**Step 1: Angular Frequency**
$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Peak Track Acceleration**
$$a_{max} = A \omega^2 = (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (314.16)^2$$
$$a_{max} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 98696$$
$$a_{max} \approx 118.4 \text{ m/s}^2$$

In units of $g$:
$$\frac{a_{max}}{g} = \frac{118.4}{9.81} \approx 12.07\,g$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is well above the typical design safety range (2–5g), suggesting that either the amplitude is too large or the frequency should be lowered for this part. (In practice, the part would be **ejected** at this acceleration.)

**Step 3: Peak Vibration Force**
$$F_v = m \cdot a_{max} = 0.25 \times 118.4 = 29.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Force Components**
$$F_h = F_v \cos\theta = 29.6 \times \cos(30°) = 29.6 \times 0.866 = 25.63 \text{ N}$$
$$F_v = F_v \sin\theta = 29.6 \times \sin(30°) = 29.6 \times 0.500 = 14.80 \text{ N}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\omega$ | 314.16 rad/s |
| $a_{max}$ | 118.4 m/s² |
| $a_{max}$ in $g$ | ≈ 12.07 g |
| $F_v$ (total) | 29.6 N |
| $F_h$ (horizontal) | 25.63 N |
| $F_v$ (vertical) | 14.80 N |

*(7 marks: 2 for ω, 2 for a_max with g, 3 for force components.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Discussion**

**Scenario:** Frequency increased from 50 Hz → 100 Hz; amplitude adjusted to keep $a_{max}$ constant.

Since $a_{max} = A(2\pi f)^2$, holding $a_{max}$ constant requires:
$$A_{new} = A_{old} \times \left(\frac{f_{old}}{f_{new}}\right)^2 = 1.2 \times \left(\frac{50}{100}\right)^2 = 0.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Effect (i) — Conveying Speed:**
Using the simplified formula $v_c \approx \frac{g \sin\alpha \cos\theta}{2 \pi^2 f}$ (with $A$ constant), doubling $f$ halves $v_c$. But here $A$ has dropped to $1/4$, so:
$$v_{c,new} = v_{c,old} \times \frac{A_{new} f_{new}}{A_{old} f_{old}} = v_{c,old} \times \frac{0.30 \times 100}{1.2 \times 50} = 0.5 \cdot v_{c,old}$$
**Conveying speed decreases by 50%.**

**Effect (ii) — Noise Level:**
Noise in vibratory feeders is proportional to $f^2$ and to track velocity. Doubling the frequency increases airborne noise by approximately **6–10 dB**, potentially violating occupational safety norms (OSHA limit: 85 dB for 8-hr exposure).

**Effect (iii) — Part Orientation Stability:**
Higher frequency with smaller amplitude leads to **micro-throws** instead of macro-throws. Parts "chatter" on the track with reduced orientation control. The lateral alignment of cylindrical parts becomes less stable, and the **deflection of the leaf springs** at 100 Hz approaches the natural frequency of the feeder, risking **resonance**.

*(7 marks: 2 for conveying speed derivation, 2 for noise reasoning, 3 for stability discussion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, explain the **role of the vibration angle θ** in a linear vibratory feeder. Show mathematically how the horizontal and vertical force components depend on θ.

**(b)** A feeder must convey a part of mass **0.4 kg** at a minimum speed of **0.25 m/s**. The track is inclined at **3°** and the friction coefficient is **0.3**. The vibration angle is fixed at **28°**. Determine the required operating frequency $f$ and amplitude $A$ using a design safety factor $k = 4$. State any assumptions.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Role of Vibration Angle**

*(Refer to the Mermaid Force Decomposition diagram in Section 4.3.)*

The vibration angle $\theta$ is the angle between the **vibration force vector** $F_v$ and the **horizontal track surface**. It is set geometrically by the orientation of the leaf springs or rubber isolators that support the track.

**Mathematical Decomposition:**
$$F_h = F_v \cos\theta \quad \text{(horizontal, drives conveying)}$$
$$F_v = F_v \sin\theta \quad \text{(vertical, lifts the part)}$$

**Role:**
- $\theta$ controls the **partition of vibrational energy** between horizontal thrust and vertical lift.
- An optimal $\theta$ maximizes the product $\cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)$, which peaks at $\theta = 45°$, but practical constraints (track geometry, part stability) restrict operation to **20°–35°**.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram/role, 2 for decomposition, 2 for energy partition.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Design Calculation**

**Given:**
- $m = 0.4$ **kg**
- $v_c \geq 0.25$ **m/s**
- $\alpha = 3°$, $\mu = 0.3$
- $\theta = 28°$
- $k = 4$, $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**

**Step 1: Required Peak Track Acceleration**
$$a_{max} = k \cdot g = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 4g$$

**Step 2: Choose Operating Frequency**
Industrial standard: $f = 50$ **Hz** (mains-locked, robust, well-characterized).

**Step 3: Compute Required Amplitude**
From $a_{max} = A(2\pi f)^2$:
$$A = \frac{a_{max}}{(2\pi f)^2} = \frac{39.24}{(2\pi \times 50)^2} = \frac{39.24}{98696}$$
$$A \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.398 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Verify Conveying Speed**
Using the simplified model:
$$v_c = \frac{2 f A g \sin\alpha \cos\theta}{a_{max}}$$
$$v_c = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 0.000398 \times 9.81 \times \sin(3°) \times \cos(28°)}{39.24}$$
$$v_c = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 0.000398 \times 9.81 \times 0.0523 \times 0.883}{39.24}$$
$$v_c \approx \frac{0.01804}{39.24} \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

This is **far below** the required 0.25 m/s.

**Step 5: Recompute Required Amplitude from Speed Equation**
From the simplified conveying speed formula, ignoring the $\alpha^2$ correction:
$$A_{req} = \frac{v_c \cdot a_{max}}{2 f g \sin\alpha \cos\theta}$$
$$A_{req} = \frac{0.25 \times 39.24}{2 \times 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.0523 \times 0.883}$$
$$A_{req} = \frac{9.81}{4.527} \approx 2.17 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Re-verify Acceleration**
$$a_{max,new} = (2.17 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2\pi \times 50)^2 = 2.17 \times 10^{-3} \times 98696 \approx 214 \text{ m/s}^2$$

This is **~21.8 g** — excessive! There is a design conflict.

**Step 7: Iterative Resolution**
The required acceleration for $A = 2.17$ **mm** is too high. The student should:
- Use a higher design acceleration $k = 5$: $a_{max} = 49.05$ **m/s²**, and accept that $v_c$ will be lower than ideal, OR
- Increase $\alpha$ to **5°** to boost $v_c$ via the $\sin\alpha$ term, OR
- Operate at a higher frequency (e.g., 100 Hz) with reduced amplitude, OR
- Use a different feeder type (e.g., a cascade bowl feeder with helical track).

**Final Recommended Operating Point:**
$$f = 50 \text{ Hz}, \quad A \approx 1.0 \text{ mm}, \quad \theta = 28°$$
$$a_{max} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 98696 \approx 98.7 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 10g$$
$$v_c \approx \frac{2 \times 50 \times 0.001 \times 9.81 \times 0.0523 \times 0.883}{98.7} \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for Students:** A nearly horizontal track ($\alpha = 3°$) cannot achieve $v_c = 0.25$ m/s with a linear feeder alone. The design must be revisited. This is a **realistic KTU design question** that tests both theory and engineering judgment.

*(7 marks: 2 for acceleration, 2 for amplitude, 3 for speed verification and design iteration.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in amplitude — leads to wrong $a_{max}$ by a factor of 1000.
> 2. **Using $a_{max} = \omega A$ instead of $a_{max} = \omega^2 A$** — this is the *velocity* peak, not acceleration. (Common 1-mark deduction.)
> 3. **Confusing vibration angle θ with track inclination α** — they are *different angles* and serve different purposes.
> 4. **Ignoring units of g** in final answers — always state $a_{max}$ in both m/s² and multiples of $g$ for clarity.
> 5. **Not checking resonance** — operating $f$ near $f_{natural}$ is a fatal design error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Peak track acceleration:** $a_{max} = A \omega^2 = A(2\pi f)^2$. Doubling $f$ **quadruples** $a_{max}$ for the same $A$.
- **Design safety factor:** $a_{design} = (2 \text{ to } 5) \cdot g = (19.6 \text{ to } 49.05)$ **m/s²** for typical industrial feeders.
- **Vibration angle range:** $\theta = 20°$ to $35°$ (optimum near $25°$–$30°$).
- **Mains-locked frequency:** $f = 50$ **Hz** (India/Europe/Kerala) or $f = 60$ **Hz** (USA).
- **Conveying speed is proportional to** $f \cdot A \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \cos\theta / a_{max}$ — a coupled function of all three parameters.
- **Higher $f$ with reduced $A$** can maintain $a_{max}$ but reduces $v_c$, increases noise, and risks resonance.
- **Force decomposition:** $F_h = F_v \cos\theta$ (conveying) and $F_v = F_v \sin\theta$ (lifting).
- **Resonance avoidance:** Operating frequency must differ from natural frequency $f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ by at least ±20%.
- **Industrial rule of thumb:** For stable conveying, $a_{max} / g$ should be between **2 and 8**.
- **For KTU numerical problems:** Always convert $A$ from **mm to m**, $f$ from **Hz** is used directly, and report $a_{max}$ in both **m/s²** and **g**.
- **Track inclination $\alpha$** is the *geometric* angle of the track; **vibration angle $\theta$** is the *dynamic* angle of the drive force. Do not confuse them.
- **The vibration angle determines the "throw angle"** of the part — the trajectory the part follows relative to the track surface during a cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Vibratory Feeders: Types (e.g., Reciprocating Tube Hopper, Center Board Hopper, External Gate Hopper, Rotary Disk Feeder)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Vibratory Feeders: Types (e.g., Reciprocating Tube Hopper, Center Board Hopper, External Gate Hopper, Rotary Disk Feeder)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Non-Vibratory Feeders — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Feeder in Automated Manufacturing?

In an automated assembly or manufacturing line, a **feeder** is a device that takes parts supplied in bulk (randomly oriented inside a bin, hopper, or magazine) and presents them one at a time, in a **predictable orientation and position**, to the next workstation (e.g., a pick-and-place robot, an indexing conveyor, or an assembly machine).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCRUT403):** A feeder is a *part orienting and feeding device* whose primary function is to convert a disordered bulk supply of components into a single-file, properly oriented stream that can be processed by downstream automation.

## 1.2 The Vibratory vs. Non-Vibratory Classification

Feeders are broadly classified based on **how energy is transferred to the part** to move it:

| Class | Energy Source | Typical Frequency | Typical Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vibratory Feeders** | Electromagnetic / mechanical vibration (50 Hz to 200 Hz) | High (cyclic) | Up to 2000 parts/min |
| **Non-Vibratory Feeders** | Pure mechanical motion (reciprocation, rotation, gravity) | Low (cycle-based) | 30 to 300 parts/min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** This module focuses on the *non-vibratory* family — feeders that use **gravity, reciprocation, rotation, or a combination** to feed and orient parts. These are essential when vibration can damage delicate parts (e.g., electronics, polished surfaces) or when part geometry prevents effective vibratory feeding.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Line at a Canteen"

Imagine a busy canteen counter where steel plates are stacked in a deep basket:

- **Vibratory feeder** = A waiter who *shakes* the basket so the plates pop up to the top and walk out one-by-one on a vibrating tray. Fast, but the plates can chip or scratch.
- **Non-vibratory feeder** = A waiter who uses a **sliding scoop, a rotating turntable, or a swinging arm** to lift each plate and place it on the counter. Slower, but gentle and reliable for oddly shaped dishes (cups with handles, soup bowls, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study non-vibratory feeders?** They are the **workhorses of medium-speed assembly lines** (automotive sub-assemblies, hardware, fasteners, pharmaceutical caps) where part geometry or material sensitivity rules out vibratory bowls.

## 1.4 Roadmap of the Four Non-Vibratory Feeders Covered in This Module

The four canonical non-vibratory feeder types discussed in KTU Module 3 are:

1. **Reciprocating Tube Hopper** — a sliding tube moves back-and-forth to advance parts.
2. **Center Board Hopper** — a vertical plate reciprocates inside a hopper bowl to push parts outward to a discharge slot.
3. **External Gate Hopper** — parts slide by gravity out of a hopper through a controlled external gate.
4. **Rotary Disk Feeder** — a rotating horizontal disk with machined features picks parts out of a surrounding hopper.

> [!TIP]
> **Cognitive Hook for Students:** Each of these four feeders solves the *same problem* (one-part-at-a-time delivery) using a *different physical mechanism*. When you study them, always ask: **"What is the source of motion? What is the orienting feature? Where does the part leave the feeder?"**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Functional Anatomy of a Non-Vibratory Feeder

Every non-vibratory feeder, regardless of type, contains **four functional zones**:

1. **Bulk Storage Zone (Hopper / Bin):** Holds the random supply of parts.
2. **Singulation Zone:** Isolates one part from the bulk.
3. **Orientation Zone:** Re-orients the part into a desired attitude.
4. **Discharge Zone:** Releases the oriented part to the downstream track or pickup point.

## 2.2 Detailed Operating Principles of Each Feeder Type

### 2.2.1 Reciprocating Tube Hopper

- **Construction:** A cylindrical tube closed at one end is mounted inside a *V-shaped* or *inclined* hopper. The tube is connected to a crank-slider or cam mechanism that drives it in a **linear reciprocating motion** (forward stroke + return stroke).
- **Working Cycle:**
  1. **Forward (loading) stroke:** The tube retracts, drawing a single part into the open mouth of the tube.
  2. **Return (discharge) stroke:** The tube advances, pushing the part out of the hopper into a delivery chute.
- **Orientation:** Achieved by the *internal bore diameter* of the tube being slightly larger than one critical dimension of the part (e.g., the head of a screw) but smaller than the other. Parts whose long axis aligns with the tube axis slide in; others are rejected.
- **Typical Parts:** Screws, bolts, pins, rivets, small cylindrical caps.

### 2.2.2 Center Board Hopper

- **Construction:** A vertical rectangular *center board* (or center plate) is mounted inside a *circular hopper bowl*. The board reciprocates **up and down** (or sometimes *horizontally* in a variant) driven by a cam.
- **Working Cycle:**
  1. **Down stroke:** The board descends, sweeping parts *outward and upward* along the curved hopper wall.
  2. **Up stroke:** The board rises, allowing the parts to fall back down and reflow inward.
  3. This *pumping action* causes parts to migrate toward a peripheral discharge slot, where they are picked off.
- **Orientation:** Parts that can roll freely (spheres, short cylinders) orient naturally; the discharge slot geometry filters out wrong-orientation parts.
- **Typical Parts:** Steel balls, short rollers, bushings, washers.

### 2.2.3 External Gate Hopper

- **Construction:** A simple inclined hopper (or bin) with a **swinging / sliding gate** mounted on the *outside* of the discharge opening. The gate is actuated by a cam, solenoid, or pneumatic cylinder.
- **Working Cycle:**
  1. The gate *closes* the discharge slot; parts rest by gravity against the inside of the gate.
  2. The gate *opens* momentarily, allowing **one part** (or a controlled batch) to fall through.
  3. The gate *closes* again, blocking the next part until the cycle repeats.
- **Orientation:** Achieved by a *restrictor plate* or *orienting lip* upstream of the gate that ensures only correctly-oriented parts reach the discharge.
- **Typical Parts:** Bottle caps, small boxy items, plastic housings.

### 2.2.4 Rotary Disk Feeder

- **Construction:** A **horizontal rotating disk** sits inside or below a stationary hopper. The disk's upper surface is machined with *slots*, *pockets*, *notches*, or *cavities* matched to the part geometry. The hopper sits over the disk and supplies parts to it.
- **Working Cycle:**
  1. Parts from the hopper drop onto the rotating disk.
  2. As the disk rotates, parts that *nest* into a feature are carried along on the disk.
  3. Parts that *do not nest* get scraped off by a wiper and fall back into the hopper.
  4. Carried parts reach a discharge station where they are lifted off (by a pickup tool, magnetic pickup, or vacuum) and transferred downstream.
- **Orientation:** The *shape of the disk cavity* matches the part in its correct orientation; mis-oriented parts simply do not fit and are rejected.
- **Typical Parts:** Bottle caps, coins, complex molded parts, electrical terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Rotary Disk Feeder is the **highest-speed** non-vibratory feeder and is preferred when part geometry allows a single-face "drop-in" orientation. It is widely used in pharmaceutical cap feeding and electrical connector assembly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Feed rate (parts/min) | $Q = N \cdot f$ | parts/min | $N$ = parts per cycle, $f$ = cycles/min |
| $f$ | Cycle frequency (for cam-driven feeders) | $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi} \cdot 60$ | cycles/min | $\omega$ = cam angular speed in rad/s |
| $T_c$ | Cycle time | $T_c = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | min/cycle | Used in production line balancing |
| $v_{avg}$ | Average reciprocating velocity | $v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_s \cdot f}{60}$ | m/s | $L_s$ = stroke length |
| $n_d$ | Disk rotational speed | $n_d = \dfrac{Q}{p}$ | rev/min | $p$ = number of pockets on disk |
| $V_h$ | Hopper volumetric capacity | $V_h = A_h \cdot h$ | m³ | $A_h$ = hopper cross-section, $h$ = fill height |
| $m_s$ | Mass of bulk parts in hopper | $m_s = V_h \cdot \rho_b \cdot \phi$ | kg | $\rho_b$ = bulk part density, $\phi$ = fill factor ($\phi \le 1$) |
| $T_{refill}$ | Refill time | $T_{refill} = \dfrac{m_s}{Q \cdot m_p}$ | min | $m_p$ = mass per part |
| $\eta_o$ | Orientation efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\text{correctly oriented parts}}{\text{total parts discharged}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Critical KPI for feeder selection |
| $P_{cam}$ | Cam drive power (approx) | $P_{cam} = \dfrac{F_{drag} \cdot v_{avg}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | W | $F_{drag}$ = drag force on part, $\eta_{mech}$ = mechanical efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the unit conversion between *seconds* and *minutes* in feed rate problems. If the cam speed $\omega$ is given in rad/s, **multiply by $\dfrac{60}{2\pi}$** to convert to rev/min before computing $Q$. The examiners routinely award zero marks if units are inconsistent.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Reciprocating tube hoppers feed bolts and studs to robotic nut-runners on engine assembly lines.
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging:** Rotary disk feeders orient bottle caps at speeds exceeding 300 caps/min.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Center board and rotary disk feeders present connectors, switches, and small molded components to pick-and-place heads.
- **Cost-Benefit:** Non-vibratory feeders are **30–50% cheaper** than equivalent vibratory bowls for parts with axial symmetry, but they require more frequent operator intervention for refilling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Feed Rate of a Reciprocating Tube Hopper

A reciprocating tube hopper is driven by a crank-slider mechanism. We derive the relationship between cam/crank speed and parts per minute.

**Given:**
- Crank rotates at $N_{crank}$ revolutions per minute (rpm).
- The tube executes **one complete reciprocating cycle per crank revolution**.
- Each complete cycle discharges **one part**.

**Step 1 — Define the cycle.** A complete cycle = one forward stroke + one return stroke = one crank revolution.

**Step 2 — Number of parts per minute.**
$$Q = N_{crank} \cdot 1 = N_{crank} \quad \text{[parts/min]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert crank rpm to angular velocity.**
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N_{crank}}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**
$$Q = \dfrac{60 \, \omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{30 \, \omega}{\pi} \quad \text{[parts/min]}$$

**Step 5 — Account for orientation rejection.** Not every cycle discharges a *correctly oriented* part. Let the orientation efficiency be $\eta_o$. Then the *effective* feed rate of usable parts is:

$$Q_{eff} = \eta_o \cdot Q = \eta_o \cdot \dfrac{30 \, \omega}{\pi}$$

**Step 6 — Average sliding velocity of the part.** If the stroke length is $L_s$ (m), the average velocity over one full cycle is:

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_s \cdot N_{crank}}{60} = \dfrac{L_s \cdot N_{crank}}{30} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of 2 appears because the part travels distance $L_s$ on the forward stroke *and* the tube travels $L_s$ back — total displacement per cycle is $2 L_s$. KTU examiners check for this factor explicitly.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Rate of a Rotary Disk Feeder

**Given:**
- Disk rotates at $n_d$ revolutions per minute.
- The disk has $p$ equi-spaced pockets (pickup features) on its upper face.

**Step 1 — Pockets passing the pickup station per minute.**

$$Q = n_d \cdot p \quad \text{[parts/min]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert disk rpm to angular velocity.**
$$\omega_d = \dfrac{2\pi n_d}{60} = \dfrac{\pi n_d}{30} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

**Step 3 — Effective feed rate with orientation efficiency.**
$$Q_{eff} = \eta_o \cdot n_d \cdot p$$

**Step 4 — Linear speed of a point on the disk at radius $r$:**
$$v_{tip} = \omega_d \cdot r = \dfrac{\pi n_d \cdot r}{30} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for design?** If $v_{tip}$ is too high, parts will be *thrown off* the disk by centrifugal force and fail to nest. The design constraint is $v_{tip} \le v_{max}$, where $v_{max}$ depends on part weight and pocket geometry. A typical safe limit is **$v_{max} \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ m/s** for plastic parts.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cycle Time of a Cam-Driven Center Board Hopper

**Given:**
- Cam rotates at $N_{cam}$ rpm.
- The center board completes one full up-down cycle per cam revolution.

**Step 1 — Time for one cam revolution.**
$$T_{rev} = \dfrac{60}{N_{cam}} \quad \text{[s/cycle]}$$

**Step 2 — If only one part is discharged per $k$ cam cycles (e.g., due to hopper refill dynamics):**
$$T_{c,eff} = k \cdot \dfrac{60}{N_{cam}} = \dfrac{60k}{N_{cam}} \quad \text{[s/part]}$$

**Step 3 — Feed rate.**
$$Q = \dfrac{1}{T_{c,eff}} = \dfrac{N_{cam}}{60k} \quad \text{[parts/s]} = \dfrac{N_{cam}}{k} \quad \text{[parts/min]}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hopper Refill Interval

To plan operator intervention, we need to know how long the hopper will last.

**Given:**
- Hopper volume $V_h$ (m³).
- Bulk density of parts $\rho_b$ (kg/m³).
- Fill factor $\phi$ (typically 0.6 to 0.8).
- Mass per part $m_p$ (kg).
- Feed rate $Q$ (parts/min).

**Step 1 — Mass of parts in the hopper.**
$$m_s = V_h \cdot \rho_b \cdot \phi$$

**Step 2 — Number of parts in the hopper.**
$$N_h = \dfrac{m_s}{m_p} = \dfrac{V_h \cdot \rho_b \cdot \phi}{m_p}$$

**Step 3 — Time to empty the hopper.**
$$T_{refill} = \dfrac{N_h}{Q} = \dfrac{V_h \cdot \rho_b \cdot \phi}{m_p \cdot Q} \quad \text{[min]}$$

**Numerical Example (for practice):**
- $V_h = 0.005$ m³ (= 5 litres)
- $\rho_b = 7800$ kg/m³ (steel)
- $\phi = 0.7$
- $m_p = 0.020$ kg (a 20 g bolt)
- $Q = 60$ parts/min

$$m_s = 0.005 \cdot 7800 \cdot 0.7 = 27.3 \text{ kg}$$

$$N_h = \dfrac{27.3}{0.020} = 1365 \text{ parts}$$

$$T_{refill} = \dfrac{1365}{60} = 22.75 \text{ min}$$

So the operator must refill **every ~23 minutes** for continuous operation.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix of the Four Feeder Types

| Parameter | Reciprocating Tube Hopper | Center Board Hopper | External Gate Hopper | Rotary Disk Feeder |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary motion** | Linear reciprocation | Vertical reciprocation | Gravity + gate actuation | Rotation |
| **Drive mechanism** | Crank-slider / cam | Cam | Cam, solenoid, pneumatic | Geared motor |
| **Typical feed rate** | 30 – 100 parts/min | 20 – 80 parts/min | 30 – 150 parts/min | 60 – 400 parts/min |
| **Part size range** | Small (1 – 25 mm) | Small to medium (5 – 50 mm) | Small to large (10 – 100 mm) | Small to medium (5 – 75 mm) |
| **Best for part shape** | Long cylindrical (screws, pins) | Spherical / short cylindrical | Boxy / flat | Cap-like / single-face orientable |
| **Orientation principle** | Tube bore geometry | Gravity + peripheral slot | Restrictor plate | Disk pocket shape |
| **Gentleness on parts** | Medium (sliding contact) | High (gentle sweeping) | High (gravity drop) | Medium (centrifugal risk) |
| **Vibration risk** | None | None | None | None |
| **Capital cost** | Low | Low to medium | Very low | Medium to high |
| **Tooling changeover** | Easy (swap tube) | Moderate (swap board) | Very easy (adjust gate) | Difficult (re-machine disk) |
| **Typical application** | Bolt feeders in auto plants | Ball bearing feeders | Cap feeding in packaging | Bottle cap lines, connector assembly |
| **Key KTU exam keyword** | *Stroke length* | *Center board reciprocation* | *External gate timing* | *Rotational pick-up* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When a question says "compare" these feeders, examiners expect **at least four parameters** from the matrix above. Do not write a one-line answer like "rotary is faster" — always back it with a number and a reason.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **functional architecture** of each feeder. Because physical 3D geometry is hard to render in Mermaid, the diagrams use **process flow notation** to capture the motion sequence, orientation logic, and discharge path of each feeder.

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Reciprocating Tube Hopper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Parts in Hopper] --> B[Tube Mouth Opens to Hopper]
    B --> C[Forward Stroke - Single Part Enters Tube]
    C --> D[Return Stroke - Part Pushed Out]
    D --> E{Orientation Check}
    E -- Correct --> F[Discharge Chute to Downstream]
    E -- Wrong --> G[Reject to Recycle Bin]
    F --> H[Pickup at Next Station]
    G --> A
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Center Board Hopper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Parts in Circular Hopper] --> B[Center Board at Top Position]
    B --> C[Down Stroke - Board Sweeps Parts Outward]
    C --> D[Parts Migrate to Peripheral Slot]
    D --> E[Up Stroke - Parts Reflow Inward]
    E --> F{Part Reaches Discharge Slot}
    F -- Oriented Correctly --> G[Discharge to Track]
    F -- Wrong Orientation --> H[Falls Back into Hopper]
    G --> I[Pickup by Next Station]
    H --> A
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — External Gate Hopper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper with Bulk Parts] --> B[Restrictor Plate Aligns Parts]
    B --> C[Single Part Reaches External Gate]
    C --> D[Cam or Solenoid Actuates Gate]
    D --> E[Gate Opens - Part Falls by Gravity]
    E --> F[Gate Closes - Next Part Waits]
    F --> G[Part Lands on Conveyor or Pickup]
    G --> H[Next Cycle Begins]
    H --> B
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Rotary Disk Feeder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Parts in Stationary Hopper Above Disk] --> B[Disk Rotates at Angular Speed w]
    B --> C[Parts Drop onto Rotating Disk Surface]
    C --> D{Part Nests in Disk Pocket}
    D -- Yes --> E[Carried by Disk to Discharge Station]
    D -- No --> F[Wiper Blade Scrapes Off Part]
    F --> G[Part Falls Back into Hopper]
    E --> H[Pickup Tool Lifts Oriented Part]
    G --> A
    H --> I[Part Delivered to Downstream Station]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Selection Flowchart — Which Non-Vibratory Feeder to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Feeder Selection Problem] --> Q1{Part Shape}
    Q1 -- Long Cylindrical --> R1[Reciprocating Tube Hopper]
    Q1 -- Spherical or Short Cylinder --> R2[Center Board Hopper]
    Q1 -- Boxy or Flat --> R3[External Gate Hopper]
    Q1 -- Cap-like Single Face --> R4[Rotary Disk Feeder]
    R1 --> S1[Verify Stroke Length vs Part Length]
    R2 --> S2[Verify Board Travel vs Hopper Diameter]
    R3 --> S3[Verify Gate Opening vs Part Dimension]
    R4 --> S4[Verify Disk Pocket Geometry vs Part]
    S1 --> End[Final Design]
    S2 --> End
    S3 --> End
    S4 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in KTU exams:** When asked to "draw the schematic," a clean process-flow diagram with labelled zones (Bulk Storage → Singulation → Orientation → Discharge) is **fully acceptable** and often scores more marks than a poorly-rendered physical sketch. Always label the *drive mechanism*, the *part motion direction*, and the *discharge point*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 3: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test *recall* and *direct conceptual understanding*. They are usually one-liner answers of 3–4 lines.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a non-vibratory feeder. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A non-vibratory feeder is a part-orienting and feeding device that uses mechanical motion (reciprocation, rotation, or gravity) — rather than vibration — to deliver bulk-supplied components one at a time in a predetermined orientation to a downstream workstation.
**Examples:** (i) Reciprocating Tube Hopper, (ii) Rotary Disk Feeder.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any four types of non-vibratory feeders covered in Module 3.**

**Model Answer:**
The four types are:
1. Reciprocating Tube Hopper
2. Center Board Hopper
3. External Gate Hopper
4. Rotary Disk Feeder

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** For Part B, students frequently lose marks because they (a) confuse the operating principles of center board and reciprocating tube hoppers, (b) forget to include the **orientation efficiency factor** $\eta_o$ in feed rate derivations, and (c) draw poorly-labelled diagrams. Always (i) state the principle, (ii) write the governing equation with all symbols defined, (iii) draw a clean labelled schematic, and (iv) provide a numerical answer with correct units.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q3 (a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Rotary Disk Feeder** with a neat schematic. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A Rotary Disk Feeder consists of a horizontally mounted rotating disk driven by a geared motor, a stationary circular hopper positioned above the disk to supply bulk parts, a wiper blade to remove mis-oriented parts, and a pickup station on the periphery where oriented parts are lifted off the disk. The upper face of the disk is machined with *pockets* (slots, holes, or cavities) whose geometry matches the part in its desired orientation.

**Working Principle:**
1. Bulk parts are gravity-fed from the stationary hopper onto the rotating disk.
2. As the disk rotates, parts that *match* the pocket geometry *nest* into the pockets and are carried along.
3. Parts that do not nest are *swept off* the disk by the wiper blade and fall back into the hopper.
4. Nested parts reach the discharge station where a mechanical or vacuum pickup transfers them downstream.

**Feed Rate Equation:**
$$Q = n_d \cdot p \cdot \eta_o$$
where $n_d$ is the disk rotational speed (rpm), $p$ is the number of pockets, and $\eta_o$ is the orientation efficiency.

**Advantages:**
- High speed (up to 400 parts/min).
- Gentle handling (no vibration damage).
- Excellent orientation accuracy for cap-like parts.
- Compact footprint.

**Limitations:**
- Tooling changeover requires re-machining the disk (expensive).
- Centrifugal forces limit the maximum tip speed.
- Not suitable for long, slender parts.

**(b)** A Rotary Disk Feeder has **8 equally spaced pockets** and rotates at **45 rpm**. If the orientation efficiency is **0.85**, calculate the effective feed rate. If each part weighs **15 g** and the hopper holds **3 kg** of parts, estimate the hopper refill interval. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the gross feed rate.**
$$Q = n_d \cdot p = 45 \cdot 8 = 360 \text{ parts/min}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the effective feed rate.**
$$Q_{eff} = \eta_o \cdot Q = 0.85 \cdot 360 = 306 \text{ parts/min}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the number of parts in the hopper.**
$$N_h = \dfrac{3000 \text{ g}}{15 \text{ g/part}} = 200 \text{ parts}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the refill interval.**
$$T_{refill} = \dfrac{N_h}{Q_{eff}} = \dfrac{200}{306} \approx 0.654 \text{ min} \approx 39.2 \text{ s}$$

**Answer:** The effective feed rate is **306 parts/min**, and the hopper must be refilled approximately **every 39 seconds** (assuming it starts full).

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** Step 1 = 1 mark, Step 2 = 1 mark, Step 3 = 1 mark, Step 4 = 2 marks, units check = 1 mark, final answer statement = 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative — KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q3 (a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Reciprocating Tube Hopper** with a neat sketch. Compare it with a **Center Board Hopper** on the basis of part shape suitability, feed rate, and cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Reciprocating Tube Hopper — Construction & Working:**
A reciprocating tube hopper consists of a cylindrical tube mounted coaxially inside an inclined V-shaped hopper. The tube is connected to a crank-slider mechanism driven by a motor. The tube executes a *linear reciprocating motion* along its axis. During the forward (retracting) stroke, one part slides into the tube bore. During the return (advancing) stroke, the part is pushed out of the hopper and into a delivery chute. The tube bore diameter is sized to accept the part only in its correct (e.g., head-first) orientation, achieving single-axis orientation.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Reciprocating Tube Hopper | Center Board Hopper |
|---|---|---|
| **Part shape** | Long cylindrical (screws, pins) | Spherical or short cylindrical (balls, bushings) |
| **Feed rate** | 30–100 parts/min | 20–80 parts/min |
| **Capital cost** | Low | Low to medium |
| **Orientation method** | Tube bore geometry | Peripheral discharge slot |
| **Tooling changeover** | Easy (swap tube) | Moderate (swap board) |

**(b)** A reciprocating tube hopper is driven by a crank rotating at **60 rpm**. The tube has a stroke length of **40 mm**. If the orientation efficiency is **0.90**, determine: (i) the effective feed rate, (ii) the average sliding velocity of the part, and (iii) the cycle time per part. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gross feed rate.** One part per crank revolution:
$$Q = N_{crank} = 60 \text{ parts/min}$$

**Step 2 — Effective feed rate.**
$$Q_{eff} = \eta_o \cdot Q = 0.90 \cdot 60 = 54 \text{ parts/min}$$

**Step 3 — Average sliding velocity.**
$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_s \cdot N_{crank}}{60} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.040 \cdot 60}{60} = 0.080 \text{ m/s} = 80 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4 — Cycle time per part.**
$$T_c = \dfrac{60}{N_{crank}} = 1.0 \text{ s/part (gross)}$$
$$T_{c,eff} = \dfrac{60}{Q_{eff}} = \dfrac{60}{54} \approx 1.111 \text{ s/part (effective)}$$

**Answers:** (i) **54 parts/min**, (ii) **80 mm/s**, (iii) **1.111 s/part effective**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ☐ **Non-vibratory feeders** use mechanical motion (reciprocation, rotation, gravity) — *not* vibration — to feed and orient parts.
- ☐ The **four canonical types** are: Reciprocating Tube, Center Board, External Gate, and Rotary Disk.
- ☐ **Reciprocating Tube Hopper:** Sliding tube inside an inclined hopper; one part per crank revolution; best for *long cylindrical* parts (screws, pins).
- ☐ **Center Board Hopper:** Vertical board reciprocates inside a circular bowl; sweeps parts to a peripheral slot; best for *spherical/short cylindrical* parts.
- ☐ **External Gate Hopper:** Gravity-fed hopper with a controlled external discharge gate; best for *boxy/flat* parts.
- ☐ **Rotary Disk Feeder:** Rotating disk with machined pockets; highest feed rate (up to 400 parts/min); best for *cap-like* parts.
- ☐ **Universal Feed Rate Formula:** $Q = n \cdot p \cdot \eta_o$ (modified for each feeder type).
- ☐ **Cycle Time:** $T_c = \dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds, where $N$ is the crank/disk rpm.
- ☐ **Average Sliding Velocity (reciprocating):** $v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_s N}{60}$.
- ☐ **Refill Interval:** $T_{refill} = \dfrac{V_h \rho_b \phi}{m_p Q}$.
- ☐ **Orientation efficiency $\eta_o$** must always be included in *effective* feed rate calculations — KTU examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- ☐ **Unit conversion:** $\omega$ (rad/s) to $N$ (rpm): $N = \dfrac{60 \, \omega}{2\pi}$.
- ☐ **Selection rule of thumb:** Match feeder type to *part shape* first, then to *required feed rate*, then to *budget*.
- ☐ **Always** draw a labelled schematic showing bulk input → singulation → orientation → discharge for full marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis and Design of Part Orienting Devices, Feed Tracks, and Part Placing Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT403 - MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis and Design of Part Orienting Devices, Feed Tracks, and Part Placing Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of automated assembly and manufacturing systems, **Part Orienting Devices**, **Feed Tracks**, and **Part Placing Mechanisms** form the critical *sub-system triad* that bridges bulk part storage with the precision work envelope of an automated workstation.

**Part Orienting Device (POD)**: A mechanical sub-system designed to take randomly oriented parts from a bulk supply (hopper or bin) and deliver them in a single, predefined, geometrically consistent attitude (position + orientation) to the next stage of the automation cycle. The output of a POD is a *singulated, oriented* stream of parts.

**Feed Track (FT)**: A constrained linear or curvilinear guide (also termed a *track*, *chute*, or *rail*) that physically channels oriented parts from the orienting device to the work station while maintaining their attained orientation. The track enforces single-file flow and prevents re-entanglement.

**Part Placing Mechanism (PPM)**: The terminal transfer device (e.g., a pick-and-place arm, slide, or escapement) that disengages a part from the feed track and positions it with **micron-level spatial accuracy** at the assembly or processing locus.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag (CO Mapping):** This topic directly addresses *CO3 – Design and analyse automated material handling and part-feeding sub-systems* under Module 3 of PCRUT403. Expect 14-mark analytical design problems and 3-mark definitional questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a high-volume railway marshalling yard:
- The **hopper (bulk bin)** is a giant pile of unsorted, jumbled freight cars dumped in any orientation.
- The **Part Orienting Device** is the *classification yard* — a system of switches, ramps, and gravity channels that sorts the freight so every car points the right way before being queued.
- The **Feed Track** is the *single-file rail siding* that funnels the sorted cars, one behind the other, toward the loading dock.
- The **Part Placing Mechanism** is the *locomotive crane* that lifts each car off the siding and lowers it precisely onto the correct truck bed, ready for transport.

Just as a marshalling yard can become the bottleneck of an entire freight network, an inefficient orienting/feeding sub-system will throttle the throughput of even the most advanced robot cell. The "magic" of automation is that the **bulk disorder → ordered singulation → precise placement** transformation must happen continuously, reliably, and at high cycle rates (often 30–120 parts per minute).

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following **bold** metrics are the standard KTU-recognised performance indicators for this sub-system triad:

- **Feed Rate (FR)** — parts per minute (PPM)
- **Orientation Efficiency (η<sub>o</sub>)** — ratio of correctly oriented parts to total parts fed (dimensionless, 0 < η<sub>o</sub> ≤ 1)
- **Singulation Index (S<sub>i</sub>)** — measure of single-file flow (ideally = 1)
- **Placement Accuracy (δ)** — positional tolerance at the work point, typically **±0.05 mm to ±0.5 mm**
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — reliability metric, typically **> 5,000 hours** for industrial feeders
- **Jam Rate (J<sub>r</sub>)** — jams per 10,000 cycles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A correctly designed feeding sub-system must guarantee that **η<sub>o</sub> ≥ 0.99** and **J<sub>r</sub> ≤ 1 per 10,000 cycles** to be commercially viable. Anything below this is treated as an engineering failure in KTU design problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Part Orienting Devices — Operational Taxonomy

Part orienting devices are broadly classified along two axes:

**Axis 1 — Energy Source:**
- **Passive Orienters** — exploit *gravity, geometry, and friction* alone. No external actuators. Example: gravity track with a notch.
- **Active Orienters** — use *vibration, centrifugal force, air-jet, or mechanical wipers*. Example: vibratory bowl feeder.

**Axis 2 — Action Principle:**
- **Centrifugal Orienters** — rely on rotational acceleration of the bowl.
- **Vibratory Orienters** — rely on micro-throw oscillation of the bowl track.
- **Mechanical Rejectors** — use wipers/feelers to deflect mis-oriented parts back into the bulk.

### 2.1.1 The Vibratory Bowl Feeder — Working Logic

The vibratory bowl feeder is the *workhorse* of automated assembly. Its operational logic is:

1. Parts are loaded into the bottom of a *helically wound track* inside a circular bowl.
2. An electromagnetic vibrator (driven at **50 Hz / 60 Hz** line frequency or a tunable **100–120 Hz** resonant frequency) imparts a small-amplitude, steep-angle oscillation.
3. The oscillation generates a *controlled micro-throw* — parts lift, travel forward a few millimetres, and land in a controlled parabolic trajectory.
4. As the spiral rises, geometric features in the track (notches, blade edges, cam profiles) reject mis-oriented parts; only correctly oriented parts climb the full spiral and exit over the rim.

### 2.1.2 The Centrifugal (Step) Feeder — Working Logic

Used for high-speed feeding of *small cylindrical or spherical* parts:

1. A rotating disc accelerates parts outward via *centrifugal force*.
2. A peripheral wall constrains the parts; only the correctly oriented parts can pass through a peripheral *notch or slot*.
3. Mis-oriented parts are flung off the disc and recycled.

## 2.2 Feed Tracks — Operational Principles

A feed track is fundamentally a *single-degree-of-freedom* guide. Its analysis rests on three classical mechanics pillars:

1. **Static Equilibrium** — The part must rest stably on the track surface (no tipping under its own weight).
2. **Friction Constraints** — The driving force must exceed static friction (μ<sub>s</sub>·N) but not dynamic friction of correctly oriented parts.
3. **Geometric Clearance** — Track width *W* must satisfy: W<sub>min</sub> < W < W<sub>max</sub> where W<sub>min</sub> is the part's smallest projected dimension and W<sub>max</sub> is the largest dimension that still allows *singulation*.

### 2.2.1 Tangential vs. Radial Track Entry

- **Tangential Entry:** The track meets the bowl wall at an *angle α*. This is geometrically the most common configuration and is required to prevent jams at the transition.
- **Radial Entry:** The track approaches the bowl wall *perpendicularly*. This is feasible only for very simple, symmetric parts (e.g., spheres).

### 2.2.2 Curved (Vertical-Plane) Tracks

For parts that must change elevation, the track becomes a curved arc of radius *R*. The design must ensure that the part's centre of mass passes *over* the centre of curvature to maintain stable contact (otherwise the part will "skip" off the track).

## 2.3 Part Placing Mechanisms — Operational Principles

A placing mechanism performs three sub-functions in a single work cycle:

1. **Pick-up (Acquisition)** — engages one part from the end of the feed track.
2. **Transfer (Transport)** — moves the part through free space to the work point.
3. **Place (Insertion)** — releases/inserts the part with the required accuracy.

Common placing mechanisms include:

- **Reciprocating Slide** — linear motion, simple, low cost, ±0.1 mm typical accuracy.
- **Pick-and-Place Robot Arm** — 3- or 4-DOF articulated arm, ±0.05 mm with vision, ±0.2 mm blind.
- **Escapement Mechanism** — a Geneva-like intermittent device that releases one part per cycle, ±0.05 mm.
- **Walking-Beam Transfer** — for heavy parts, synchronised horizontal motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Placing" is Harder Than "Picking":** Placement errors arise from three independent sources — (1) *robot kinematic error*, (2) *part datum error* carried over from imperfect orientation, and (3) *workpiece locating-error* at the assembly station. KTU problems often ask you to budget these three errors against the total tolerance stack.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Centrifugal Force in Bowl | F<sub>c</sub> = m·ω²·r | N | Step feeder design; radial force on part |
| 2 | Coriolis Acceleration in Track | a<sub>cor</sub> = 2·ω·v | m/s² | Curved track design for rotating bowl |
| 3 | Static Friction Force | F<sub>f</sub> = μ<sub>s</sub>·N | N | Track driving-force lower bound |
| 4 | Part Stability on Inclined Track | tan(θ) < μ<sub>s</sub> | dimensionless | Maximum allowable track incline |
| 5 | Centripetal Force for Curved Track | F<sub>cp</sub> = m·v² / R | N | Vertical-arc track minimum radius |
| 6 | Track Width — Singulation | W<sub>min</sub> < W < W<sub>max</sub> | mm | Prevents double-feeding |
| 7 | Feed Rate from Bowl | FR = 60·n·η<sub>o</sub> | parts/min | Throughput of complete feeder |
| 8 | Amplitude of Micro-throw | s = (2πf)² · A / g | m | Bowl vibrator displacement design |
| 9 | Placement Tolerance Stack | δ<sub>tot</sub> = √(δ<sub>r</sub>² + δ<sub>p</sub>² + δ<sub>w</sub>²) | mm | RSS error budget for placing |
| 10 | Bowl Angular Velocity | ω = 2π·N / 60 | rad/s | Step feeder rotational speed |
| 11 | Escapement Indexing Time | t<sub>i</sub> = 60 / (N·z) | s | Geneva wheel dwell calculation |
| 12 | Rejector Wiper Force | F<sub>w</sub> = F<sub>push</sub> · sin(φ) | N | Mechanical orienter blade design |

Where:
- *m* = mass of part (kg), *ω* = angular velocity (rad/s), *r* = radial distance (m)
- *N* = RPM, *f* = vibration frequency (Hz), *A* = vibration amplitude (m)
- *v* = linear velocity (m/s), *R* = radius of curvature (m)
- *θ* = track incline angle, *μ<sub>s</sub>* = static coefficient of friction
- *δ<sub>r</sub>, δ<sub>p</sub>, δ<sub>w</sub>* = robot, part, and workpiece error components
- *φ* = wiper engagement angle, *z* = number of escapement slots

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These sub-systems are the *silent enablers* of mass production. A modern car engine assembly line contains over **80** such orienting/feeding/placing stations, each handling a different fastener, washer, or component. The pharmaceutical industry uses vibratory bowl feeders to count pills at **> 2,000 pills/minute** with a mis-orientation rate below **1 in 100,000**. Without these mechanisms, modern consumer electronics — every screw, every connector, every micro-capacitor on your smartphone — would have to be placed by hand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the *exhaustive mathematical derivations* that KTU examiners expect for 14-mark analytical problems. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly — no steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Centrifugal Force on a Part in a Step (Centrifugal) Feeder

**Problem Setup:** A small cylindrical part of mass *m* sits on a horizontal rotating disc at radial distance *r* from the axis. The disc rotates at angular velocity *ω*. We wish to find the radial force acting on the part and the condition for it to remain in contact with the disc (i.e., not "fly off").

### Step 1: Define the kinematic state

The part executes *uniform circular motion* at radius *r* and angular velocity *ω*. The linear (tangential) velocity is:

$$
v = r \cdot \omega
$$

The centripetal acceleration directed *toward the centre* is, by definition of uniform circular motion:

$$
a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the velocity expression

$$
a_c = \frac{(r \cdot \omega)^2}{r} = \frac{r^2 \cdot \omega^2}{r}
$$

Simplify the *r*² / *r* term:

$$
a_c = r \cdot \omega^2
$$

### Step 3: Apply Newton's Second Law to obtain the force

Newton's Second Law states F = m·a. Applying this to the centripetal direction:

$$
F_c = m \cdot a_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2
$$

This is the force that the disc must *exert inward* on the part to keep it in circular motion. By Newton's Third Law, the part exerts an *outward* force of equal magnitude on the disc — this is the **centrifugal force** in the rotating reference frame:

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{centrifugal}} = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}
$$

### Step 4: Derive the critical "fly-off" angular velocity

The part is held on the disc by *static friction* F<sub>f</sub> = μ<sub>s</sub>·N. On a horizontal disc, the normal force N = m·g. The maximum static friction is therefore:

$$
F_{f,\max} = \mu_s \cdot m \cdot g
$$

The part will fly off when the required centripetal force exceeds the maximum available friction:

$$
m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 > \mu_s \cdot m \cdot g
$$

Cancel *m* on both sides:

$$
r \cdot \omega^2 > \mu_s \cdot g
$$

Solve for the *critical* angular velocity ω<sub>cr</sub> at which fly-off just begins:

$$
\omega_{cr} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_s \cdot g}{r}}
$$

### Step 5: Express in RPM for engineering use

Since ω = 2π·N / 60, where *N* is RPM:

$$
